ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P128605
ACTIVITY TITLE: SUSTAINABLE CITIES
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2014-03-18
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-07-31

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 145,246,845

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: TR 100.00% | Location: Muğla; Malatya; Gaziantep; Denizli

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 15473

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Sustainable Cities project aims to improve the quality, efficiency, and sustainability of service delivery in participating municipalities. The proposed project is structured into three components. Component A, \"Sustainable City Planning and Management Systems,\" provides technical assistance for comprehensive and integrated planning, including support for spatial, social, environmental, and capital investment planning and management. This component also finances feasibility studies, environmental assessments, and designs for sustainable investments and is financed entirely through an EU-IPA2 Recipient Executed Trust Fund Grant, also supporting Iller Bank (Ilbank) in grants management and capacity building. Component B, \"Municipal Investments,\" finances sustainable investments identified through Component A or 'no regret' investments that support environmental, social, or financial sustainability, covering sectors like public transport, water and sanitation, solid waste management, and energy. Participation in Component B is demand-driven, requiring municipalities to meet financial eligibility criteria set by Ilbank. Component C, \"Project Management,\" provides financing for project management, including monitoring and evaluation, outreach, communication, and local technical consultants for engineering supervision of Component B. The total project cost is 172.20 USD Million, with 138.70 USD Million from International Bank for Reconstruction and Development, 28.00 USD Million from free-standing TFs for ECA CU4 Country Unit, and 5.50 USD Million from the borrower. This project is expected to be the first in a series of projects (SOP), allowing Iller Bank and the World Bank to expand the approach as investments are identified and municipalities meet eligibility requirements.

Initially, three municipalities were identified for participation in both Component A and B: Mugla, Malatya, and Gaziantep. Seven additional metropolitan municipalities expressed interest in Component A and were selected based on Government of Turkey – Ministry of Environment and Urbanization priorities; these include Balikesir, Kahramanmaraş, Tekirdağ, Hatay, Samsun, Ordu, and Erzurum. Iller Bank serves as the Implementing Agency for the project and has established a Project Management Unit (PMU) within its International Relations Department to manage procurement, technical aspects, and finance, ensuring World Bank compliance.

Several safeguard policies are triggered, indicating potential environmental and social considerations. Environmental Assessment (OP/BP 4.01) is triggered because municipal investments for public goods like water, wastewater, solid waste, and urban transport infrastructure are expected, which, despite primarily positive environmental impacts, will likely have temporary environmental impacts (e.g., wastewater discharge, dust, noise) during construction and operation. Natural Habitats (OP/BP 4.04) is triggered in case a project site is selected in a natural habitat, with the project explicitly stating that investments in critical natural habitats are ineligible. Physical Cultural Resources (OP/BP 4.11) is triggered due to potential sub-projects in built-up urban areas. Safety of Dams (OP/BP 4.37) is triggered for potential water supply projects under Component B that may access water from an upstream dam, requiring Iller Bank and sub-borrowers to review existing dams. Involuntary Resettlement (OP/BP 4.12) is also triggered as the project may involve temporary and permanent land acquisition and minor resettlement impacts.

To address these safeguards, an Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) was prepared by Iller Bank and approved by the World Bank, guiding the integration of social and environmental aspects into all project stages and identifying measures to prevent or minimize adverse impacts. The ESMF, disclosed publicly in 2016, outlines requirements for meeting WB safeguard standards, including assessing physical cultural resources and incorporating 'chance find' provisions in construction contracts. A Land Acquisition and Resettlement Policy Framework (LARPF) has also been prepared by Iller Bank and approved by the World Bank, detailing entitlement and mitigation measures for social impacts and institutional arrangements for implementation and reporting of resettlement action plans (RAPs) or abbreviated RAPs (ARAPs). The project's safeguard specialists are Arzu Uraz and Esra Arikan. The World Bank contact point for the project is Soraya Goga, Lead Urban Specialist."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The targeted outcomes of the Sustainable Cities activity are to improve the quality, efficiency, and sustainability of service delivery in participating municipalities. Specifically, Component A aims to enable municipalities to improve their environmental, social, and financial/economic dimensions of sustainability, moving them from a planned city to a smart city. Component B is intended to finance sustainable investments that support environmental, social, or financial sustainability in sectors like public transport, water and sanitation, solid waste management, and energy. For the activity to be considered an overall success, there would need to be demonstrable improvements in the quality, efficiency, and sustainability of service delivery across participating municipalities. Furthermore, successful implementation of this project, as the first in a series, would enable Iller Bank and the World Bank to expand this approach as more sustainable investments are identified and additional municipalities meet eligibility requirements for financing."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "Turkey's economic growth has been impressive, averaging nearly 7% during 2003-2007, and recovering quickly after the 2008-2009 global financial crisis, reaching 4.2% in 2015. This growth has been inclusive, with poverty incidence more than halved between 2002-2012, and extreme poverty declining significantly. Access to health, education, and municipal services, especially for the poor, has remarkably improved, with near-universal health access by 2015 and primary education coverage. At least 90% of the poor had access to piped water and indoor sanitation by 2012. Urbanization has been a key driver of Turkey's economic achievements, with the urban population increasing by 15.4% during 2007-2014, and structural shifts seeing industrial and service sector contributions to GDP rise significantly. The urbanization process has also been inclusive, with growing urban growth in secondary and smaller cities like Gaziantep, Balikesir, and Malatya, which have created jobs at a comparable rate to the largest cities. However, the external context also presents challenges: Turkey's macroeconomic achievements are challenged by an uncertain economic and political outlook, slowing economic growth, exchange rate depreciation, and inflation reaching 8.8% by end-2015, well above the Central Bank's target. Despite service improvements, urbanization has led to new challenges such as sprawl exceeding mandated boundaries and significant infrastructure investment needs, with remaining gaps in services like public transport and water quality (only 52% of drinkable water treated in 2010)."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The project has three components, including demand-driven municipal investments across multiple municipalities. It triggers five World Bank safeguard policies (e.g., environmental, natural habitats, involuntary resettlement), requiring an ESMF and LARPF. Overall risk is moderate, with substantial stakeholder risk, indicating significant technical, environmental, social, and institutional complexities requiring careful management.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is explicitly designed as the first in a series of projects (SOP), which allows Iller Bank and the World Bank to expand its approach to sustainable urban development. It is therefore cohesively integrated into a larger, evolving programmatic framework, with the potential to scale up investments and municipality participation over time.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Sustainable Cities project costs USD 172.20M. Sub-activities: Component A (EUR 23.125M, EU-IPA2) covers city planning, feasibility studies, designs, Ilbank capacity. Component B (EUR 121.200M, IBRD) funds demand-driven municipal investments in urban infrastructure. Component C (EUR 1.000M, Ilbank) provides project management. The 'Financing Gap' is 0.00, indicating initial funding adequacy. Success relies on municipalities meeting Component B eligibility and future financing approvals for the SOP."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: Ilbank, the implementing agency, has demonstrated capacity from previous World Bank projects (MSP, MSP-AF). It has a dedicated PMU with specialized staff (procurement, finance, technical) and comprehensive support from 18 regional directorates and various internal departments (e.g., Project, Investment Appraisal, Infrastructure Implementation). Ilbank is the direct borrower for Component B, showing significant financial skin-in-the-game, and views this as the first in a series of projects. Participating municipalities must meet financial eligibility and establish PIUs, ensuring their commitment. A three-level GRM, including municipal 'White Tables' and Ilbank, addresses feedback. Government alignment, robust safeguard frameworks (ESMF, LARPF prepared by Ilbank), and attention to gender considerations further aid outcomes.
ACTIVITY RISKS: "Municipalities failing to meet financial eligibility for Component B could limit project scope. Unmanaged temporary environmental impacts (e.g., dust, noise, wastewater discharge) during construction/operation, or delays from physical cultural resource discoveries, pose risks. Significant social impacts from involuntary land acquisition/resettlement, access issues in new water schemes, wastewater discharge near residences, or economic resettlement of minibus drivers, if not addressed timely by municipalities, could hinder success. Additionally, dam safety concerns for water supply projects could cause delays or cancellation, and uncertain safeguard application exists if the World Bank doesn't finance technical assistance output implementation."

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "[\"The project leverages Ilbank's decade of preparation and implementation experience with previous projects and international experience.\",\"Project design is embedded in existing Government policies (10th Development Plan, KENTGES, SCALA, Metropolitan Municipality Law), ensuring ownership and validation for sustainable local service delivery.\",\"The Sustained Operations Program (SOP) approach builds on predecessor projects, expands World Bank engagement sectorally and spatially, and envisages a 15-year horizon for sustained engagement and capacity building.\",\"Clear investment selection tools and rules allow for phased, readiness-based access to financing for municipalities, ensuring manageable supervision by Ilbank and the World Bank.\",\"Multiyear customized institutional development and TA support, based on lessons learned, will improve service delivery, O&M performance, and the sustainability of investments.\",\"A strong pre-identified investment pipeline ensures project readiness, which is critical for successful and timely implementation.\",\"Ilbank has demonstrated capacity in managing World Bank loans through previous projects (MSP and MSP-AF) and is an experienced borrower with moderately satisfactory past procurement performance.\",\"An existing Project Management Unit (PMU) within Ilbank, with staff capacity in procurement, financial management, and technical sectors, will continue to implement the project.\",\"The PMU is extensively supported by several specialized Ilbank departments and 18 regional directorates, providing comprehensive expertise and monitoring capacity.\",\"Strict municipal and subproject eligibility criteria (economic, financial, technical, environmental, social) ensure only viable, right-sized, and sustainable investments are selected and funded.\",\"The Systematic Operations Risk-Rating Tool (SORT) assesses the risk in 'Sector Strategies and Policies' as Low.\",\"Stakeholder risk is mitigated by requiring formal Municipal Executive Board decisions and feasibility studies with financial assessments to ensure strong commitment from participating entities.\",\"Procurement risks are addressed through regular Ilbank PMU support and capacity building for municipalities, preparation of standard bidding documents, and continuous World Bank supervision and training.\",\"Ilbank has prepared an Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and a Land Acquisition and Resettlement Policy Framework (LARPF), both integrated with national and World Bank safeguard policies.\",\"A robust three-level grievance redressal and feedback mechanism (GRM) will be established, building on existing 'White Tables' with online, toll-free, and SMS channels to ensure transparency and accountability.\",\"Grievance Committees (GCs), including representatives of affected people, will be established to resolve land acquisition and resettlement issues through negotiation, aiming to minimize litigation.\"]"

YOUR TASK:
 Aggregate the your considerations above. Think like a superforecaster (e.g. Nate Silver). On the very last line of your response, write 'FORECAST: ' followed by exactly one option from this rating scale with no extra words: 'Highly Satisfactory', 'Satisfactory', 'Moderately Satisfactory', 'Moderately Unsatisfactory', 'Unsatisfactory', 'Highly Unsatisfactory'.

Respond only in English.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P147342
ACTIVITY TITLE: Peru: Enhancement of Environmental Quality Services
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-08-07
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-10-15

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 51,234,754

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: PE 100.00% | Location: Trujillo; Piura; Iquitos; Chiclayo; Rio Rimac; Lima; Huancayo; Cusco; Callao; Moche

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 8452

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The activity, \"Peru: Enhancement of Environmental Quality Services,\" proposes to enhance environmental management by increasing the quality, availability, and reliability of environmental data for decision-makers and civil society, improving mechanisms to identify and address environmental priorities at regional and national levels, and opening up decision-making on environmental aspects to public scrutiny. The achievement of this objective will be measured through key results indicators including the number of laboratories certified by a reference laboratory, the production of an annual report identifying environmental priorities based on economic analysis of environmental degradation costs, and user perception of environmental data and information provided by SINIA (from a survey). The ultimate goal is to strengthen environmental management systems in Peru, particularly the capacity of national, regional, and local governments, to promote environmental sustainability in key economic sectors, enhance competitiveness, establish a national system of environmental information for decision-making, and reduce social conflicts. This project aligns with Peru's Strategic Axis of Environmental Management and the National Agenda for Environmental Action, as well as the FY12-FY16 Peru Country Partnership Strategy. The project will address existing weaknesses such as ill-prepared public servants and lack of institutional channels for environmental complaints. It will implement five components: investments in a National Environmental Information System to provide transparent, scientifically-based data, including a reference laboratory and monitoring networks; investments in Demand for Good Environmental Governance to establish a participatory system for stakeholders; Environmental Certification for Investments in Key Economic Sectors, which involves creating a sustainability framework for investments, enhancing Environmental Impact Assessment capacity in MINAM, SENACE, and other ministries/regional governments, and providing facilities for SENACE; Organizational Strengthening at the regional level, including financing for environmental decentralization; and Project Implementation, with MINAM having overall responsibility, though intersectoral coordination mechanisms are still to be defined. The project builds on the legal framework established by the National Environmental Impact Assessment System (SEIA) and the National Service of Environmental Certification for Sustainable Investments (SENACE), created in December 2012, recognizing that full implementation of SENACE requires special efforts from various sectors and a strengthened MINAM. The total project cost is USD 75 million, with USD 35 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development and USD 40 million from the Republic of Peru, indicating a fully funded initiative. Safeguard policies triggered include Environmental Assessment (OP/BP 4.01), Natural Habitats (OP/BP 4.04), Pest Management (OP 4.09), Indigenous Peoples (OP/BP 4.10), and Involuntary Resettlement (OP/BP 4.12), highlighting potential environmental and social risks that require careful management."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The targeted outcomes of the \"Peru: Enhancement of Environmental Quality Services\" activity are to enhance environmental management by increasing the quality, availability, and reliability of environmental data for decision-makers and civil society, improving mechanisms to identify and address environmental priorities at regional and national levels, and opening up decision-making on environmental aspects to public scrutiny. The ultimate goal is to strengthen environmental management systems in Peru, particularly the capacity of national, regional, and local governments, to promote environmental sustainability in key economic sectors, enhance competitiveness, establish a national system of environmental information for decision-making, and reduce social conflicts. It also aims to address existing weaknesses such as ill-prepared public servants and a lack of institutional channels for environmental complaints. Furthermore, the activity intends to enable the creation of an institutional and policy framework that promotes environmental sustainability of key economic sectors, foster a national system of environmental information that supports decision-making in investment and production activities, and bolster the demand side of good environmental governance. An amendment also added environmental health initiatives to prevent COVID-19 contagion and promote counter-transmission measures of infectious diseases.To consider the activity an overall success, the following key results indicators would need to be achieved: an increase in the number of laboratories certified by a reference laboratory; the production of an annual report identifying environmental priorities based on an economic analysis of environmental degradation costs; and an improved user perception (as measured by a survey) of environmental data and information provided by SINIA (Sistema Nacional de Información Ambiental)."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: Peru's strong economic growth and government commitment to environmental management (aligned with national strategies and significant co-financing) are positive external factors. However, persistent high costs of environmental degradation (3.5-5.0% of GDP) highlight the scale of the problem. Significant challenges include a disconnect between data and decision-making, insufficient and non-standardized environmental data, lack of a reference laboratory, limited analytical capacity outside major cities, ill-prepared public servants, and weak civil society participation mechanisms. The legal framework (SEIA, SENACE) exists but requires substantial strengthening for full implementation, indicating institutional hurdles. Triggered safeguard policies also signify potential risks requiring careful management.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is highly complex, involving establishing a National Environmental Information System, a reference laboratory, and monitoring networks, which requires technical standardization, quality control, and certification of existing laboratories. It demands significant institutional capacity building across national, regional, and local governments for environmental management, Environmental Impact Assessment, and the new SENACE certification system, requiring substantial intersectoral coordination. The project also triggers multiple safeguard policies, highlighting complex environmental and social risk management.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is a large program, referred to as a "Project" with a total cost of USD 75 million. It is cohesively integrated into Peru's broader national environmental management strategies, including the Strategic Axis of Environmental Management and the National Agenda for Environmental Action. Furthermore, it is fully aligned with and supports the FY12-FY16 Peru Country Partnership Strategy, demonstrating its role as a significant, integrated component of a larger national development framework.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The activity, \"Peru: Enhancement of Environmental Quality Services,\" has a total project cost of USD 75 million. This is budgeted with USD 35 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development and USD 40 million from the Republic of Peru, indicating a fully funded initiative. The specific activities and their general components are: investments in a National Environmental Information System, which includes designing, constructing, and equipping a state-of-the-art national environmental analysis laboratory, and conducting a study on improving and expanding the scope of SIGERSOL to cover medical waste management. Other activities include investments in Demand for Good Environmental Governance to establish a participatory system; Environmental Certification for Investments in Key Economic Sectors, involving creating a sustainability framework, enhancing Environmental Impact Assessment capacity, and providing facilities for SENACE; Organizational Strengthening at the regional level, including financing for environmental decentralization; and Project Implementation. Additionally, the project involves the development and deployment of user-friendly IT platforms and online tools for disseminating environmental quality information and for environmental health initiatives to prevent COVID-19 contagion and promote counter-transmission measures of infectious diseases. The provided documents do not detail the specific budgeted amounts for each individual activity or component, nor do they provide information on typical costs of these activities or any discrepancies."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: ["The Ministry of Environment (MINAM) has overall responsibility but suffers from ill-prepared public servants, inadequate funding, varying technical/human resource capacities, and unstandardized environmental monitoring. SENACE, a key implementing partner created in 2012, requires special efforts, facilities, and EIA capacity enhancement. Regional governments also have limited analytical capability. The Republic of Peru demonstrates significant 'skin-in-the-game' by contributing USD 40M (over 50% of the total project cost of USD 75M) and aligning the project with national environmental strategies.", "Civil society's participatory mechanisms (like CARs) are weak, indicating a need for project support to aid outcomes, but currently pose a risk. Other ministries and regional governments are crucial for intersectoral coordination and EIA capacity, and their cooperation is essential. The project will address their coordination needs, which are currently undefined. Third-party monitoring, currently lacking rigorous supervision and quality control, will be improved through the project's reference laboratory and certification system."]
ACTIVITY RISKS: "[\"Insufficient and incomparable environmental data due to a lack of baselines, standardized protocols, validated methods, and adequate monitoring frequency, compounded by limited analytical capacity in laboratories and the absence of a reference laboratory for certification and validation.\", \"Weak institutional and governance frameworks, including a disconnect between data and decision-making, non-rigorous supervision of monitoring, unstandardized investment project information, limited civil society participation, ill-prepared public servants, and lack of complaint channels. Special efforts are required for full SENACE implementation and MINAM strengthening. Intersectoral coordination mechanisms for project implementation remain undefined.\", \"Potential environmental and social risks requiring careful management, highlighted by the triggering of safeguard policies: Environmental Assessment, Natural Habitats, Pest Management, Indigenous Peoples, and Involuntary Resettlement.\"]"

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "[\"Peru's economy has shown broad-based rapid growth and sound macroeconomic management over the last decade, leading to resilient GDP growth and sufficient fiscal space. This stable economic environment provides a strong foundation for the project's success and sustainability.\",\"The Government of Peru (GoP) has a proven track record of successfully implementing environmental interventions in land rehabilitation, flood protection, sustainable fisheries, and deforestation control over the last decade, demonstrating its capacity for effective environmental management.\",\"Peru has made substantial progress in improving public services relevant to environmental quality, including increased access to improved drinking water and sanitation, and a decline in air pollution in Lima-Callao, providing a positive foundation for further enhancements.\",\"The project is built upon established legal and institutional frameworks, including the National Environmental Impact Assessment System (SEIA) and the National Service of Environmental Certification for Sustainable Investments (SENACE), created in December 2012. This leverages existing structures for implementation.\",\"The project aligns with Peru's FY12-FY16 Country Partnership Strategy, the Strategic Axis of Environmental Management, and the National Agenda for Environmental Action. This strong strategic alignment indicates robust governmental commitment and international support, crucial for overcoming implementation challenges.\",\"The project is fully funded with a total cost of USD 75 million and a financing gap of zero. The Republic of Peru is contributing USD 40 million, exceeding the USD 35 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development, demonstrating strong country ownership and financial commitment.\",\"A key component focuses on establishing a transparent, unbiased National Environmental Information System based on scientifically-based data. This includes developing a reference laboratory and a quality control assurance system for accrediting environmental analysis laboratories, directly addressing existing data quality and comparability weaknesses.\",\"The project includes direct investments in strengthening key environmental institutions, specifically enhancing Environmental Impact Assessment capacity for staff in MINAM, SENACE, and regional governments, and providing facilities/equipment for SENACE. This targeted capacity building supports overcoming institutional weaknesses.\"]"

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P147921
ACTIVITY TITLE: OMVS - TRANSMISSION EXPANSION  PROJECT
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-06-18
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-12-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Regional

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 97,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Location: Tambacounda; Republic of Mali; Kayes Region | Recipient countries: ML 33.33%; MR 33.33%; SN 33.33%

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The OMVS - Transmission Expansion Project aims to enhance electricity trade among Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal. The project, with an economic lifetime assumed to be 40 years, involves financing selected investments to reinforce and extend RIMA, the backbone of the OMVS regional power grid. This includes the construction of two new transmission lines and associated substations, considering future power generation at Gouina (to be commissioned in 2019) and the necessity of interconnecting with the OMVG network, alongside the implementation of a SCADA system. Specifically, the project will finance the Kayes-Tambacounda transmission line, which will provide a planned connection between the OMVS and OMVG systems and help transmit additional energy from the Gouina hydropower plant to Senegal and Mauritania. This line is a strategic connection identified by the WAPP Master Plan to create redundancy and ensure system availability. The project also provides technical assistance for its implementation, and the amount of contingencies is estimated at 12 percent of the total investment cost. A 225 kV voltage line has been validated by a feasibility study, and the power wheeling capacity of the Kayes-Tambacounda double circuit in aluminum alloy (AAAC), 228 mm², with two conductors per phase, has been defined. The total project cost (CapEx) is estimated at 99 million USD, with O&M costs at 2% of CapEx. \n\nDirect project beneficiaries are existing and future customers of power utilities in Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal, who will benefit from improved electricity services, reliable energy supply, increased productivity, competitive edge for firms, job creation, and economic growth. SOGEM will also receive technical assistance. The project's benefits are broadly an increased efficiency in electricity supply, characterized by reduced transmission losses, N-1 security, fewer outages, and stabilized voltage fluctuations, as well as an increased supply of electricity to Senegal and Mauritania to meet existing suppressed demand and expected growth. The economic viability of the project was evaluated starting in 2017, with commissioning expected by the end of 2020, meaning benefits from the flow of electricity are assumed to accrue from 2021. The assumed electricity flow through the transmission line is equally distributed to both Senegal and Mauritania upon the commissioning of the Gouina power plant. The economic analysis indicates a robust economic viability with a base case Economic Internal Rate of Return (EIRR) of 24.95% and a Net Present Value (NPV) of US$305 million (at a 6% discount rate). Sensitivity analysis confirms this robustness against potential cost overruns and changes in generation availability, showing an EIRR of 20.99% and an NPV of US$287 million even with a 120% cost overrun. Key performance indicators for the project include an additional annual electricity traded between Mali and Mauritania and between Mali and Senegal, each with an end target of 147 GWh, and a reduction in the average duration of power outages in the RIMA system from a baseline of 72 hours to an end target of 60 hours per year. Intermediate results include 288 km of transmission lines constructed, specifically the Kayes-Tambacounda line, and the implementation of a SCADA system."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "Targeted outcomes include enhancing electricity trade among Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal, aiming for an additional 147 GWh annually traded between Mali and Mauritania, and between Mali and Senegal. Other outcomes are reducing the average annual power outage duration on the RIMA system to 60 hours, increasing efficiency and supply of electricity, and providing technical assistance to SOGEM. Success requires achieving these trade and reliability targets, constructing 288 km of transmission lines (Kayes-Tambacounda line), implementing a SCADA system, addressing 100% of grievances, and demonstrating robust economic viability (EIRR of 24.95%, NPV of US$305 million)."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "[\"The project's probability of success is significantly enhanced by a strong external context. There's a high and growing energy demand in Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal, driving the need for this expansion and a shift from high-cost thermal power to cheaper hydropower. The project aligns perfectly with national development strategies and is deeply integrated into the well-established OMVS and the developing WAPP regional frameworks, ensuring broad institutional and political support. Financial backing from the World Bank and AFD, along with complementary regional generation (Gouina, Banda Gas) and transmission (OMVG, CLSG) projects, further strengthens its viability.\", \"External challenges that could impact success include existing generation deficits across the WAPP region, which could limit the full utilization of expanded transmission capacity. Member states face economic vulnerabilities and governance issues: Mali suffers from an electricity gap, security concerns, and needs to consolidate peace. Mauritania's economy is exposed to external shocks (e.g., commodity price collapse), with calls for improved governance and resilience. These factors could introduce instability and affect the sustained operational environment for the project.\"]"

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: Significant technical complexity arises from reinforcing and extending the RIMA regional power grid through the construction of two new 288 km 225 kV double-circuit transmission lines (Kayes-Tambacounda) and substations, integrating with the OMVG network and future Gouina power generation, and implementing/upgrading a SCADA system. It also includes substantial technical assistance.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This is a major regional program enhancing electricity trade among Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal. It directly reinforces the OMVS regional power grid (RIMA backbone) and is integrated with the OMVG network and the broader WAPP system. It's a strategic connection within the WAPP Master Plan, contributing to a unified regional power grid.

FINANCING DETAILS: "Specific activities include RIMA Reinforcement and Expansion (US$94M, IDA US$88M), covering the Kayes-Tambacounda Transmission Line (US$83.6M, all IDA) and SCADA System (US$10.4M, IDA US$4.4M, SOGEM US$6M). Technical Assistance is budgeted at US$9M (all IDA). The total project CapEx is stated as US$99M in the description, but documents show a Total Project Cost of US$150M, fully financed by IDA with a US$0.00 financing gap. The detailed components sum to US$103M. Contingencies are 12% of investment cost, O&M 2% of CapEx. The activity is well-financed, showing robust economic viability (EIRR 24.95%, NPV US$305M) even with 120% cost overruns, indicating strong financial prospects for success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: "[\"SOGEM, the implementing entity, is a public company equally owned by Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal, demonstrating high skin-in-the-game. It has experience with World Bank projects but its capacity is currently stretched. To mitigate this, an Owner's Engineer and a Regional Coordination Unit (RCU) with specialized experts will be recruited to assist SOGEM, enhancing its abilities in planning, scheduling, bidding, bid evaluation, contract award, and environmental/social oversight. SOGEM's subsidiary, SEMAF, will manage O&M post-commissioning.\", \"The Republics of Senegal, Mali, and Mauritania, as owners of SOGEM and direct beneficiaries, are highly invested. The World Bank (IDA) provides funding and oversight, approving key support roles. The OMVS Council of Ministers and Steering Committee provide governance. The Owner's Engineer and RCU aid SOGEM's stretched capacity. Contractors' performance is crucial, with requirements for CESMP, H&S plans, local labor, and a Code of Conduct. An NGO will monitor social aspects. The OMVG system interconnection and WAPP integration are supportive regional elements.\"]"
ACTIVITY RISKS: "The activity faces risks including high-risk country environments, particularly Mali's political and security situation, fragility, low capacity, and weak institutions. Elections in Senegal may cause delays. Challenges in the OMVS electricity sector, notably utilities' poor financial standing and Mali's institutional/financing issues (risk of EDM non-performance if reforms fail), are significant. ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P148099
ACTIVITY TITLE: Governance and Competitiveness Programmatic DPO
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-04-21
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2017-09-01

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Single location

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 24,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: KG 100.00% | Location: Bishkek

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 2419

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Programmatic Governance and Competitiveness Development Policy Operation (DPO) series, with this being the first in a two-part series, aims to support the Kyrgyz government's reforms to improve public sector governance and increase private sector competitiveness. This initial operation provides a total of $24 million, comprising an IDA Credit of SDR9.4 million (US$13.2 million equivalent) with a 38-year maturity and a 6-year grace period, and an IDA Grant of SDR7.7 million (US$10.8 million equivalent). The Kyrgyz Republic is the borrower, with the Ministry of Finance serving as the implementation agency. The program aligns with the National Sustainable Development Strategy (NSDS) themes of sustainable governance and a sustainable economy, and it builds upon a previous programmatic DPO series and a recent Energy Sector DPO.

The proposed DPO series has two main pillars. Pillar 1 focuses on improving public sector governance through reforms aimed at enhancing the integrity of government decisions, increasing the quality of public services, and improving energy sector governance and financial viability. Key policy actions under this pillar include the introduction of conflict of interest legislation, implementation of the public procurement law, adoption of public service standards, and various measures to boost transparency and accountability within the energy sector. These initiatives are expected to combat both grand and petty corruption, fostering a more conducive environment for private investment and growth. Pillar 2, which supports competitiveness reforms, draws from the Private Sector Development Program and aims to facilitate trade, improve the business environment, and enhance connectivity. The measures under this pillar are designed to enable Kyrgyz businesses to fully capitalize on the country's accession to the Customs Union and Eurasian Union.

The operation's design is justified by its strong alignment with the government's own reform priorities, thereby strengthening ownership and the likelihood of positive results. Macroeconomic stability is addressed through an IMF-supported program, and the DPO tackles weak governance and the rule of law, which have been identified as the most significant constraints to growth and investment in the Kyrgyz Republic. This programmatic DPO series is part of a broader reform agenda supported by other development partners, including the International Monetary Fund (IMF), the Asian Development Bank (ADB), the UK's Department for International Development (DfID), and the European Union (EU), who are also providing support in critical areas such as public sector strengthening, anti-corruption, accountability, energy sector reform, and the business environment.

Expected poverty and social impacts include long-term positive distributional effects from economic growth, improved institutional capacity of the Government to deliver quality public services, particularly to the bottom quintiles, and more client-oriented public services resulting from fair and transparent government procedures. The improved business and regulatory climate is anticipated to facilitate private sector growth, benefiting the bottom 40 percent of the population engaged in labor activities. Overall, the operation is expected to have a positive impact on economic growth and sustainability, contributing to shared prosperity and inclusive growth, with the World Bank team committed to monitoring the moderate cumulative effect of rising energy tariffs and discussing improvements in targeting energy-related subsidies.

To forecast success, specific result indicators with baselines and targets have been established. For Pillar 1, by 2018, the target is to verify 15% of personal interest declarations from high-level/high-risk public officials (from a 2016 baseline of 0%), improve the PEFA (PI 19) public procurement score from B (2014) to B+ (2018), and reduce the graft index in key public service areas from 21% (2016) to 10% (2018). Energy sector targets by 2017 include increasing cash collected per kWh of power from 0.76 KGS/kWh (2015) to 0.97 KGS/kWh, and per Gcal of heat from 921.83 KGS/Gcal (2015) to 1364.31 KGS/Gcal, alongside disclosing quarterly key performance indicators for the heating sector by 2017 (baseline 2015: no disclosure). For Pillar 2, by 2018, the export sales tax rate on goods and services is targeted for reduction from 2-3% (2016) to 0%, the number of firms with certified HACCP is targeted to increase from 9 (2016) to 20, and the number of firms included in the EEU register of FBOs is also targeted to increase from 9 (2016) to 20. The share of VAT refunds processed through the "green channel" is targeted to reach 25% by 2018, up from 0% in 2016.

The DPO includes several prior actions supported by analytical underpinnings and technical assistance. For Pillar 1, these include the Government submitting the draft Law on Conflict of Interest to parliament, the Ministry of Finance issuing a Regulation establishing an Independent Complaint Review Commission for public procurement, and the Government adopting service-delivery standards for at least 90 public services. Additionally, the State Regulatory Agency of the Fuel and Energy Complex is to adopt a tariff-setting methodology for heating sector companies, the Government is to adopt a Resolution instituting a new cash settlement mechanism between electricity companies, and the State Regulatory Agency is to adopt a Performance Reporting and Monitoring Framework for the heating sector. For Pillar 2, prior actions involve the Government submitting to parliament amendments to the Tax Code abolishing sales taxes for exporters and streamlining VAT administration through measures like eliminating paper-based invoicing and simplifying VAT refund procedures for trustworthy taxpayers. A specific Prior Action #10 requires the Government, through the Ministry of Transport and Communication and the State Border Service Agency, to adopt regulations for authorized telecommunications companies to deploy cross-border cables, ensuring competition and transparent procedures in the international connectivity market. An indicative Trigger #10 for the subsequent operation involves the State Communications Agency submitting to parliament amendments to the Law on Electronic Communications to promote competition in radio spectrum and adopting a dispute resolution procedure for electronic communications service providers. These connectivity-related actions are expected to boost competition, measurably increase international internet bandwidth, expand broadband internet access, enhance international connectivity, increase private investment in wireless infrastructure, and improve market competitiveness and grievance redress, aligning with findings from the 2016 World Development Report and laying groundwork for the regional Digital Central Asia-South Asia (CASA) Project. The comprehensive nature of the objectives, detailed policy actions, measurable indicators, and strong external and internal support provide a robust foundation for forecasting the overall success of this programmatic activity.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: ["Targeted outcomes include: By 2018, verifying 15% of high-risk public officials' interest declarations, improving public procurement score to B+, reducing graft index to 10%, increasing power/heat cash collection (0.97 KGS/kWh, 1364.31 KGS/Gcal by 2017), disclosing heating sector KPIs. Also, reducing export sales tax to 0%, increasing HACCP/EEU FBO firms to 20 each, 25% VAT refunds via green channel, 50% firms filing social contributions, 20% annual sanitary inspections, 500 firms providing inspection feedback, and increasing international internet bandwidth to 6500 Mbps.", "The activity targets improving public sector governance (integrity, service quality, energy sector financial viability) and strengthening private sector competitiveness (trade, business environment, connectivity). Overall success requires achieving these specific quantitative targets, realizing long-term positive distributional effects from economic growth, improved institutional capacity to deliver quality public services (especially to bottom quintiles), more client-oriented public services, private sector growth benefiting the bottom 40%, and contributing to shared prosperity and inclusive growth."]

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: - The activity faces significant external headwinds. Regional and global economic downturns, especially in Russia and Kazakhstan (key trading partners), have led to sharp declines in trade and remittances to Kyrgyzstan, and som depreciation (20% in 2015) has fueled uncertainty and increased public debt. Growth is projected to decelerate marginally in 2016-2017, and inflation is expected to rise, creating a challenging macroeconomic environment for reforms.
- Despite economic challenges, the activity's success is bolstered by strong alignment with the government's National Sustainable Development Strategy and support from multiple development partners including IMF, ADB, DfID, and EU, which helps address macroeconomic stability and critical reform areas. Accession to the Eurasian Economic Union, while requiring adaptation, also presents opportunities for trade and business, which the DPO aims to leverage.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: This programmatic DPO series involves significant policy, legal, and institutional reforms across public sector governance and private sector competitiveness. Structured into two pillars with detailed prior actions, it addresses deep-rooted systemic issues through new legislation, procurement reforms, service standards, and energy sector governance improvements.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This is the first in a two-part Programmatic DPO series, constituting a larger program. It is deeply integrated into the government's National Sustainable Development Strategy, builds on prior DPOs, and is part of a broader reform agenda supported by multiple development partners (IMF, ADB, DfID, EU).

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Governance and Competitiveness DPO provides $24M ($13.2M IDA Credit, $10.8M IDA Grant) for general budget support. Sub-activities fall under two pillars: Pillar 1 for public sector governance (e.g., integrity, public procurement, energy sector governance); Pillar 2 for competitiveness (e.g., trade facilitation, business environment, connectivity). Existing social assistance programs impacted by reforms and their 2013-2015 spending (mln KGS): Electricity compensations to pensioners (1,747.7); Compensations in lieu of privileges (1,530.1); Life-line tariff (118); Public servants (10.44); Utility subsidy (Bishkek) (25.1). MBLIF budget was KGS 2,061M in 2014."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The Ministry of Finance, along with the broader Kyrgyz government and other relevant government agencies, is the primary implementing organization. The government demonstrates strong investment and "skin-in-the-game" through its alignment with national reform priorities, which the activity explicitly states strengthens ownership and the likelihood of positive results. The government has actively engaged in extensive public hearings on prior actions and reforms, including involving civil society and adapting legislation based on feedback to achieve consensus. High-level officials, including those from the Ministry of Finance, have shown strong commitment to strengthening public sector accounting and are undertaking politically sensitive reforms such as restructuring untargeted energy benefits and piloting new social safety net programs. The activity's design includes numerous prior actions requiring the government to submit laws, issue regulations, and adopt standards, indicating concrete steps towards reform.Strong support from various stakeholders significantly aids activity outcomes. The International Development Association provides substantial funding and manages the activity, while a broad coalition of development partners, including the International Monetary Fund, Asian Development Bank, UK's Department for International Development, European Union, Japan International Cooperation Agency, Germany Development Bank, and the United States Agency for International Development, provides coordinated support across critical reform areas. The International Finance Corporation offers advisory services for private sector competitiveness, and civil society organizations are engaged in monitoring and designing aspects of the program.However, potential deteriorating factors and areas of concern exist. Progress in restructuring energy benefits and improving social benefit targeting has been slow and inconsistent, and improving the targeting of the Monthly Benefit for Low-Income Families has proven difficult and lengthy. There are significant weaknesses in public financial management systems, including a lack of comprehensive automation for accounting and reporting, with progress on a fully integrated system being very slow. National public sector accounting and reporting standards are not fully developed, and institutional frameworks to transition to International Public Sector Accounting Standards are insufficient. The Chamber of Accounts, the supreme audit institution, is still in early stages of development with limited auditing functions. While the government is committed to addressing these issues and receives ongoing technical assistance, these systemic challenges could impact the pace and effectiveness of implementation.
ACTIVITY RISKS:

ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-04-28
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-03-29

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 100,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: TN 100.00% | Location: Gouvernorat de Siliana; Sidi Bouzid Ouest; Gouvernorat de Sidi Bouzid; Gouvernorat de Jendouba; Gouvernorat de Bizerte; Gouvernorat de BEja; Aïne Draham; Gouvernorat de Kairouan; Gouvernorat de Kasserine; Gouvernorat de Kef

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 4350

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Integrated Landscapes Management in Tunisia's Lagging Regions Project (P151030) is an Investment Project Financing instrument led by Task Team Leader Taoufiq Bennouna, managed by GEN05. The estimated appraisal date is 16-Jan-2017, with an estimated Board date of 17-Apr-2017. With a total project cost of $100.00 million and no financing gap, the project is fully funded by the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development. The project is classified under Environmental Category B - Partial Assessment and is not a repeater project. Its development objective is to improve access to economic opportunities derived from more sustainable management of agricultural resources in selected lagging regions of Tunisia. Direct beneficiaries include approximately 250,000 rural households (about 1.5 million people) in the NW and CW regions, with women constituting 51% and youth (15-29 age category) about 25% (400,000 people) of these beneficiaries, by improving asset-based livelihoods through capacity building and investments in local natural, physical, human, and social capital. National and regional institutions will also benefit indirectly.

The project focuses on the Northwest and Center-West regions of Tunisia, where agriculture (crops, forestry, and livestock) dominates the economic life, providing the bulk of employment and income, yet income levels remain low, poverty is high, and economic prospects are threatened by unsustainable agricultural and natural resource management practices and climate change. These regions also exhibit poor development indicators in education, employment, and health, with the highest unemployment rates for university graduates. Key constraints hampering sustainable development include fragmented and centralized administrative approaches, limited infrastructure and public services (transportation, education, health, water/sanitation), limited availability of common services (logistics, business intelligence, R&D, financial services), and national agricultural and forestry policies that do not foster sustainable agricultural development. Unequal opportunities, partly due to favoritism and elite capture by certain powerful groups, manifest as privileged access to agricultural inputs and stronger voices in local planning, marginalizing women and unemployed youth. The cost of rangeland degradation and natural forest clearing is estimated at US$36 million per year.

The project's theory of change posits that current regulations restricting access to natural resources, inadequate government services, poor infrastructure, remoteness, coordination failures, and limited market/credit access are key constraints to private investment and sustainable growth. The project seeks to address these by supporting intensified localized sustainable production and improving market access for the bottom 40 percent of beneficiaries, while reducing pressure on the fragile landscape. This approach aims to create a win-win situation by boosting beneficiary incomes and regenerating agricultural, forested, and pastoral areas. Strategic public investment is crucial to help private entrepreneurs understand market trends, support efficient producer groups, and establish effective links between producers, processors, and buyers. Increased productivity in agriculture, forest, and rangelands is projected to significantly impact economic growth, with forest and rangeland market value potentially increasing five-fold from US$16 million to US$75 million, creating around 25,000 jobs. There is also significant scope for local actors to reorient products towards higher value-added, global, fair-trade, organic, and ethical markets. Ecotourism promotion is also identified as a potential avenue for rural economic diversification and employment creation. To mitigate elite capture, investments in job creation and local development will be identified through analytically underpinned and inclusive participatory processes, informing pro-poor local development plans, policy reforms, and private sector development.

The project aligns with Tunisia's New Five-year Development Plan (2016-2020), contributing to its pillars of restoring an environment for sustainable economic growth and private sector-led job creation, reducing regional disparities, and promoting increased social inclusion, particularly by improving regulatory environment in agroforestry, fostering job creation, and promoting economic inclusion for youth and marginalized groups. It also contributes to the World Bank Group's strategic goals of ending extreme poverty and boosting shared prosperity, fostering inclusion, and aligning with the MENA Regional Strategy by promoting a new bottom-up development model, more effective public services for the poor, and private sector development for job creation, while creating synergies with external partners such as AFD, IFAD, AfDB, JICA, UNDP, and FAO. Furthermore, the project supports the World Bank Forest Action Plan FY2016-20 (Focus Areas 1 and 2), through investments optimizing forest potential for income and jobs, and forest-smart interventions in other economic sectors to improve agricultural productivity and resilience, including climate change mitigation and adaptation actions. It also supports the World Bank Agricultural Strategy's four pillars: improving food supply/nutrition, providing rural livelihoods/income, economic growth/environmental sustainability, and addressing climate change. An inter-ministerial decision from December 20, 2016, involving multiple ministries, ensures a joint approach to value-chain development activities in targeted lagging regions, including this project (P151030).

The project will support local development councils, Civil Society Organizations (CSOs), and Groups for Agricultural Development (GDAs) in implementing subprojects related to production systems, forest and rangeland co-management, and fostering agricultural and nonagricultural products. These entities will act as interfaces between populations and regional planning bodies, initiating dialogue for Integrated Landscapes Development Plans (ILDPs). Support will include training and logistical assistance for effective participation. Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs), including Mutual Societies for Agricultural Services (SMSAs), will receive support to enhance local product processing, marketing, commercialization, market access, and ecotourism development. The project will also facilitate commercial arrangements between local organizations and national or international buyers, assist in formalizing associations, preparing funding applications, and accessing microfinance through banking or specialized institutions. The institutional arrangement involves a National Steering Committee co-chaired by MARHP & MIDCI, a Unit of Management by Objective (UGO), Regional Councils, CRDA/RPIT (Regional Project Implementation Teams), and Local Development Councils, with technical support from consultants and service providers and coordination across different levels.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: Targeted outcomes: improved economic opportunities from sustainable agricultural resource management for 250k rural households (1.5M people, including women/youth), boosting incomes, creating 25k jobs. Increase forest/rangeland market value 5x ($16M to $75M). Implement climate-smart agriculture and sustainable forest/rangeland management, reducing 5.5M tons CO2e, conserving biodiversity. Strengthen value chains, enhance market access, upgrade local infrastructure. Enhance governance, reduce elite capture. Success requires achieving these targets and a 14% economic IRR.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: Tunisia is navigating a fragile political transition post-2011 revolution, with orderly elections and a strong civil society, but also facing significant socio-economic challenges. Economic growth has been slow (1.4% post-revolution vs. 4.4% pre-revolution), with high and persistent unemployment (15.4% nationally, 31% for youth graduates, 19-26% in target regions). Deep regional disparities in development indicators and poverty (concentrated in the NW and CW regions, where 47% of the poor live) pose a significant challenge. Security incidents and regional instability further strain the environment. The agricultural sector, dominant in target areas, suffers from unsustainable practices, climate change threats, and limited infrastructure. Elite capture and favoritism risk undermining equitable project benefits. However, the project benefits from strong government commitment to decentralization and alignment with national development plans and green economy policies, as well as synergies with multiple international partners (AFD, IFAD, AfDB, JICA, UNDP, FAO), which are crucial for success.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: High complexity is driven by the project's multi-faceted approach to poverty, climate change, and institutional weaknesses. It integrates diverse climate-smart agriculture, phased forest/rangeland co-management, value chain strengthening for MSMEs, and infrastructure upgrades, requiring intricate coordination across many stakeholders and policy reforms.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This is a substantial $100 million project, not a sub-activity. It is highly integrated into national and international frameworks, aligning with Tunisia's New Five-year Development Plan, World Bank Group's strategic goals, MENA Regional Strategy, World Bank Forest Action Plan, and Agricultural Strategy, further reinforced by an inter-ministerial decision.

This comprehensive funding is crucial for strategic public investments, intensifying sustainable production, improving market access, and mitigating elite capture. Adequate financing is projected to significantly boost economic growth, potentially increasing forest and rangeland market value to US$75 million and creating around 25,000 jobs, fostering inclusion and sustainable development.\"]"
