ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P151086
ACTIVITY TITLE: Vietnam Energy Efficiency for Industrial Enterprises(VEEIE)
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-03-04
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-07-31

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 179,092,155

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: VN 100.00% | Location: Socialist Republic of Vietnam; Ho Chi Minh City; Thành Phố Thái Nguyên; Hanoi

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 4717

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Vietnam Energy Efficiency for Industrial Enterprises (VEEIE) project aims to improve energy efficiency within Vietnam's industrial sector, thereby contributing to the Government's broader energy efficiency and greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction objectives. The project is strategically aligned with the World Bank Energy Strategy and its Energy Engagement Strategy for Vietnam, focusing on supply- and demand-side energy efficiency. It also leverages the ongoing Clean Production and Energy Efficiency (CPEE) Project, which provides analytical support for industrial energy efficiency. Furthermore, the project aligns with Vietnam's National Energy Development Strategy, Green Growth Strategy, Law on Energy Efficiency and Conservation, and Power Development Plan VII, indicating strong government commitment and a favorable policy environment crucial for success. Beyond its primary objective, the project is expected to generate positive poverty reduction outcomes by lowering energy bills for consumers, reducing air pollution and CO2 emissions, and mitigating climate change impacts.

The primary beneficiaries of the project include industrial enterprises (IEs) and participating financial institutions (PFIs), with participating government agencies also receiving support. IEs will benefit from adopting improved technologies and optimizing production, leading to reduced energy consumption, lower production costs, and increased competitiveness in both domestic and international markets. PFIs will gain from the creation of new loan products tailored for industrial energy efficiency, enhancing their technical capacity in investment appraisal and monitoring, which is expected to facilitate scaled-up energy efficiency lending to industries. Support to government agencies will strengthen their capacity in developing regulatory frameworks, relevant energy efficiency standards, and guidelines, fostering a sustainable enabling environment for energy efficiency improvements. The project's success will be measured by key outcome performance indicators, including projected lifetime energy savings in MWh and the number of industrial enterprises adopting improved energy efficiency technologies, which are direct measures of its impact.

Intermediate performance indicators provide further insights into progress and factors influencing overall success. These include tracking the number of direct project beneficiaries, encompassing the percentage of female beneficiaries, total annual reductions of GHG in tons of CO2, and the development of a national energy efficiency database and a measuring, reporting, and verification (MRV) tool for the industrial sector. Additionally, the development of an energy efficiency circular specifically for the food processing industry and the number of bankable energy efficiency projects developed are important indicators of systemic and replicable progress. These detailed and multifaceted indicators, alongside strong alignment with national and international strategies and capacity building for key stakeholders, provide a robust framework for forecasting the overall success of the VEEIE activity.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The Vietnam Energy Efficiency for Industrial Enterprises (VEEIE) activity targets several key outcomes. Primarily, it aims to improve energy efficiency within Vietnam's industrial sector, contributing to the government's broader energy efficiency and greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction objectives. This includes generating positive poverty reduction outcomes by lowering energy bills for consumers, reducing air pollution and CO2 emissions, and mitigating climate change impacts. Industrial enterprises (IEs) are expected to benefit from adopting improved technologies and optimizing production, leading to reduced energy consumption, lower production costs, and increased competitiveness. Participating financial institutions (PFIs) will gain from new loan products for industrial energy efficiency and enhanced technical capacity, facilitating scaled-up lending. Government agencies will see strengthened capacity in developing regulatory frameworks, energy efficiency standards, and guidelines, fostering a sustainable enabling environment. The project also aims to remove principal barriers impeding industrial EE investments, addressing knowledge, institutional, and capacity-building needs for the banking and industrial sectors, and strengthening government supervision. Ultimately, the project seeks to meet future energy demand, help consumers save, reduce pollution, and mitigate climate change by reducing wasteful practices and adopting more efficient energy use technology, potentially saving up to 11 GW of new generation capacity by 2030 if comprehensive demand-side EE investments are carried out.To consider the activity an overall success, the following specific targets would need to be met by the end of the project: a projected lifetime energy savings of 4,639,000 MWh; 25 industrial enterprises adopting improved energy efficiency technologies; 37,822 direct project beneficiaries, with 30% being female beneficiaries; an annual reduction of 5,027,000 tons of CO2 equivalent in GHG emissions avoided in IEs; and the development of 60 bankable energy efficiency projects. Additional indicators of progress towards success include the development of a national energy efficiency database and a measuring, reporting, and verification (MRV) tool for the industrial sector, as well as an energy efficiency circular specifically for the food processing industry.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: Vietnam faces rapidly growing energy demand and resource constraints, making energy efficiency crucial for security. The government demonstrates strong commitment through VNEEP, EE laws, and ongoing electricity tariff reforms that boost EE investment attractiveness. High GHG emissions provide an environmental imperative. A favorable financial environment (strong credit growth, stable rates) supports project financing. The project directly targets existing market barriers (e.g., awareness, capacity, financing gaps), enhancing its success probability.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: High complexity due to addressing systemic market failures and barriers to industrial energy efficiency investments. It involves developing regulatory frameworks and standards, building technical capacity for financial institutions and industrial enterprises in project appraisal and development, and mitigating investment risks. This requires coordination among diverse stakeholders within a complex existing policy and institutional environment.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is highly integrated into larger national and international programs. It aligns with the World Bank Energy Strategy, Vietnam's National Energy Development Strategy, Green Growth Strategy, and relevant laws. It leverages the ongoing CPEE Project and is part of the broader Vietnam Energy Efficiency Program (VNEEP), a comprehensive national target program for EE and conservation.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Vietnam Energy Efficiency for Industrial Enterprises (VEEIE) project is budgeted at a total of US$315 million, with a financing gap of US$0.00, indicating that planned funding for the defined scope is secured. The financing is sourced from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) with US$200 million, the International Development Association (IDA) with US$3 million, and the Borrower/Recipient (Socialist Republic of Vietnam) contributing US$112 million. Two main components detail the specific sub-activities and their budgets:\n\n1.  **Energy Efficiency Investment Lending:** This component is budgeted at US$312 million. It constitutes an energy efficiency lending program designed to fund industrial energy efficiency projects over five years. The financing for this component is structured as follows: US$200 million from IBRD debt financing, an estimated US$50 million from Participating Financial Institutions (PFIs) through co-financing (20 percent of the loan to Industrial Enterprises, IEs), and an estimated US$62 million from IEs as equity financing (20 percent of total investments). Funds from the IBRD loan will be on-lent by the Ministry of Finance to selected PFIs, who will then lend to IEs and/or energy service companies. Examples of potential sub-projects include waste heat recovery for cement, steel, and iron industries; specific projects for six cement companies alone are identified with a potential investment cost of US$70.5 million. This significant lending component aims to drive direct investments in energy-saving technologies and practices within industries.\n\n2.  **Technical Assistance and Capacity Building for Improving Energy Efficiency (also referred to as Project Implementation Support):** This component is budgeted at US$3 million, financed through IDA. Its purpose is to provide crucial technical assistance and capacity building across various stakeholders. This includes assisting the Ministry of Industry and Trade (MoIT) and other government agencies in implementing energy efficiency policies, developing mandatory energy efficiency standards and guidelines, and improving incentives for industries. It also aims to enhance the technical capacity of PFIs in identifying, appraising, and implementing energy efficiency lending projects, and to support IEs and Energy Service Companies (ESCOs) in developing bankable projects. Additionally, it covers project monitoring, supervision, and audits.\n\nRegarding potential under-funding, the project officially states a \"Financing Gap\" of US$0.00, suggesting that the project is fully funded for its intended scope. However, the document notes that the total financing requirement for waste heat recovery across *all* cement production lines in Vietnam is approximately US$650 million. The project's US$312 million investment lending component, while substantial, addresses only a fraction of this broader national need for industrial energy efficiency investments. This implies that while the project's direct financial goals are met, it will only contribute to, rather than fully address, the overall energy efficiency investment requirements in the sector.\n\nThe activity would likely be well-financed given the explicit commitment of US$315 million from various sources and the absence of a stated financing gap. The structure, which includes contributions from IBRD, IDA, the Vietnamese government, PFIs, and IEs (through equity), promotes shared responsibility and aligns incentives, enhancing the likelihood of effective implementation. The project's emphasis on building the capacity of PFIs to create new energy efficiency loan products and IEs to adopt improved technologies is designed to create sustainable financing mechanisms beyond the project's timeframe, thereby improving long-term activity success. However, several risks, including \"Substantial\" ratings for Macroeconomic conditions, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Fiduciary aspects, could affect how effectively these funds are utilized. If these risks materialize, they could strain resources, hinder project execution, or reduce the ultimate impact of the investment, potentially leading to effective under-funding in terms of achieving optimal outcomes."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The Ministry of Industry and Trade (MoIT) is the primary implementing organization, responsible for overall project coordination, supervision, and implementation of the capacity building and technical assistance (TA) component. The MoIT leverages an existing Project Management Board (PMB) from the ongoing Clean Production and Energy Efficiency (CPEE) Project, which has a track record of effectively coordinating project activities, indicating an existing capacity for project management. However, the MoIT's knowledge and experience in safeguard implementation are considered limited, and new safeguard staff may be assigned, which could pose an initial challenge, though TA is planned to address this. The broader Government of Vietnam, of which MoIT is a part, demonstrates strong investment and commitment, as evidenced by the project's alignment with national energy strategies, laws, and decrees, and the successful implementation of the Vietnam Energy Efficiency Program (VNEEP) phases I and II since 2006. The MoIT's role in signing contracts and coordinating auditors, along with the government's strategic alignment and previous initiatives, suggests a foundational commitment.Key actors with significant skin-in-the-game are the participating financial institutions (PFIs), BIDV and VIETCOMBANK. They were competitively selected and will bear all financial risks, including debt service for the World Bank loan and FOREX risk if lending in Vietnamese dong, without any interest subsidy. This financial exposure demonstrates a strong commercial investment in the activity's success. However, PFIs currently have insufficient capacity in energy efficiency (EE) lending, investment appraisal, and risk management, necessitating substantial TA from the World Bank and IFC to develop new loan products, conduct due diligence, and build their technical capabilities. Despite this, some PFIs have prior experience participating in renewable energy development projects, which may offer transferable skills.Industrial enterprises (IEs) are the ultimate beneficiaries and will be responsible for implementing mitigation measures for safeguards. Similar to PFIs, IEs will also require significant TA and capacity building to identify, prepare, and implement EE projects, indicating a current need for enhanced capabilities at the enterprise level.Other relevant stakeholders are poised to significantly aid activity outcomes. The Government of Vietnam's strong policy environment and commitment, demonstrated through various national strategies and laws, create a favorable context for success. The World Bank and IFC provide essential funding, technical assistance, and project oversight, leveraging lessons from past similar projects. Crucially, a range of international donors, including the governments of Israel, Switzerland (SECO), Denmark (DANIDA), Germany (GIZ), and organizations like the Nordic Development Fund (NDF) and UNIDO, are engaged in discussions or parallel operations to support EE, providing critical complementary aid. The existing CPEE Project also offers analytical support. While the initial capacity gaps in PFIs and IEs, along with limited safeguard experience, present potential challenges for fund uptake and effective implementation, the comprehensive TA and capacity-building components are designed to mitigate these risks. The sheer scale of Vietnam's energy demand and GHG emissions also represents a substantial challenge that could deteriorate outcomes if the project's interventions are not robustly implemented.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- Weak government commitment and limited institutional capacity to promote industrial energy conservation, due to current policies, financial incentives, and overall capacity being insufficient to meet energy efficiency targets. A lack of institutional champions and fragmented EE measures also contribute to this risk.
- Slow subproject pipeline development, a common issue for credit lines. This is aggravated by a lack of awareness and capacity among industrial enterprises and financial institutions to identify and develop viable energy efficiency projects.
- Unfavorable macroeconomic developments, including interest rate volatility, FOREX risk, and low oil prices, could reduce incentives for industrial enterprises to invest in energy efficiency projects. Low or subsidized energy pricing and a small share of energy costs in operating costs further diminish interest.
- Institutional constraints and challenges related to energy efficiency investments in Vietnam, along with dependence on macroeconomic and fiscal stability, pose a substantial overall project risk. Concerns exist regarding the quality of lending due to quick credit growth and limited financing for upfront capital expenditure.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The activity demonstrates several positive indicators and reasons for potential success: 1. It utilizes a well-established success formula for credit line instruments, including careful selection of participating financial institutions (PFIs) against well-defined criteria, fostering competition among PFIs, ensuring strong management commitment, dedicated teams, and staff incentives within PFIs, providing technical assistance (TA) for project pipeline development and capacity building for both PFIs and industrial enterprises (IEs), and implementing aggressive marketing with new financial products tailored for energy efficiency (EE) financing. 2. Many Vietnamese banks are already familiar with credit lines, with several credit lines currently under implementation in Vietnam, and examples of significant financing for EE projects for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). 3. The project directly addresses the risk of weak government institutional capacity by providing TA and capacity building to the Ministry of Industry and Trade (MoIT) to strengthen EE policies and institutional capacity. 4. To mitigate slow subproject pipeline development, the project will provide TA and capacity building to PFIs for business development, link with Government's voluntary agreements with key IEs, and integrate with IFC's ongoing EE financing advisory services to PFIs. 5. Technical assistance will be provided to IEs and PFIs to identify, prepare, and implement EE projects, and a sensitivity analysis indicates that expected economic and financial returns are robust against macroeconomic risks. 6. The project is anticipated to yield overall positive social benefits, including reduced greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and pollutants, lower energy costs for consumers, enhanced competitiveness for IEs, and improved job security with potential workforce expansion. 7. The social risk for the project is rated as Low, following a comprehensive gender analysis as part of the social impact assessment. 8. ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-12-10
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-11-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 39,079,406

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: IQ 100.00% | Location: Erbil; Baghdad; Muḩāfaz̧at Bābil; Republic of Iraq; Kurdistan

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 6073

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: {
  "activity_information": "The Modernization of Public Financial Management Systems Project, also known as the Public Financial Management (PFM) Institutional Development and Capacity Building Project, aims to contribute to better fiscal management and improve budget management practices at both federal and governorate levels in Iraq, including the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG). Its development objective is to enhance the management and transparency of financial information, improve cash management, strengthen public investment management, and modernize public procurement in selected federal and governorate institutions. The project focuses on institutional development, capacity building, change management, and a gradual introduction of new practices supported by information systems to strengthen the broader PFM modernization environment, with expected results being foundational and discrete. The main output of the project is the Integrated Financial Management Information System (IFMIS) operating in the Ministry of Finance.\n\nKey sub-components include the modernization of public procurement and project management/reform coordination. Under public procurement, the project supports the implementation of recently enacted procurement instructions and the establishment of an e-portal for the Kurdistan Regional Government. This involves strengthening the capacities of the General Contracts Directorate in the Ministry of Planning, developing complaint review mechanisms, increasing transparency by publishing procurement notices and contract awards on the e-portal for wider access, and building sustainable capacity and professional integrity among public sector staff in procurement through online courses. For project management and reform coordination, the project provides resources to the Kurdistan Region's Ministry of Planning to lead technical aspects and overall program management, ensuring project objectives are met on time and aligned with quality and budget requirements. The overall General Financial Management component has a cost of 32.30 million USD.\n\nThe project addresses the challenge of rebuilding state institutions in Iraq, which have been systematically weakened over the past thirty years, and the impact of oil price and ISIS shocks on Iraq's economy and public services. Strengthening PFM is seen as critical for enhancing governance, optimizing public resource outputs, and fostering inclusive and broad-based development. The IFMIS is intended to improve the transparency and accountability of the budget process and enhance the skills and capacity of budget regulatory institutions and line ministries. The project aligns with the Bank Group's FY13-FY16 Country Partnership Strategy for Iraq, focusing on delivering basic public services, reducing poverty, enhancing trust in government institutions, addressing fiscal situations, and increasing private sector investment. It is expected to contribute to greater accountability, transparency, lower corruption, more effective public service delivery, and sustainable economic growth. The technical design is informed by the World Bank's experience in Iraq and other fragile/conflict-affected environments, involving experts from the Federal Government of Iraq, Kurdistan Regional Government, World Bank, IMF, and UK's Department for International Development."
}

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The activity targets better fiscal management, improved budget practices (federal/governorate, including KRG), enhanced financial information transparency, improved cash and public investment management, and modernized public procurement in selected institutions. It aims for an operational Integrated Financial Management Information System (IFMIS) in the Ministry of Finance, a KRG e-portal for procurement, strengthened capacities, and increased transparency. Ultimately, it seeks greater accountability, lower corruption, effective public service delivery, and sustainable economic growth."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "Iraq faces severe macroeconomic instability due to declining oil prices (US$40B revenue loss, 14.5% GDP deficit in 2015) and a volatile oil market. The ISIS insurgency and broader state institution weakness after 30 years divert resources, increasing security/humanitarian expenditures. Competing government priorities (security, reconstruction) and potential bureaucratic resistance pose significant risks to sustained reform focus and implementation success. Linking strategies to budget allocations also remains a challenge."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The project involves a complex set of reforms aiming to modernize public financial management systems in Iraq, a country with weakened institutions and operating in a fragile environment. It requires significant institutional development, extensive capacity building, and change management for a transition to a digital, data-driven culture, along with the implementation and integration of an Integrated Financial Management Information System (IFMIS) across federal and subnational levels.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is part of a larger programmatic approach to PFM reforms in Iraq. It lays the foundations for subsequent projects for the further roll-out of these reforms. It is also directly aligned with the Bank Group's FY13-FY16 Country Partnership Strategy for Iraq, indicating it is cohesively integrated into broader strategic objectives.

FINANCING DETAILS: "Specific activities include modernizing public procurement (e-portal, capacity building), project management/reform coordination (KRG MoP), and implementing an Integrated Financial Management Information System (IFMIS). The General Financial Management component is budgeted at 32.30 million USD. No typical cost or discrepancy details are given. Iraq faces severe financial constraints from war and low oil prices, with deficits up to 13.4% of GDP. External financing from IBRD is crucial. Urgent security/financial needs could challenge project success, though PFM strengthening is vital for fiscal health and governance."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: "The activity will be implemented by three Project Management Teams (PMTs) established within the Federal Ministry of Finance, Federal Ministry of Planning, and the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) Ministry of Planning. Each PMT will handle activities aligned with its expertise and will report directly to the respective Minister or Deputy Minister, indicating a high level of institutional investment and oversight. The PMT in the Federal Ministry of Finance is responsible for IFMIS components, PFM capacity building, internal audit updates, and local financial management, coordinating with governorates. The Federal Ministry of Planning's PMT will manage public investment and procurement reforms, while the KRG Ministry of Planning's PMT will implement all regional activities. Each PMT will include a full-time team leader, technical staff, liaison officers for sub-components, procurement, finance/accounting, internal control, and monitoring & evaluation specialists. The Federal Ministry of Finance's PMT will also have an IFMIS manager. These implementing organizations demonstrate prior experience, as their departments (Budget, Accounting, IT, Internal Audit, Debt Management, and Treasury in Baghdad/Babil) were previously involved in a 2009-2013 PFM project, building a foundation for current efforts. Readiness for the project is also evident, with ongoing activities such as IFMIS bidding preparations, capacity needs assessments, and drafting of roles for IFMIS, financial, and procurement officers. A Steering Committee, comprising representatives from participating ministries, will provide strategic guidance, ensure effective coordination, and monitor project progress, indicating collective investment in successful outcomes. The activity benefits from significant support from various development partners, aiding its outcomes. The International Bank for Reconstruction and Development is a key funder and manager, providing technical assistance and informing the project's technical design with its experience in fragile contexts. USAID provides advisors for PFM in Baghdad and KRG, contributing to payroll, tax, and chart of accounts improvements, and supports broader economic and PFM reforms through UN development mechanisms. The IMF's Rapid Financing Instrument and Extended Fund Facility support fiscal management, a treasury single account, commitment controls, IFMIS, public investment management, and anti-corruption efforts. JICA provides budget support for public investment and debt management reforms. The European Union is preparing a 'Democratic Governance Program' to assist Iraqi accountability institutions (Board of Supreme Audit, Commission of Integrity, parliamentary committees), complementing World Bank efforts to complete the accountability loop. This strong network of committed development partners and prior experience within the implementing ministries suggests a high likelihood of effective performance."
ACTIVITY RISKS: "Relevant risks include: public financial management reform being in its early stages with disparate budget/accounting systems; absence of accurate/timely reports, effective contingency reserve, and a genuine investment budget; weak institutional linkages and procurement procedures for conflict of interest; ineffective internal/external audit functions; weak PFM systems at governorate levels; lack of political capital to overcome bureaucratic resistance; urgent security and financial imperatives diverting government focus; and difficulty in linking strategies to budget allocations with inefficient resource allocation."

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Project builds on recent PFM reform progress, including a new PFM Law (2016) drafted with IMF/World Bank consultation. It features improved budget preparation with a medium-term expenditure framework, new GFS-aligned budget classification/chart of accounts, and a formal public investment management framework (Oct 2015 Prime Minister's decision). Transparency is enhanced by publishing federal budget information online. The technical design is informed by experts from the World Bank, IMF, UK, and Iraqi federal/KRG governments."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P151502
ACTIVITY TITLE: Poverty Reduction Support Credit III
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-11-12
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2017-12-31

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Single location

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 20,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: LR 100.00% | Location: Monrovia

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 846

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Poverty Reduction Support Credit III is a Development Policy Lending operation for Liberia, with a tentative financing of $20.0 million from the International Development Association (IDA), and an estimated Board Approval date of July 3, 2016. Its proposed objectives are to sustain and deepen government-owned efforts to reform governance and civil service, and to support the broadening of reforms to include economic transformation and human capital development within the context of Liberia's second Poverty Reduction Strategy, the Agenda for Transformation (AfT). The AfT, launched in 2012 following a relatively peaceful 2011 election and a fresh mandate to President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf, aims for Liberia to achieve middle-income country status by 2030. The operation focuses on three primary pillars: (i) strengthening governance, particularly emphasizing transparency, accountability, anti-corruption, budget execution, and oversight; (ii) creating an environment for economic transformation by addressing infrastructure constraints such as energy, roads, ports, and telecommunications; and (iii) improving human capital development through enhanced access and quality of health and education services. The reforms are selective, targeting issues of poverty, fragility, and conflict, with special attention to rebuilding more resilient health systems, and are intended to build resilience to future shocks. The project is initiated against a backdrop of both progress and significant challenges. Since returning to democratic governance in 2006, Liberia achieved notable economic and social gains, including an average GDP growth of 7% between 2006-2011, a reduction in national poverty incidence from 64% in 2007 to 56% in 2010, and a slight decrease in the GINI coefficient from 0.36 to 0.35. However, the country faces primary development challenges related to sustaining peace, achieving economic transformation, human capital development, and improving governance and public institutions, exacerbated by a history of exclusion, inequality, and corruption. A major factor impacting the activity's potential outcome is the Ebola Virus Disease crisis, which struck in March 2014, severely impairing the government's capacity to deliver basic services, disrupting economic activities across all sectors, heightening social and political tensions, and incurring substantial fiscal costs. The government's fiscal resources from tax and non-tax revenues were also well below pre-crisis levels due to a substantial slowdown in economic activities and sustained low prices for rubber and iron ore. Despite these setbacks, the AfT remains the central framework, and the proposed reforms are specifically intended to build resilience to future shocks like the Ebola and commodity price crises, with prior actions expected to yield direct and indirect positive poverty and social effects by increasing fiscal space and promoting efficient resource use and inclusive growth. The reforms are not expected to have any significant direct environmental effects. The implementing agency is the Ministry of Finance and Development Planning of Liberia, and the PID was prepared on February 29, 2016."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "[\"The activity targets sustaining governance and civil service reforms, and broadening reforms to include economic transformation and human capital development under the AfT. Outcomes include strengthening transparency, accountability, anti-corruption, budget execution, and oversight; addressing infrastructure constraints for economic expansion, diversification, and job creation; and improving access/quality of health/education for the poor. It also aims to build resilience to shocks, increase fiscal space, and reduce poverty/inequity.\",\"Success would be evidenced by sustained governance and civil service reforms, and broadened reforms in economic transformation and human capital development. Key indicators include greater transparency, accountability, and reduced corruption, leading to efficient public service delivery, especially to the poor. Also, economic expansion and diversification with more jobs, improved infrastructure, and better access/quality of health/education, building resilience to shocks, increased fiscal space, and reduced poverty/inequity.\"]"

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: Liberia's economy faces significant external vulnerabilities, largely due to the 2014 Ebola epidemic and a severe fall in commodity prices (iron ore, rubber), leading to flat GDP in 2015 and large fiscal deficits. These shocks exacerbated pre-existing challenges like sustaining peace, inequality, and weak governance. The IMF projects a recovery with 2.5% GDP growth in 2016 and 5.5% medium-term, driven by services, mining, and infrastructure. Key risks include further commodity price shocks, worsening security, Ebola re-emergence, and policy slippages. Success depends on the government's ability to implement sound macroeconomic policies, structural reforms, and financial resilience measures amid this volatile external environment, as emphasized by international partners.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity involves complex multi-pillar reforms in governance, economic transformation, and human capital development within Liberia's 'Agenda for Transformation'. It addresses poverty, fragility, and conflict in a post-Ebola context, aiming to build resilience to future shocks. This policy lending operation requires significant strategic oversight and coordination.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity, 'Poverty Reduction Support Credit III', is a Development Policy Lending operation directly and cohesively integrated into Liberia's larger national program, the 'Agenda for Transformation' (AfT). The AfT is Liberia's second Poverty Reduction Strategy, a comprehensive framework aiming for middle-income status by 2030.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Poverty Reduction Support Credit III operation has total financing of SDR 28.7 million, equivalent to US$40 million. This includes US$8 million from the Crisis Response Window (CRW), and the full amount will be provided to the Government of Liberia (GoL) as a single tranche. The specific budgetary allocation for each sub-activity is not detailed in the provided documents. The activity is structured around three primary pillars:\n(i) Strengthening governance, with a focus on transparency, accountability, anti-corruption, budget execution, and oversight.\n(ii) Creating an environment for economic transformation by addressing infrastructure constraints such as energy, roads, ports, and telecommunications.\n(iii) Improving human capital development through enhanced access and quality of health and education services.\n\nRegarding the cost of the activity, the total grant from IDA is US$40 million. The activity appears to be crucial in addressing a broader fiscal environment in Liberia that is severely under-funded. Due to the Ebola crisis and sustained low commodity prices, Liberia's revenues are significantly below pre-crisis forecasts and deemed \"inadequate to meet the expanded expenditure needs.\" The government's fiscal deficit widened substantially to 8.4 percent of GDP in FY15. The overall FY16 budget, for example, projected US$622.7 million for expenditure, with US$514.9 million for recurrent expenditure and US$107.8 million for the public sector investment program; however, domestic revenues faced a 12 percent reduction. The government explicitly requested \"urgent budgetary support from donors to help finance this abnormally large financing gap,\" noting that \"Such support will be crucial to help maintain the delivery of key social services.\" This indicates that the government's overall reform efforts and essential service delivery are likely under-funded by its domestic resources.\n\nTherefore, the US$40 million financing from this credit is critical for the activity's success. It provides vital fiscal space to the GoL, enabling it to sustain government-owned reform efforts, maintain essential social services, and pursue broader development goals within the context of the Agenda for Transformation. This support is intended to build resilience to future shocks by increasing fiscal space and promoting efficient resource use and inclusive growth. Without this external financing, the government's capacity to implement the outlined reforms and deliver essential services would be severely hampered due to its constrained domestic revenue base."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: Implementing organization: Ministry of Finance and Development Planning of Liberia. Government's skin-in-the-game: "government-owned efforts" (AfT), presidential commitment to fiscal prudence, and robust audit/confirmation mechanisms. Capabilities are hampered by generally weak state capacity, Ebola's impact, and fiduciary weaknesses, yet bolstered by donor-supported M&E, technical assistance, and World Bank training. Aiding stakeholders: IDA, EU, AfDB, IMF providing financial/technical support; UNMIL aiding security. Deteriorating factors: potential resistance from "perceived losers" of anti-corruption reforms.
ACTIVITY RISKS: Substantial overall risk. Key risks: Ebola epidemic (recurrence affecting all domains/reforms). Political and Governance (fragile security, 2017 election instability, difficulty securing reform commitment). Macroeconomic (dependence on FDI/exports, low commodity prices, external shocks, fiscal risks, economic slowdown). Institutional capacity (weak state capacity, implementation delays, sustainability issues). Fiduciary (weaknesses allowing misappropriation). Also unemployment and financial sector adverse effects. These factors impede success.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "[\"Prior actions are expected to yield direct and indirect positive poverty and social effects, increasing fiscal space for Ebola recovery and AfT priorities, and mitigating commodity price shocks. Reforms will foster efficient and transparent use of public resources, promote inclusive growth, employment, and reduce poverty and inequity. The reforms are not expected to have significant negative direct environmental effects, with specific mitigation measures like the Oil Spill Response Plan outlined to address risks proactively.\",\"The Government demonstrates strong political commitment to Public Financial Management (PFM) reforms, with considerable actions taken to improve PFM since 2008. Mitigation efforts for Ebola include maintaining hand-washing protocols, government collaboration with partners to build resilient health systems, and direct budget support. The operation's reforms are likely to reduce corruption and rent-seeking, supported by external assistance for security and ongoing World Bank engagement on reform.\",\"The President's commitment to fiscal prudence and demonstrated prudent macroeconomic management, along with joint monitoring by the World Bank and IMF, contribute to stability. The World Bank consistently provides training and technical assistance for state building, complemented by multiple donor projects. The continued engagement of development partners has enhanced Liberia's fiduciary environment, with notable progress, government's IFMIS roll-out, and strengthened procurement capacity.\",\"The Government of Liberia remains committed to its Agenda for Transformation, has learned from crises to improve its strategy, and recent policy actions incrementally improve recovery ability. Legislative actions like the Freedom of Information Act and Anti-Money Laundering Law, alongside the establishment of a Financial Intelligence Unit, demonstrate commitment to transparency and anti-corruption. Executive Directors commended the authorities' measures to tackle shocks, ambitious FY2017 budget, and revenue mobilization while securing essential social spending.\"]"

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P151835
ACTIVITY TITLE: BI-Social Safety Nets (Merankabandi)
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-07-23
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: BI 100.00% | Location: Mabanda; Matana; Bukirasazi; Butaganzwa; Taba; Nyabikere; Butezi; Bweru; Gisuru; Mutumba

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 153

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Merankabandi social safety net program aims to reach its development objectives by alleviating poverty, preventing chronic malnutrition, and promoting investments in human capital among extreme poor and vulnerable rural households in Burundi. The project is structured around three components, with Component 1 supporting the Merankabandi cash transfer program, Component 2 supporting the development of key delivery mechanisms for a basic social safety net system, and Component 3 supporting project implementation, management, monitoring, and evaluation. The total project cost is US$40 million, financed entirely by the International Development Association (IDA).\n\nComponent 1, with an equivalent of US$26.7 million, focuses on the development and phased implementation of the Merankabandi cash transfer program, envisioned as a precursor to a national program targeting approximately 300,000 of the poorest households across Burundi. Initially, it will support 48,000 households with children under 12 years of age in the Northern and Eastern regions, specifically 24,000 in Karuzi and Ruyigi provinces in the first wave, and another 12,000 in Gitega and Kirundo in the second wave. The program combines regular cash payments with promotion activities to encourage positive behaviors in nutrition, early childhood development, and school attendance. Beneficiary households are expected to remain in the program for three years. The targeting process involves community-based targeting followed by a proxy-means test, with the final list validated by the community. While payments are initially unconditional, the project will explore making them conditional on participation in behavior change communication (BCC) activities and the use of basic services based on implementation performance at the mid-term review. The program specifically targets women as cash transfer recipients and children as targets for behavior change activities.\n\nSubcomponent 1.2, allocated US$7.2 million, is dedicated to behavior change for investments in human capital. Its curriculum covers program objectives, child and pregnant women's nutrition, early childhood development (e.g., positive parenting, play-based learning), mother and child health (e.g., pregnancy risks, family planning, malaria prevention), and education, skills, and income-generation (e.g., micro-finance access). These modules are implemented through facilitator manuals, support materials (posters, booklets, radio, video), developed with UNICEF's contribution, and tailored to the Burundian rural context, addressing gender norms, chronic child malnutrition, and interpersonal violence. The promotional activities target the entire household, engaging all members above 15 years, including mothers, fathers, and children through age-appropriate play-based learning, with sessions potentially open to other community members. The program relies on other social and development actors to design promotion activities and ensure the supply of basic services. Implementation responsibilities are distributed among a steering committee, the Permanent Executive Secretary of the National Social Protection Commission (SEP-CNPS), the Ministry of Human Rights, Social Affairs and Gender (MDPHASG), and an Implementation Support Unit responsible for technical and financial execution, M&E, and managing social and environmental impacts. Indicators for monitoring progress include the number of beneficiaries (total, female, unconditional cash transfer recipients) and the proportion of beneficiary households below the extreme poverty line."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The BI-Social Safety Nets (Merankabandi) activity primarily targets the alleviation of extreme poverty, prevention of chronic malnutrition, and promotion of human capital investments among vulnerable rural households in Burundi. Key outcomes include providing regular cash transfers, strengthening social safety net delivery mechanisms, and encouraging positive behaviors in nutrition, early childhood development, and school attendance through behavior change communication (BCC) activities. It also aims to boost productive investments, reduce negative coping strategies, and stimulate local economies."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "Burundi's extreme poverty (184th HDI), widespread chronic malnutrition (71% stunting), and food insecurity present a challenging baseline. The context of \"recurrent fragility and entrenched structural poverty\" and weak government institutional capacity (financial management, procurement) results in \"Substantial\" project risk. Cultural norms limiting women's economic roles further complicate gender-focused goals. Yet, strong alignment with the National Social Protection Strategy (PNPS, 2015), developed with multi-partner support (WB, UNICEF, AfDB), provides a supportive policy framework. Transparency in targeting and coordination with other aid programs enhance success probability in this difficult environment."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity, BI-Social Safety Nets (Merankabandi), presents significant complexity. It is structured into three components, encompassing the phased implementation of a cash transfer program, the development of key delivery mechanisms for a national social safety net system, and comprehensive project management, monitoring, and evaluation. The cash transfer component involves targeting approximately 300,000 extreme poor households in phases, initially assisting 48,000 households with children in specific regions. This requires a robust targeting process combining community-based methods with proxy-means tests. A novel aspect is the integration of regular cash payments with extensive behavior change communication activities designed to promote human capital investments in nutrition, early childhood development, and school attendance. The BCC curriculum is tailored to the Burundian rural context, addressing sensitive issues like gender norms and interpersonal violence, and requires collaboration with UNICEF and other social actors for content design and basic service supply. Furthermore, the program faces logistical challenges inherent in cash transfers in Burundi, a country where such programs are new. This includes the potential use of multiple payment methods to ensure timely and accurate delivery, considering limited infrastructure and beneficiary accessibility. The development of a beneficiary database and management information systems, including support for beneficiaries to obtain national ID cards, adds further layers of technical and operational complexity.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The Merankabandi program is a large-scale initiative intended as a direct precursor to a national social safety net program in Burundi. It is structured as a significant program in its own right, composed of three main components: a cash transfer program, the development of key delivery mechanisms for a basic national social safety net system, and project management. Rather than being independent or isolated, it is explicitly designed to lay the groundwork for a broader, country-wide system. Its activities, particularly Component 2, are focused on establishing foundational delivery mechanisms that will serve a larger, future national social safety net. The program is also highly integrated within Burundi's national social protection framework, involving coordination with national government bodies at both central and local levels.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The BI-Social Safety Nets (Merankabandi) project has a total budget of US$40 million. Component 1, the Merankabandi cash transfer program, is budgeted at US$26.7 million. Subcomponent 1.1 (Cash transfers) is US$19.5 million, providing BIF 20,000/month (US$145/year) per household, consistent with comparable African countries (18% of food poverty line). Initial payments may include BIF 40,000 and a cell phone, then quarterly, moving to monthly. Subcomponent 1.2 (Behavior change) is US$7.2 million, supporting nutrition, ECD, health, and education activities via various materials. Major procurement includes Vehicles (US$360k), Motorbikes (US$72k), Hardware (US$70k), Server (US$200k). Implementation support is US$100k for the first 12 months, US$100k for 12-36 months, and US$100k annually thereafter."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: "The organization implementing the activity, the Ministry of Human Rights, Social Affairs and Gender (MDPHASG), along with its Permanent Executive Secretariat of the National Social Protection Commission (SEP-CNPS) and a dedicated Project Implementation Unit (PIU), appears to have a structured and capable approach. The SEP-CNPS, established in 2014, has a clear mandate for coordinating, executing, monitoring, and evaluating social protection programs, including outreach at provincial and communal levels. The National Directorate of Social Assistance and National Solidarity within MDPHASG brings experience in humanitarian assistance to vulnerable groups and links with commune-level Family Development Centers and social workers for local implementation and monitoring. The PIU will be led by experienced technicians, co-located with the SEP, and responsible for comprehensive project functions including procurement, financial management, M&E, and social/environmental impact mitigation. A key strength is the strategic plan to embed the PIU within the SEP/CNPS at mid-term review, based on capacity building results, indicating a long-term vision for institutional strengthening and sustained performance. The project also directly aims to improve the country's social protection infrastructure by developing key delivery mechanisms such as a beneficiary database, payment module, and M&E system to address current inefficiencies, which demonstrates commitment to improving core abilities.Strong 'skin-in-the-game' and investment from key actors are evident. The International Development Association (funder) and International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (manager) bring "
ACTIVITY RISKS: "The project faces substantial risks: it's a new approach in Burundi during a crisis, with limited coordination and implementation capacity. ACTIVITY TITLE: Mali - Economic & Environmental Rehabilitation of the Niger River
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-11-16
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 27,800,000

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Mali - Economic & Environmental Rehabilitation of the Niger River project (P151909), approved on March 14, 2018, with a closing date of September 15, 2023, is classified as Investment Project Financing under Environmental Assessment Category A (Full Assessment). This project is part of a broader effort linked to Mali's Country Partnership Framework, the Niger Basin Climate Resilience Investment Plan, and the World Bank's Africa Climate Business Plan. Its primary objectives are to demonstrate the effectiveness of navigation and port service improvement measures, the feasibility of environmental restoration and livelihood improvement activities at targeted sites in the Niger Inner Delta (NID), and to enhance the Recipient's capacity to respond promptly and effectively to Eligible Crises or Emergencies. The project, which does not involve Bank/IFC collaboration or regional tagging, comprises three main components: Strengthening the Strategic Management of the Niger River Resources (US$11.00 million), Enhancing Viability of Socio-Economic Activities Contributing to Climate Change Resilience (US$11.80 million), and Project management (US$5.00 million). Key expected results include removing barriers to navigability through dredging, improving the reliability and safety of Inland Water Transportation (IWT) through infrastructure and enhanced regulations, ensuring the sustainability of IWT through maintenance definition, decreasing sediment deposition via effective trapping mechanisms, limiting impacts on critical flora and fauna, and implementing efficient fishing and agricultural practices. Beneficiaries are primarily the local population of the NID, including transport operators, village groups, farmers, fishers, youth organizations, women, and vulnerable populations, with an initial focus on communities in Kolongo, Kokry, and Macina, totaling about 100,000 people within a larger project area of 0.5 million inhabitants. Additional beneficiaries include various subnational actors, decentralized technical departments, civil society organizations, associations, and local authorities, reflecting a participatory design approach that consulted civil society, considered fragile population segments, and took into account local Social, Economic, and Cultural Development Plans (PDSEC). Project performance will be monitored using indicators such as average vessel speed between Diafarabe and Macina, area restored/developed in the NID through opening lateral channels, number of local villages with access to improved livelihood activities, and quantity of traffic at ports by COMANAV. Rural community development activities and site selections were determined via multicriteria analysis based on commune priorities and local development plans, supplemented by field investigations and consultations. Acknowledging significant investment needs in areas like irrigated fields, fishponds, and bourgoutieres (inundated areas for animal fodder grass), the project aims to serve as a catalyst for further development beyond its immediate financing limitations, demonstrating the synergy between livelihoods and improved river transport. The original intervention strategy emphasized reinforcing productivity and competitiveness through intensification, natural resource safeguarding, and value chain development. Main activities supported, derived from PDSEC analysis and local workshops, include 136 hectares of rice farming with PIV, 27 hectares of market gardens with PIV for women, 205 hectares of tree crops for youth, 11.5 hectares of fishponds, 1,000 hectares of ponds for fish stocking, and 2,051 hectares of bourgoutieres to be restored or developed, with prioritization based on demand and site suitability for longer-term implementation. A primary challenge addressed by the project is the degradation of riverbanks and channels caused by water and wind erosion, exacerbated by increased siltation since 1965. Protective measures include bank stabilization, such as vegetative plantations along 4.5 linear km of riverbanks and 93 km of channels, covering approximately 471.25 hectares in total, to protect rehabilitated areas from vegetation loss, human and animal usage, and river current erosion. Priority channels identified for rehabilitation in the Mopti region include Daga Kironguel (approx. 4 km) for Soye village and Wango (approx. 10 km) for Deboye village, both supporting fishing, agriculture, and transport. The long-term maintenance of rehabilitated sites will necessitate monitoring by communities and local collectives, requiring developed procedures, organized committees, revisited local regulations, and capacity building, with previous successful demonstrations of protective vegetation management in Banankoro suggesting the feasibility of long-term viability with effective management."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "Targeted outcomes: Improve Niger River's economic/environmental services for NID population/ecosystems. Demonstrate effective navigation/port services, feasible environmental restoration, improved livelihoods, and enhanced crisis response. Key results: removed navigability barriers (dredging), safer/more reliable IWT (infrastructure/regulations), sustainable IWT (maintenance), decreased sedimentation, protected flora/fauna, efficient fishing/agriculture, specific rehabilitation (e.g., 136ha rice, 2051ha bourgoutieres). Success: vessel speed Diafarabé-Macina 14->30km/hr; 50ha NID restored via lateral channels; 12 villages access improved livelihoods; port traffic (passengers) 1,000->10,000. Requires community monitoring, capacity building, and acts as catalyst for further development."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "The external context presents significant challenges for project success. Mali's civil strife since 2012, weak governance, and high poverty (36% below the poverty line) create an unstable operational environment. The economy is highly vulnerable, dependent on rain-fed agriculture and volatile commodity prices, making it susceptible to external shocks. Upstream dams, existing and planned across the Niger River's international basin, significantly impact water flow and Inner Delta livelihoods, posing a major hydrological risk outside Mali's direct control. A critical impediment is the lack of an adopted national regulatory framework for Inland Water Transportation and insufficient local capacity and funding for waterway maintenance, threatening project sustainability. These factors collectively increase the probability of implementation delays and long-term viability issues."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is highly complex, involving multi-sectoral interventions including environmental engineering (dredging, bank stabilization, sediment trapping, ecosystem restoration), infrastructure development (ports, channels), socio-economic development (livelihoods, agriculture, fishing, market gardens, tree crops, fishponds, bourgoutieres), and institutional strengthening (regulations, capacity building for long-term maintenance). Addressing the degradation of riverbanks and channels due to erosion and siltation, implementing large-scale vegetative plantations, and ensuring sustainable management with community involvement are significant technical and logistical challenges. The project aims to demonstrate effectiveness and feasibility, requiring robust monitoring and adaptive management across diverse beneficiary groups.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is a significant program in itself with a total cost of US$27.80 million and multiple components. It is deeply integrated and cohesive within larger regional and national development frameworks, explicitly described as 'part of a broader effort linked to Mali's Country Partnership Framework, the Niger Basin Climate Resilience Investment Plan, and the World Bank's Africa Climate Business Plan.' It also ensures close coordination with a portfolio of other pipeline and active projects in disaster resilience, natural resources management, and water resources development, establishing a holistic vision for the Niger Inner Delta.

FINANCING DETAILS: "Total project cost/financing: US$27.80M from IDA. No financing gap for the approved scope. Main components: Strategic Management (US$11M), Socio-Economic Viability (US$11.8M), Project Management (US$5M). Specific activities: Dredging (US$3.25M for 7 sandbars, reduced from US$13.5M due to resource limitations). Riverbank/Channel works total US$8.3M, including engineering (US$3.85M), channel rehabilitation (US$3.42M), new channels (US$0.58M), and vegetative plantations (US$0.56M). Financing limitations mean community activities aim to be a 'catalyst' beyond immediate funding. High/Substantial risks (Political, Macroeconomic, Fiduciary) may affect success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: "The Agence de Bassin du Fleuve Niger (ABFN) is the primary implementing organization, responsible for day-to-day activities and leading the Project Management Unit. While ABFN was established in 2002, it has historically been \"relatively ineffective... due to lack of resources and unclear mandate.\" The project aims to counter this by providing clear responsibilities, establishing a comprehensive governance structure, and allocating dedicated technical staff (e.g., specialists in river hydrology, civil engineering, M&E, social development, environmental/natural resources, and GIS) to its Mopti office. This direct address to ABFN's past weaknesses, along with its central role in project oversight and execution, indicates a significant institutional investment and effort to enhance its capabilities for this activity.\n\nThe Republic of Mali is accountable for the project and bears the responsibility for resettlement indemnities, demonstrating a financial and governmental commitment. ABFN's Board of Directors is a 25-member multisectoral body, including representatives from various ministries (Environment, Water and Energy, Agriculture, Finance, etc.), local collectivities (Regional Assemblies, Municipalities), and user groups (Chambers of Agriculture, Commerce, and Industry). ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Second Energy Sector Project, identified as P152343, is an Investment Project Financing initiative in Mongolia with an estimated total cost of $35.00 million, fully financed by $5.00 million from the Borrower/Recipient and $30.00 million from the International Development Association (IDA), resulting in a $0.00 financing gap. Prepared and updated on June 7, 2016, and approved/disclosed on June 10, 2016, by the GEE02 managing unit, the project is led by Task Team Leader Peter Johansen, with an estimated appraisal date of October 3, 2016, and board date of March 23, 2017. Classified as a "B - Partial Assessment" environmental category and a repeater project, its development objective is to improve the reliability of and access to electricity services and revenue collection in two regional distribution utilities in Mongolia. The project targets energy efficiency in heat and power and transmission and distribution of electricity, with themes including regulation and competition policy, city-wide infrastructure and service delivery, and rural services and infrastructure.

The project description outlines investments in high priority distribution infrastructure, information communication technology (ICT), and technical assistance (TA). It aims to create new assets or rehabilitate existing power system parts in two regional distribution companies—Baganuur-Southeastern Region Electricity Distribution Network (BSEDN) and Erdenet-Bulgan Electricity Distribution Network (EBEDN)—identified by the Government of Mongolia for investment. Infrastructure investments will cover distribution lines and substations, and to a lesser extent, transmission lines. ICT investment will support improved corporate and operations management, while TA will enable project implementation by distribution and transmission companies.

The project is structured into three components: Component A focuses on the Upgrade and Expansion of Regional Distribution Companies, with an estimated cost of $29.2 million ($24.2 million IDA credit). This component targets BSEDN and EBEDN to reduce system losses and improve electricity service reliability and access through power infrastructure strengthening. Activities include rehabilitating, upgrading, and expanding existing substations and lines, constructing new substations and lines, installing e-meters and advanced metering infrastructure, and providing training in network design, equipment installation, power loss reduction, and basic financial management. It also plans for additional substation capacity to meet new loads and enhance system reliability. Component B, with an estimated cost of $1.8 million (all IDA credit), aims to improve the efficiency of the National Power Transmission Grid (NPTG) through installing switchgears with vacuum circuit breakers, providing software for planning and management, and building staff capacity, thereby reducing transmission losses and improving network efficiency. Component C, Project Management, Technical Assistance, and Capacity Building, costs an estimated $4.0 million (all IDA credit) and seeks to strengthen the capacity of the Project Management Office (PMO) under the Ministry of Energy and the implementing units (PIUs) of the regional distribution companies through technical assistance for project implementation and special studies, training programs to enhance project management capabilities, and covering project management costs.

For forecasting overall activity success, several factors are pertinent. The project is explicitly linked to broader sector reforms, with the Bank providing parallel support to the Government of Mongolia through the Scaling up Renewable Energy Program (SREP) for renewable energy development and capacity building for the Energy Regulatory Commission (ERC). This SREP support to ERC, which focuses on improving tariff methodologies and financial pass-through provisions, is deemed "critical" for ensuring improved financial performance and sustainable operation of the regional distribution companies under ESP2. Additionally, a TA activity from the Asia Sustainable and Alternative Energy Program (ASTAE) is being designed to review investment plans, establish social baselines, address affordability issues, and diagnose the power sector's regulatory environment, which will inform and strengthen the project's foundation. The project's design also incorporates lessons learned from previous Bank projects in Mongolia, emphasizing the critical importance of early, intensive, and continuous training and capacity building for PIUs, hands-on technical assistance to utilities, focused and pragmatic regulatory support, and ongoing high-level policy dialogue.

Project location details indicate BSEDN serves 46,568 customers in the Baganuur District and 56 soums across 7 aimags, primarily residential. EBEDN serves 65,400 customers across 108 soums in 11 aimags, with a majority of residential customers residing in ger areas, where most residents are poor, highlighting a focus on critical needs. The Borrower's institutional capacity for safeguard policies appears robust, with the PMO familiar with Bank-funded projects and having developed internal capacity, plus plans to hire environmental and social safeguards consultants. Environmental Assessment (OP/BP 4.01), Natural Habitats (OP/BP 4.04), Forests (OP/BP 4.36), and Physical Cultural Resources (OP/BP 4.11) policies are triggered, necessitating specific measures for managing hazardous materials like PCBs and wood preservatives, developing waste management plans for electrical meters, ensuring screening criteria for investments affecting natural habitats and forests, and implementing "chance finds" procedures for cultural resources. The project is not expected to involve pesticide use nor cover areas of specific indigenous groups such as the Kazakhs or Tsaatan peoples who might be disproportionately disadvantaged, as most of Mongolia's ethnic groups share similar customs and are not considered marginalized. The proactive identification and planning for these environmental and social aspects, coupled with the integrated capacity building and policy linkages, suggest a comprehensive approach to mitigating risks and fostering project success.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The activity targets improved reliability and access to electricity services, and enhanced revenue collection in BSEDN and EBEDN. It also aims to reduce system losses, strengthen power infrastructure, reduce transmission losses, improve National Power Transmission Grid efficiency, and build capacity in the Project Management Office and implementing units. ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The National Hydrology Project (P152698) in India, approved around November 23, 2016, is structured as an Investment Project Financing initiative with a total cost of US$350.00 million. This cost is equally financed by the Borrower (Government of India) and the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development, each contributing US$175.00 million, resulting in no financing gap. Expected disbursements from the Bank span from 2018 to 2025, totaling US$175.00 million. The project's lead practice area is Water, complemented by contributions from Agriculture, Climate Change, and Governance, and it holds an Environmental Category B - Partial Assessment.

The project's development objective is to improve the extent, quality, and accessibility of targeted water resources information and to strengthen the capacity of targeted water resources management institutions in India. This endeavor builds upon the foundation laid by previous phases (HP-I and HP-II), which established hydrology information platforms and institutional capacity primarily in peninsular states. The current project expands this effort nationwide to include the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra basins, addressing existing weaknesses in institutional capacity for Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM), flood, and drought management, and a shortage of skilled hydrologists. It aims to close disparities between states involved in prior phases and new states, focusing on upgrading networks in the former and establishing basic infrastructure in the latter, all while contributing to the GOI Digital India Initiative by integrating water resources information across agencies.

The project implements a strategy based on previous experiences, focusing on modernizing monitoring and enhancing analytical tools, through four main components. The **Water Resources Monitoring Systems** component, costing US$150.00 million, will improve the timeliness and reliability of water resources data by establishing or upgrading hydromet data systems (meteorology, streamflow, groundwater, water storage measurements), constructing data centers, and deploying hydromet observation networks and Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems across all states/UTs. The **Water Resources Information Systems** component, allocated US$50.00 million, aims to strengthen national and subnational water information centers with web-enabled WRISs through database standardization and the integration of comprehensive, timely water resources information from real-time acquisition networks, remote sensing, and topographical maps for decision-makers.

The **Water Resources Operations and Planning Systems** component, costing US$66.00 million, focuses on developing interactive analytical tools and decision-support platforms to integrate various data, models, and scenario management for improved hydrological flood forecasting, reservoir operations, and water resources accounting for both surface and groundwater. This includes creating a web-based, cloud-computing river basin management platform with water accounting software for standardized planning, assessments, and optimized reservoir operations for hydropower, irrigation, municipal, and industrial water supply, along with strengthening streamflow forecasting and alert systems for disaster management. Finally, the **Institutional Capacity Enhancement** component, with a budget of US$83.56 million, is designed to strengthen the human and organizational capabilities for effective water resources management, although specific activities are not detailed in the provided pages. A Front End fee of US$0.44 million is also included.

Overall activity success is anticipated through the generation of knowledge, increased open access to information, and strengthened institutional capacity, leading to a shift towards IWRM at the basin scale. These advancements are expected to result in improved water allocation and use efficiency, better management of drought and flood risks, and substantial socioeconomic benefits for India. The project's comprehensive national coverage, its foundation on previous project successes, and its emphasis on modern technology, data standardization, and inter-agency integration are key factors contributing to its potential for positive impact.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The activity targets improving water resources information extent, quality, and accessibility, and strengthening management institutions' capacity in India. Outcomes include modernizing monitoring, establishing hydromet data systems, web-enabled WRISs, analytical tools for forecasting/operations, and enhanced human capacity. Specific aims cover better irrigation design/operation, water quality, and groundwater management. Success requires: 12,000 online monitoring stations, 50 information products, 25 institutions reaching benchmark levels, 50% WRIS user satisfaction, 30 functional data centers, 900k WRIS page views, 30 regular water availability reports, 325 improved streamflow forecasting stations, and 2,000 trained professionals, leading to a shift to IWRM, improved water allocation, and better drought/flood risk management with socioeconomic benefits.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: India's external context for the National Hydrology Project features high water demand, overexploited groundwater, and pervasive quality issues. Seasonal rainfall leads to widespread droughts and floods, exacerbated by inadequate forecasting. Success probability is significantly boosted by strong GOI commitment aligned with national strategic priorities and data dissemination policies, plus World Bank support. The project builds on proven successes of prior phases (HP-I/II) in establishing hydrological information systems and aims for synergies with programs like NGMIP. Key risks include India's complex federal water management, classified transboundary data, and a shortage of hydrologists.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: High technical and logistical complexity. Expands sophisticated hydrological information systems nationwide. Integrates sparse, manual, and unstandardized data across major basins. Establishes real-time monitoring (SCADA), advanced WRIS, decision-support platforms for forecasting/accounting, and institutional capacity building/inter-agency integration across many states.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This is a large-scale, comprehensive national program (US$350M total cost) with national coverage. It is not integrated into a larger program, but rather builds upon and significantly expands previous national-level hydrology projects (HP-I and HP-II), continuing and expanding a nationwide effort to improve water resource management in India.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The National Hydrology Project is budgeted at US$350.00 million, equally financed by the Government of India and IBRD (each US$175.00 million), with no financing gap. Sub-activities and budgets: 1. Water Resources Monitoring Systems: US$150.00M (hydromet networks, SCADA, hydro-informatics centers). 2. Water Resources Information Systems: US$50.00M (web-enabled WRIS, data integration). 3. Water Resources Operations and Planning Systems: US$66.00M (analytical tools, decision-support platforms, river basin management). 4. Institutional Capacity Enhancement: US$83.56M (knowledge centers, professional development, project management, operational support). 5. Front End fee: US$0.44M. No financing gap indicates full funding, making under-funding unlikely. Comprehensive financing supports successful nationwide implementation, data standardization, and institutional strengthening."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The Department of Water Resources, RD & GR, Ministry of Jal Shakti leads implementation, supported by 49 IAs across central, state, and basin levels, guided by an NPMU and NLSC. The Government of India shows strong investment, co-financing 50% (US$175M) and aligning with the 'Digital India Initiative.' Abilities are built on prior successful phases (HP-I & II), but acknowledged capacity gaps in IWRM and skilled hydrologists are addressed by a US$83.56M component. The IBRD provides crucial funding/oversight. Stakeholders like central technical agencies aid implementation. Institutional compartmentalization in 10 states is a potential challenge.
ACTIVITY RISKS: Risks include: sparse, unvalidated, non-standardized data in northern river basins (Ganga, Brahmaputra); outdated national water resources assessment due to unsystematic data acquisition, especially for demand-side water use; classified data in transboundary river basins requiring updated sharing policies; weak institutional capacity for IWRM and a shortage of skilled hydrologists; weak procurement and technical capacity in one-third of implementing agencies; slow fund transfers and delayed budget availability; and unassessed e-procurement systems for some agencies.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The project features flexible management, robust central and state support with dedicated funding for capacity building, and adaptive strategies including performance-based fund reallocation. It mitigates fiduciary risks through centralized procurement support, clear fund flow mechanisms, and ongoing training. Positive indicators include real-time information dissemination, strong beneficiary engagement, gender mainstreaming, and proactive environmental assessments that mainstream water quality monitoring and integrate environmental principles into planning, ensuring no project-affected people and extensive stakeholder consultation."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "The Fortaleza Sustainable Urban Development Project operates within a complex and challenging external context, which presents both significant risks and opportunities for its overall success. The city's rapid growth has led to deforestation, pollution, urban sprawl, and the invasion of environmentally protected areas, with only 46% of households connected to the sewage network, causing widespread pollution.
