ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: PK 100.00% | Location: Balochistan

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 1484

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The PK-Balochistan Integrated Water Resources Management & Development Project (P154255) aims to strengthen provincial government capacity for water resources monitoring and management, and to increase the adoption of water-efficient practices and technologies by water users in targeted communities within the Nari and Porali basins of Balochistan. The project seeks to transform water management in Balochistan from a narrow irrigation focus to an integrated multi-sectoral river basin planning and development approach. This will be achieved through institutional restructuring and strengthening, investments in hydro-meteorological data and water information systems, and priority infrastructure investments across irrigation, potable water supply, flood protection, watershed, and rangeland management. The project is designed to improve water resource management by promoting equitable water distribution for economic development, environmental sustainability, and improved livelihoods, guided by stakeholder participation and climate change scenario assessments. It will particularly focus on improving water use efficiency in agriculture, the largest and most inefficient water-use sector, which is critical in a water-scarce province facing future reductions in water availability due to climate change. The project will also develop professional and institutional capacity for Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) and support hydromet data collection, management, and use. A core aspect involves quick-impact sub-projects, implemented with active beneficiary participation, to address deficiencies in irrigation infrastructure, on-farm water management, water supply, flood damage, and watershed degradation. The estimated appraisal date for the project was February 22, 2016, with an estimated board date of June 2, 2016. It is an Investment Project Financing instrument. The environmental category is 'A - Full Assessment', and it is not an emergency recovery or repeater project. The project is located in Pakistan. \n\nAll project sub-components will be implemented in the Nari Basin (69,224 km2) and the Porali Basin (11,616 km2). For the Pishin Lora Basin (16,671 km2), only sub-components A3 (Installation and Operation of Hydro-meteorological System) and B4 (Watershed & Rangeland Management and Environmental Protection) will be implemented. Key activities include promoting water savings technologies in irrigated agriculture such as high-efficiency irrigation systems and land leveling to improve soil and water availability, as well as improved agricultural practices and drought-tolerant cropping varieties to enhance productivity and soil and water conservation. The project will also facilitate better farmer decision-making on crop selection based on soil analysis, water availability, and weather advisories; improve watershed and rangeland management technologies for soil moisture retention, erosion reduction, and groundwater recharge; and promote the conservation of two vital ecosystems in Balochistan: the Juniper forest in the Nari river basin's catchment areas and the Mangroves forest in the Porali river delta. These interventions aim not only for environmental sustainability but also to build climate change resilience against floods and droughts. The managing unit for the project is GWA06.\n\nComponent A, 'IWRM Institutions, Capacity and Information,' has a total base cost of US$27.54 million, fully funded by IDA. This component will establish the foundation for a gradual transition to IWRM in Balochistan through institutional restructuring, professional development, installation and operation of hydro-meteorological systems, and the establishment of river basin information and decision support systems. It will set up a hydro-meteorological observation network across the three project river basins, including telecommunication equipment, software for data transmission and analysis, data storage conversion, and training for network operation and maintenance. A flood early warning system will be installed and operated based on real-time data. Appropriate institutional arrangements for IWRM will be determined and progressively implemented, with significant investment in professional development. A Water Resources Center of Excellence will also be established, likely hosted by a local university or existing technical institution.\n\nComponent B, 'IWRM Sub-projects,' has a total cost of US$213.20 million, with IDA providing US$203.09 million and beneficiaries contributing US$10.11 million. This component supports the implementation of IWRM sectoral investments in the Nari and Porali basins.\n\nThe total project cost is US$253.74 million, with total bank financing amounting to US$205.56 million and a financing gap of US$0.00. The financing sources include US$10.11 million from the Borrower/Recipient, US$205.56 million from the International Development Association (IDA), and US$38.07 million from the International Fund for Agriculture Development, totaling US$253.74 million. An earlier financing summary from the Integrated Safeguards Data Sheet indicated a total project cost of US$209.70 million, with US$200.00 million from IDA and US$9.70 million from the Borrower/Recipient.\n\nMultiple safeguard policies are triggered by this project. These include Environmental Assessment OP/BP 4.01, Natural Habitats OP/BP 4.04, Forests OP/BP 4.36, Pest Management OP 4.09, Physical Cultural Resources OP/BP 4.11, Indigenous Peoples OP/BP 4.10, Involuntary Resettlement OP/BP 4.12, Safety of Dams OP/BP 4.37, Projects on International Waterways OP/BP 7.50, and Projects in Disputed Areas OP/BP 7.60. The project involves various sectors: irrigation and drainage (60%), forestry (10%), public administration - water, sanitation and flood protection (10%), water supply (10%), and flood protection (10%). Key themes include natural disaster management (10%), rural services and infrastructure (40%), other rural development (10%), climate change (10%), and water resource management (30%). The World Bank contact for this project is Mahwash Wasiq, Sr Water Resources Management Specialist, reachable at Tel: 473-3465."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "Targeted outcomes include: strengthening provincial government capacity for water resources monitoring & management; increasing water-efficient practice adoption by users in Nari & Porali basins; transforming water management to an integrated multi-sectoral river basin approach for equitable distribution, environmental sustainability & improved livelihoods, focusing on agricultural efficiency. Also, developing IWRM capacity, improving hydromet data, addressing infrastructure, promoting ecosystem conservation & climate resilience. Success entails: ~705,000 direct beneficiaries (50% female); developed & maintained river basin information systems; ~38,000 water users adopting new irrigation/drainage services; ~16,000 farm households adopting improved agricultural technologies; ~82,000 people better protected from floods."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "Balochistan faces severe external challenges: it's Pakistan's least developed province (47% poverty), resource-rich but prone to conflict, experiencing economic stagnation, insecurity, and brain drain. It is Pakistan's least water-secure province, highly vulnerable to climate change (droughts, floods), with critical water scarcity, over-abstracted groundwater, and inefficient irrigation. Governance is weak, lacking national water policy and provincial political will, and institutional capacity for IWRM is limited. Positively, improved security and success of a prior project (BSSIP) have increased government interest in water reform, offering an opportunity for progress."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The project is highly complex due to its multi-sectoral approach to transform water management in Balochistan, involving institutional restructuring, advanced hydro-meteorological systems, and extensive infrastructure across irrigation, water supply, flood protection, and rangeland. It's an 'A - Full Assessment' environmental category, triggering numerous safeguard policies, which necessitates significant technical capacity and external support for implementation and mitigation in a water-stressed region with fragile ecosystems.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is a substantial, integrated program designed to transform water management in Balochistan from a narrow irrigation focus to an integrated river basin approach. It is structured as a comprehensive, multi-sectoral initiative with various components for institutional capacity, information systems, and sectoral sub-projects across specific basins, signifying a cohesive and self-contained larger program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "PK-Balochistan Project total cost: US$253.74M, US$0.00 financing gap. Earlier summary reported discrepancy: total US$209.70M.Component A, 'IWRM Institutions, Capacity and Information,' budgeted at US$27.54M (IDA-funded). Activities: institutional restructuring, professional development, hydro-meteorological system setup.Component B, 'IWRM Sub-projects,' budgeted at US$213.20M (IDA US$203.09M, beneficiaries US$10.11M). Activities: IWRM sectoral investments and quick-impact sub-projects for irrigation, water management, water supply, flood protection, watershed improvements. Beneficiary contributions are vital."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: "The Balochistan Irrigation Department, Government of Balochistan, is the implementing agency and demonstrates a renewed commitment to water sector reform. This is evidenced by the Government of Balochistan's financial contribution of US$10.11 million (as part of the Borrower/Recipient) and the adoption of an IWRM Policy in 2006. Crucially, the 'relative success of the recently completed Balochistan Small Scale Irrigation Project (BSSIP)' has 'significantly increased the government appetite for tackling water reform,' indicating growing political will and a track record of past success for similar initiatives, which provides a positive signal for their investment in the activity. The project explicitly aims to strengthen the provincial government's capacity and undertake institutional restructuring and professional development, directly addressing historical 'lack of technical and policy capacity within government to change from within'. Major external funding from the International Development Association (US$205.56 million) and the International Fund for Agriculture Development (US$38.07 million) also signifies strong external belief and support. Stakeholders aiding outcomes include the active participation of beneficiaries in quick-impact sub-projects, crucial for on-the-ground success and sustainability. There is also a 'changing mood and perspective at the national level' which could provide 'encouragement and stronger direction to the provincial process'. However, several factors could deteriorate activity outcomes. Historically, progress on IWRM policy was hindered by the 'highly political nature of water in the province and the lack of real political will'. While the appetite for reform has increased, these underlying political challenges could persist. Water Users' Associations (WUAs) are noted as generally ineffective outside canal irrigation areas, and significant effort is required to improve their functioning, including for cost-sharing and operation. The 'very limited involvement of women in WUAs' due to social and economic factors presents a significant challenge to equitable water management. The broader context of Balochistan as Pakistan's least developed province, characterized by 'security difficulties' leading to 'brain drain,' weak governance, and severe water scarcity exacerbated by climate change, also poses substantial inherent risks to the activity's performance."
ACTIVITY RISKS: "The project faces several risks to its overall success. These include: the possibility of pasture land conversion, decreased freshwater flows to mangrove forests, and possible introduction of newer crop varieties due to increased cultivated area and water diversions. Construction-related environmental impacts such as air quality deterioration, water and soil contamination, land use and land form changes, and impacts on biological resources are also risks. Negative environmental impacts from diverting floodwater, impacting ecological flows, biodiversity hotspots, protected areas, and livelihoods downstream are possible. Expansion of agricultural activity into traditionally non-agricultural lands (forest and rangelands) due to improved irrigation systems is a concern. The project requires land for civil works investments, potentially leading to land taking and possible relocation. There is a risk that major interventions to enhance agricultural productivity and newer crop varieties could affect local pest populations, leading to increased use of pesticides and other agro-chemicals. Impacts on local significant heritage places (religious buildings, graveyards, shrines) are also possible. The Balochistan Irrigation Department has limited capacity to implement safeguard requirements and will need considerable support. Broader contextual risks include security difficulties, high costs of delivering goods and services due to sparse population and lack of infrastructure, absence of coordinated economic policy, and the province being the least water-secure and most at risk from climate change. Existing spate irrigation is poorly managed and reliant on inadequate infrastructure, leading to inefficiency and unproductivity. Over-abstraction of groundwater has led to major declines in levels. Institutional challenges include the absence of a national water policy framework and limited functioning Water Users' Associations (WUAs), with very limited involvement of women in WUAs due to social and economic status and mobility restrictions."

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Project addresses risks via PSIA consultants (political), specialists & training (institutional/technical), and robust financial management (FM specialist, independent audits, transparency website). Strong fiduciary controls and public financial reporting are planned. Counterpart funds cover land, resettlement, and O&M (Year 4+), indicating ownership. High focus on citizen engagement: surveys, training, matching grants, farmer organizations, and open information. Comprehensive EIAs/EMPs with costs and prior ID safeguard experience contribute to success. Grievance redress mechanisms are in place."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P154275
ACTIVITY TITLE: Burunga Wastewater Management Project
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-10-30
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2020-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 81,200,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: PA 100.00% | Location: Provincia de Panama; Bahia de Panama; Panama; La Chorrera; Burunga; Arraijan

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 19102

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Burunga Wastewater Management Project (P154275), managed by The World Bank, was approved on March 17, 2017, with a closing date of December 31, 2021. Classified under Environmental Assessment Category B - Partial Assessment and utilizing an Investment Project Financing instrument, the project is not regionally tagged and does not involve Bank/IFC collaboration. The Borrower is the Republic of Panama. The project's development objectives are to (a) improve access to sewerage services in selected areas of the Borrower's province of Panama Oeste; and (b) strengthen wastewater pollution management capacity in key sector institutions of the Borrower.

The project is structured into three main components: Component 1, "Construction of a New Sewerage System in Burunga," is allocated US$49.50 million; Component 2, "Institutional Strengthening for Sustainable Wastewater Management," has a budget of US$12.70 million; and Component 3, "Project Management and Administration," accounts for US$19.00 million. This project is a crucial part of the broader Panama Sanitation Program (PSP), which is itself a second phase of investments under the Basic Sanitation Plan (Plan de Sanidad Basica 100/Cero). The PSP aims to achieve 100 percent, 24-hour-a-day access to potable water and eliminate dry-pit latrines, with total funding of US$577 million from various sources including IADB, CAF, EIB, and the China Fund for Latin America and the Caribbean. The specific Burunga project addresses the need for additional financing and technical assistance for sanitation investments in the Northwest part of Burunga, totaling US$81.2 million from the World Bank and CAF, and aims to develop planning and monitoring tools to strengthen wastewater pollution management capacity.

The project contributes to higher-level objectives by reducing untreated wastewater discharge, thereby improving conditions for the environmental recovery of rivers in the intervention area and Panama Bay. It also seeks to reduce the incidence of gastrointestinal diseases caused by contaminated water, enhancing the population's quality of life and productivity. Expected benefits include improvements in health, reduced river contamination, decreased costs for septic tank cleaning, and increased safety for women/girls through improved intra-domiciliary sanitation facilities. Furthermore, the project is designed to contribute to climate change resilience through the construction of ancillary hydraulic works and the implementation of non-infrastructure measures, such as an Integrated Urban Water Management (IUWM) Plan. Preliminary analysis estimates climate co-benefits at 50 percent of the project amount.

This initiative is strongly aligned with the World Bank's Twin Goals, promoting shared prosperity by improving sanitation access for the bottom 40 percent of the population and enhancing natural resource sustainability by reducing domestic wastewater. It also supports the 2015 Country Partnership Framework (CPF), particularly its pillars of ensuring inclusion for marginalized groups through improved basic services and bolstering resilience and sustainability via integrated urban water management. The project directly contributes to the CPF's 2.5.1 indicator for additional people gaining access to reliable water supply or improved sanitation services in targeted lower-income urban and rural areas, disaggregated by gender. The project addresses significant institutional challenges within Panama's Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) sector, including IDAAN's limited operational and financial autonomy, high water consumption and non-revenue water rates, and insufficient technical capacity. Recognizing these shortcomings, the Government of Panama (GoP) has initiated a new water law and supported efforts, including previous World Bank projects like Metro Agua I, to improve IDAAN's efficiency. Due to IDAAN's initial capacity limitations, the GoP delegated implementation of the PBCSP, including this project, to the Ministry of Health (MINSA) through a Program Coordinating Unit (PCU), though ongoing modernization is building IDAAN's future capacity.

Project success will be monitored through several key indicators. The direct project beneficiaries target is 20,000, calculated by multiplying the number of new connections to the sewerage network by five. Among these, female beneficiaries are targeted at 49 percent and poor beneficiaries at 29 percent. An additional 4,600 people are targeted to be provided with access to "improved sanitation facilities," specifically in urban areas, representing an estimated 23 percent of project beneficiaries based on a social and environmental survey. The project also aims for the establishment of an Integrated Urban Water Management Plan. All these indicators have a baseline of 0.00, with progress reported via supervision reports (annually for beneficiaries and sanitation access) and progress reports (twice a year for the IUWM Plan), with the PCU responsible for data collection.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: ["The Burunga Wastewater Management Project targets two main outcomes: (a) improving access to sewerage services in selected areas of Panama Oeste, aiming for 20,000 direct beneficiaries (49% women, 29% poor) and providing 4,600 people with improved urban sanitation facilities by constructing 4,000 new household and inter-domiciliary sewer connections and closing down 80% of septic tanks. (b) strengthening wastewater pollution management capacity in key sector institutions by preparing and implementing an Integrated Urban Water Management (IUWM) Plan, publishing annual water quality monitoring reports for Panama Bay, establishing a real-time hydrological/water quality monitoring system, and training 40 staff on hydrodynamic modeling. Higher-level outcomes include reducing untreated wastewater discharge, improving river and Panama Bay environmental recovery, decreasing gastrointestinal diseases, enhancing quality of life, reducing septic tank costs, increasing safety for women/girls, and contributing to climate change resilience and natural resource sustainability for the bottom 40% of the population.", "Overall success would be achieved when the project meets its development objectives and specific targets: (1) At least 20,000 direct project beneficiaries (49% women, 29% poor) and 4,600 people in urban areas gain access to improved sanitation facilities. This includes constructing 4,000 new household and inter-domiciliary sewer connections and properly closing 80% of septic tanks. (2) Wastewater pollution management capacity is strengthened by an Integrated Urban Water Management Plan being prepared and implemented, annual water quality monitoring reports for Panama Bay published, a real-time hydrological/water quality monitoring system established and operational, and 40 staff (20 female, 20 male) trained on hydrodynamic modeling. (3) Four socio-environmental community management committees are operational, and 95% of grievances related to project benefits are addressed."]

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: - Panama's strong economic growth, high-level government commitment via the 'Plan de Sanidad Basica 100/Cero' (a flagship project), and the project's alignment with broader national and World Bank strategic goals (Twin Goals, CPF, SCD) create a supportive external environment. Multi-donor financing from World Bank, CAF, OFID, IADB, EIB, and the China Fund for the wider program, combined with MINSA's PCU's proven track record, significantly enhance the probability of success. High demand due to rapid urban growth and poor WSS in Burunga further justifies the project.
- Despite strong government commitment, significant systemic institutional weaknesses within Panama's Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) sector pose challenges. IDAAN's limited operational and financial autonomy, high non-revenue water, and insufficient technical capacity, alongside unclear institutional responsibilities, are key risks to long-term sustainability. While the GoP is actively addressing these through new laws and modernization, potential land acquisition issues for the WWTP site (privately owned, owners unidentified) could impact project execution.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity involves significant technical and institutional complexities. Technically, it requires the construction of a new sewerage system, ancillary hydraulic works for climate resilience, and the development and implementation of an Integrated Urban Water Management (IUWM) Plan. Institutionally, it addresses challenges within Panama's Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) sector, including IDAAN's limited operational and financial autonomy, high water consumption, non-revenue water rates, and insufficient technical capacity, necessitating institutional strengthening.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is an integral and crucial part of the broader Panama Sanitation Program (PSP). The PSP is a larger program, representing a second phase of investments under the Basic Sanitation Plan (Plan de Sanidad Basica 100/Cero). The Burunga project is directly and cohesively integrated into this larger program, which aims to achieve 100 percent, 24-hour-a-day access to potable water and eliminate dry-pit latrines across Panama.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Burunga Wastewater Management Project has a total cost of US$81.2 million, fully financed with no gap, by the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) providing US$65 million (80%), the Development Bank of Latin America (CAF) providing US$11.5 million (14%), and the Government of Panama (GoP) contributing US$4.7 million (6%). The project's success is well-supported by these financing commitments. The project is structured into three main components with specific budgets and sub-activities:\n\nComponent 1: Construction of a New Sewerage System in Burunga (Budget: US$49.5 million; IBRD: US$47 million, GoP: US$2.5 million)\nThis component will finance the construction of a comprehensive sewerage system throughout the township of Burunga in the district of Arraijan. Specific sub-activities include:\n- Detailed construction designs and a sewerage network for wastewater: This is implemented through a US$47 million works contract. The network will serve approximately 20,000 people by 2020 and includes primary collectors, secondary sewers, and a tertiary network. This activity covers Sector 4.4 Northwest Burunga, with US$44.4 million allocated (US$42.2 million from IBRD, US$2.2 million from GoP).\n- Construction of long inter-domiciliary connections from households to the main sewerage network: This aims to ensure every household has a flush toilet connected to the system. The project specifically funds the connection of the system to existing bathrooms and facilities.\n- Closure and decommissioning of existing septic tanks or latrines.\n- Minor drainage works, road access improvements, and other ancillary hydraulic works to protect infrastructure from flash floods.\n- Contingencies for this component are budgeted at US$5.1 million (US$4.8 million IBRD, US$0.3 million GoP).\nIt is noted that the sewerage system will connect to a Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) currently under tender and financed in parallel by CAF outside of this project. The WWTP construction contract includes O&M for the plant and network for ten years, indicating a reliance on external funding for a critical part of the overall wastewater management system. CONADES will support the construction of bathroom facilities for an estimated 800 poor households lacking them, including internal plumbing and appliances, which is a crucial complementary activity not directly funded by the project's components.\n\nComponent 2: Institutional Strengthening for Sustainable Wastewater Management (Budget: US$12.7 million; IBRD: US$12 million, GoP: US$0.7 million)\nThis component aims to enhance the technical capacity of the PCU in implementing the PSP and managing water pollution in Panama Bay. It has three sub-components:\n- Sub-component 2A: Technical capacity building for the PCU and provision of management tools (Budget: US$7.5 million; IBRD: US$7 million, GoP: US$0.5 million). Activities include: (i) carrying out a water quality baseline and environmental/economic impact assessment; (ii) a technical audit of Panama City's sewer and drainage system; (iii) design and implementation of a real-time hydrological/water quality monitoring system; (iv) design and implementation of a hydrodynamic model for pollutant transport; (v) preparation of a map classifying sensitive coastal and river areas; (vi) preparation of an action plan for critical sewerage areas. It will also finance activities to develop an Integrated Urban Water Management (IUWM) Plan for an urban river basin in Panama Oeste, including diagnostics, stakeholder identification, participatory interventions, and implementation of selected pilot activities.\n- Sub-component 2B: Strengthening PCU's and IDAAN's technical capacity to supervise O&M and management of wastewater infrastructure (Budget: US$2.6 million; IBRD: US$2.5 million, GoP: US$0.1 million). This involves technical training, short courses, workshops, technical assistance, and study tours.\n- Sub-component 2C: Strengthening PSP's social interventions (Budget: US$2.6 million; IBRD: US$2.5 million, GoP: US$0.1 million). This aims to raise awareness and encourage commitment among community-based organizations in Burunga through: (i) creating/strengthening socio-environmental community management committees; (ii) developing monitoring exercises for sanitary conditions; and (iii) other community initiatives like sweeping campaigns and cultural/sport events.\n\nComponent 3: Project Management and Administration (Budget: US$19.0 million; IBRD: US$6 million, CAF: US$11.5 million, GoP: US$1.5 million)\nThis component covers the operational aspects of the project, including:\n- Costs associated with supervision, quality control, monitoring, inspection, and procurement support for Component 1 works.\n- PCU's Project management and administration activities, including fiduciary management (financial audits), procurement, reporting, monitoring and evaluation (M&E), and implementation of safeguard instruments. The Project Manager for Burunga (all sectors) is specifically financed by CAF (US$11.5 million) and GoP (US$0.8 million), totaling US$12.3 million."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The activity will be implemented by the Project Coordinating Unit (PCU) under the Ministry of Health (MINSA), a decision made due to the initial capacity limitations of IDAAN, the primary water utility. The PCU demonstrates strong capabilities, managing a contract portfolio exceeding US$1.5 billion for large-scale projects. It is well-staffed with qualified multidisciplinary personnel, and an international project management consulting firm was recruited to support technical designs and documentation, though fiduciary responsibilities remain with the PCU. The PCU is also responsible for project monitoring and evaluation. The Republic of Panama (GoP) is highly invested, serving as the borrower, contributing an estimated US$20 million in counterpart funding (including US$4.70 million directly from borrower funds), and has designated this project as a crucial part of the broader national Panama Sanitation Program, a significant government priority. The GoP has also initiated a new water law and supported previous World Bank projects to improve the Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) sector. Key stakeholders aiding the activity include the World Bank, providing US$65 million in funding, international expertise, knowledge exchange, and capacity development; the Development Bank of Latin America (CAF) and the OPEC Fund for International Development (OFID), which are substantial co-financiers; and a Design, Build, and Operate (DBO) contractor responsible for constructing, operating for seven years, and maintaining the wastewater treatment plant and sewerage system, while also training IDAAN and PCBSP staff. Although IDAAN currently faces institutional challenges such as limited autonomy and technical capacity, the project includes specific components to strengthen its long-term operational and management capabilities, and it also aims to strengthen ANAM's monitoring capacity.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- Risk of the Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) not being built and operational by Project completion due to construction delays or failed bidding/adjudication processes.
- Lack of information about the design of the sewerage system's minor civil works, potentially affecting competition, leading to higher priced bids, or causing problems during contract implementation.
- Delays in WWTP commissioning due to the need for coordination between contracts for the sewerage system and the WWTP financed by CAF.
- Potential intervention of the Comptroller General's Office (CGR) prior to contract signing, which could cause bottlenecks.
- Untimely availability of counterpart funding that could delay Project implementation.
- Local procurement regulations that may include practices not acceptable to the World Bank.
- Sewerage works being completed before the WWTP is functional, leading to untreated sewage discharge into the Aguacate River or its tributaries.
- Negative environmental impacts during the construction phase and operation phase if not adequately managed by environmental management plans and appropriate technology.
- Uncertainty regarding the precise location of land needed for construction of pumping stations, culverts, and other minor infrastructure that may need to be acquired.
- Need to negotiate easement rights for collectors and the sewerage network, with insufficient information to identify exact locations.
- Requirement for land under public domain to be transferred from public agencies to MINSA.
- Uncertainty about whether resettlement of social units or economic activities will be necessary, triggering the Involuntary Resettlement Policy.
- Significant institutional challenges within Panama's Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) sector, including IDAAN's limited operational and financial autonomy, high water consumption, high non-revenue water rates, and insufficient technical capacity.
- IDAAN's initial lack of capacity to oversee infrastructure investments and subsequent operation and maintenance (O&M) of works.
- Unclear institutional responsibilities, a lack of sector policy leadership, and weak regulatory agencies in the WSS sector.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Strong risk mitigation plans for sustainability (10-year DBO, conditional IDAAN handover), technical design (WWTP action plan, launched bidding, land secured), fiduciary (corrective measures, agreements with CGR/MEF), and environmental/social (approved RPF/EIA, EMPs). Institutional strengthening budget, PCU support staff, and a social management strategy for inclusive participation. Clear targets for beneficiaries (20,000), improved sanitation (4,600), and specific intermediate results indicators provide measurable success. Strong alignment with World Bank goals."

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "VIETNAM: IMPROVED LAND GOVERNANCE AND RESOURCES INFORMATION MANGEMENT PROJECT (VILG)" (P154387) is an Investment Project Financing instrument in Vietnam, led by Dzung The Nguyen, with an estimated appraisal date of January 4, 2016, and an estimated board date of March 24, 2016, managed by GSULN. The project has a total cost of $180.00 million, fully financed with no gap, including $150.00 million from the International Development Association (IDA) and $30.00 million from the Borrower/Recipient. Its sector focus is General information and communications (50%), General public administration (30%), and General agriculture, fishing and forestry (20%), with themes centered on Land administration and management (40%), e-Government (40%), and Administrative and civil service reform (20%). The Project Development Objective (PDO) is to improve the efficiency and transparency of land administration services in the Project Provinces, to meet the needs of government, business, and citizens, achieved through the development and implementation of (i) an improved policy and institutional environment for the land sector, (ii) a multi-purpose national Land Information System (LIS) to support key land management functions (registration, land use planning, land valuation, and taxation), and (iii) a national system to monitor the implementation and effectiveness of the 2013 Land Law. Key PDO level results indicators include the number of entities accessing the Multi-Purpose Land Information System (MPLIS), average time to register a simple land transfer, customer satisfaction with land services, and the percentage of Land Use Rights Certificates (LURCs) with women, single, or co-land users.

The project beneficiaries encompass land users and the general public, including disadvantaged and vulnerable groups, government institutions at central and sub-national levels, and the private sector, all of whom will gain improved access to integrated digital data, more efficient services, and increased access to land information. The project is structured into three main components: Component 1, "Strengthening the Quality of Land Service Delivery" ($6.84 million), aims to improve the land regulatory and institutional environment to ensure consistent implementation of the Land Law 2013 at national and sub-national levels, by improving land services delivery through establishing unified regulatory frameworks and infrastructure for Land Registration Offices (LROs), enhancing public participation, and monitoring the Land Law's implementation. This includes developing nationally consistent LROs procedures, guidelines, standards, and strengthening LROs, as well as raising public awareness and engagement among all stakeholders, including vulnerable groups, to foster confidence and adoption of the land administration system. Component 2, "Establishment and Development of the Multi-Purpose Land Information System and Land Database" ($160.39 million), which accounts for the largest share of funding, focuses on developing a national MPLIS based on uniform system architecture, infrastructure, and software, and establishing and operating a national land database (comprising cadastral, land-use plan, land price, land statistics, and inventories) to make land information available to government institutions, the business community, notaries, land users, and the general public. This component also supports strengthening land-use management and establishing a system to monitor the uniform implementation of Land Law 2013. Component 3, "Project Management" ($12.77 million), covers overall project administration.

The project's geographical scope includes Project Provinces, building on the successful implementation of the Vietnam Land Administration Project (VLAP, 2008-2015), and aims to scale up and strengthen progress in a larger number of provinces, which will be selected based on their development potential, commitment, implementation capacity, representativeness, and readiness to provide a model for future national mainstreaming. For forecasting overall activity success, the project's clear, comprehensive objectives, substantial IDA financing, and strategy of building on previous successes are positive indicators. However, the project's overall risk is rated "Substantial," with specific "Substantial" risks identified for Sector Strategies and Policies, Technical Design of Project or Program, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Fiduciary aspects, suggesting potential challenges in policy alignment, complex technical implementation, institutional readiness, and financial management. Macroeconomic, Political and Governance, Environment and Social, and Stakeholder risks are rated "Moderate." The project is categorized as Environmental B - Partial Assessment, and does not depart from the Country Assistance Strategy (CAS) or require any waivers of Bank policies.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The activity targets improving land administration service efficiency and transparency in selected provinces through a better policy/institutional environment, a multi-purpose National Land Information System (MPLIS), and monitoring the 2013 Land Law. Success requires: 1. Increased entities accessing MPLIS (target TBD, from 0 baseline). 2. Average simple land transfer registration time reduced from 35 to 10 days. 3. Enhanced customer satisfaction with land services. 4. Higher percentage of LURCs with women, single, or co-land users.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The project benefits from a highly supportive external context, directly aligning with the Government of Vietnam's (GoV) Socio-Economic Development Plan (SEDP) 2016-20 and the Vietnam-World Bank Group Country Partnership Strategy (CPS) 2012-16. Both strategies prioritize effective and sustainable land management and improved governance, recognizing land reform as a centerpiece of the GoV's "Doi Moi" reform process and a critical driver of rapid growth and poverty reduction. The promulgation of the 2013 Land Law provides a comprehensive legal framework. Furthermore, improving land administration is seen as essential for addressing key macroeconomic challenges, reducing transaction costs, fostering efficient land markets, and enabling investments. The project also builds on the successful implementation of the Vietnam Land Administration Project (VLAP, 2008-2015), indicating prior government commitment and experience in this sector. However, the broader external context includes challenges such as lagging governance and accountability systems, which have made it difficult for Vietnam to address issues like competitiveness, sustainability, persistent poverty (particularly among ethnic minorities), rising inequality, and macroeconomic vulnerabilities. These broader governance weaknesses could pose challenges to the project's long-term success and sustainability, despite the strong sector-specific alignment.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity, Vietnam: Project for Improved Land Governance and Databases (VILG), is highly complex. It involves developing and implementing a national Multi-Purpose Land Information System (MPLIS) based on a uniform system architecture and infrastructure, establishing a national land database through digitizing existing records, and integrating various land data (cadastral, land-use plan, land price, land statistics, and inventories). This requires significant software development, IT expertise, and data management. Furthermore, the project aims to improve the policy and institutional environment for the land sector, necessitating broad institutional and policy reforms at national and sub-national levels, including strengthening Land Registration Offices (LROs) and standardizing procedures. The project's overall risk is rated "Substantial," with specific "Substantial" risks identified for Sector Strategies and Policies, Technical Design of Project or Program, and Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is not an independent program but is directly and cohesively integrated into a larger, ongoing program of land administration reform in Vietnam. It builds on the successful implementation of the previous Vietnam Land Administration Project (VLAP, 2008-2015), aiming to scale up and strengthen progress in a larger number of provinces. The project's objective includes developing a multi-purpose national Land Information System (LIS) and a national system to monitor the implementation and effectiveness of the 2013 Land Law, which will be mainstreamed into national government processes. The Multi-Purpose Land Information System (MPLIS) will serve as a platform for the National Land Database, indicating a broad, national-level integration.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The VILG project has a total budget of $180.00 million, fully financed with no gap ($150M IDA, $30M Borrower). Component 1 (Strengthening Land Service Delivery) is $6.84M, including $5.01M for LRO modernization. Component 2 (MPLIS & Land Database) is $160.39M, and Component 3 (Project Management) is $12.77M. While fully funded, the project's 'Substantial' risk rating for technical design, institutional capacity, and fiduciary aspects, alongside weak sub-national capacity and private sector limitations, suggests potential challenges in efficient fund utilization and implementation success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The implementing organizations, MoNRE's GDLA and sub-national DONREs/LROs, leverage experience from the previous VLAP project, indicating a foundation of capacity. However, the project identifies a "Substantial" risks for Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, citing "fundamental bottlenecks" and inconsistent implementation capacity across provinces, suggesting ongoing challenges. The Socialist Republic of Vietnam demonstrates "skin-in-the-game" through a significant $30 million financial contribution and commitment to new procurement frameworks. Other stakeholders include land users, businesses, and ethnic minority groups, whose engagement is actively planned to foster adoption and inclusion. Cooperation from key ministries (Justice, Finance, Construction) is crucial for ensuring the sustainability of outcomes.
ACTIVITY RISKS: Overall project risk is rated Substantial. Key risks include: Sector Strategies and Policies (Substantial), due to inconsistency in implementation capacity across provinces, gaps between policy framework and implementation, and lack of effective land policy and planning coordination. Technical Design of Project or Program (Substantial), owing to new approaches for land database management and lack of clarity on MPLIS software requirements. Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability (Substantial), reflecting weak sub-national capacity, limited private sector capacity for technical work, and MoNRE/GDLA's limited experience with software development. Fiduciary (Substantial), due to potential delays in procurement and implementation of IT development and land registration works.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The project has clear, comprehensive objectives, substantial IDA financing, and builds on the successful Vietnam Land Administration Project (VLAP). Mitigation measures are incorporated for risks like technical design and institutional capacity, leveraging VLAP's experience, detailed planning (PPSD), and training. It's expected to yield significant financial benefits, including increased revenues and a positive FIRR (21%). The design ensures strong civic engagement, addresses gender equity and ethnic minority inclusion with specific plans (EMPF, EMDPs), and aims for positive environmental and social impacts."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P154389
ACTIVITY TITLE: Myanmar Financial Sector Development Project
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2015-07-14
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-12-31

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 200,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: MM 100.00% | Location: Yangon Region; Union of Burma; Nay Pyi Taw

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 1359

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The activity is titled the Myanmar Financial Inclusion Project, identified by Project ID P154389, and is not a repeater project. The Integrated Safeguards Data Sheet (ISDS) for this concept stage activity was prepared and updated on April 20, 2015, and approved/disclosed on April 27, 2015, under Report No. ISDSC12454. The estimated appraisal date is July 28, 2015, and the estimated board date is September 21, 2015. The activity is managed by the GFMDR unit, with Jose De Luna-Martinez and Alexandra L. Drees-Gross serving as Task Team Leaders. It will be implemented using an Investment Project Financing lending instrument, with sectoral focuses on Microfinance (40%), Banking (40%), and the General finance sector (20%), and a thematic focus entirely on Other Financial Sector Development (100%).

The total estimated project cost is USD 200.00 million, fully financed by the International Development Association (IDA), leaving no financing gap. The environmental category assigned to the project is B - Partial Assessment. The primary development objective of this IDA operation is to help increase access to financial services for the poor in Myanmar. This objective is set within the context of financial inclusion being a key priority for Myanmar, aligning with the government's recently formulated Myanmar Financial Inclusion Roadmap (2015-2020), which targets increasing the percentage of adults with access to basic financial services from 30 percent to 40 percent by 2020. To achieve this, the IDA operation will finance selected government actions, instruments, and activities, beginning with the modernization of financial infrastructure. The clear alignment with national development priorities, a specific roadmap target, identified task team leaders, and a fully funded financial structure with no apparent financing gap could be useful indicators for forecasting the activity's potential success.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: ["The activity targets expanding financial access for the poor in Myanmar, aiming to boost adults with basic financial services from 30% to 40% by 2020, aligning with the national roadmap. Key outcomes include modernizing financial infrastructure, improving banking sector competitiveness via state-owned bank restructuring (reducing fiscal risks, enhancing service delivery), corporatizing MEB and MADB, expanding financial products for the poor (e.g., electronic civil servant salaries, MFI funding, digital finance), and strengthening the legal/regulatory framework, with a crisis response objective.", "Overall success requires achieving specific targets by 2020: increasing active transaction accounts to 32% (males) and 27% (females); raising adults in the poorest 40% with financial accounts to 20%; and boosting commercial bank loans (% GDP) to 15%. Success also hinges on fulfilling key DLIs: CBM passing mobile finance regulation, MADB ownership transfer to MOF, and Presidential signing of the Financial Inclusion Roadmap/Sector Development Strategy. Effective modernization of payment systems, state-owned bank restructuring, and expanded poor-focused financial products are also critical."]

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: - Myanmar's financial sector is profoundly underdeveloped, characterized by extremely low penetration (e.g., credit to GDP at ~12%, only 23% of adults with financial accounts), a banking system dominated by state-owned entities with limited competition, and supervisory bodies lacking adequate capacity. The country also experienced macroeconomic instability from 2015-2016, with growth easing, inflation rising to 11.7%, and the currency depreciating 30%, which introduces significant external risks to the project's success.
- The project benefits from a supportive external environment driven by major political and economic reforms since 2011, signaling increased openness and government commitment to financial inclusion, as evidenced by the Myanmar Financial Inclusion Roadmap (2015-2020). The enactment of the new Financial Institutions Law in January 2016 provides a crucial modern legal and regulatory framework for banking, addressing decades of outdated legislation. This foundational legal change is a strong enabler for the project's objectives, despite the multi-year implementation ahead.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is highly complex, involving comprehensive financial sector reforms, including modernizing financial infrastructure with advanced payment systems (RTGS, CSD, ACH, SSS, national payment switch), corporatizing and restructuring two large state-owned banks (including international audits, due diligence, partial privatization, and upgrades to corporate governance and IT), expanding financial products for the poor (such as switching civil servant salaries to electronic payments, scaling up MFI funding, and matching grants for digital finance), and strengthening the legal and regulatory framework. These reforms are being implemented in the context of Myanmar's specific institutional capacity and financial sector development stage, requiring substantial technical assistance and capacity building.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity, titled the Myanmar Financial Sector Development Project, is a larger, multi-component program. It is directly and cohesively integrated into Myanmar's national development priorities, specifically aligning with the government's recently formulated Myanmar Financial Inclusion Roadmap (2015-2020) and the Myanmar Financial Sector Development Strategy. The project aims to finance selected government actions, instruments, and activities to increase access to financial services for the poor, operating as a core part of the national financial inclusion agenda.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Myanmar Financial Sector Development Project is budgeted at US$100.0 million, fully financed by IDA with no gap. Sub-activities (US$M): Comp 1: Reform of State-owned Banks (37.0) incl. bank reform (30.0), consultancy (7.0). Comp 2: Upgrading Legal/Regulatory (37.0) for banking (20.0), microfinance (5.0), insurance (5.0), and related consultancy/IT (7.0). Comp 3: Modernizing CBM/Infrastructure (24.0) for CBM consultancy (2.0), training (6.0), payment systems (16.0). Comp 4: Project Coordination (2.0) for MOPF PCU (1.0), CBM PCU (1.0). Comp 5: Contingent Emergency Response (0.0). Zero budget for emergency response relies on reallocation; this is the only unfunded aspect upfront. Overall, robust financing and strategic alignment suggest high success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The primary implementing organizations for the Myanmar Financial Sector Development Project are the Ministry of Planning and Finance (MOPF) and the Central Bank of Myanmar (CBM). The Republic of the Union of Myanmar serves as the Borrower. Key actors from the implementing organizations demonstrate significant skin-in-the-game and investment: Maung Maung Win, the Deputy Minister of Planning and Finance, and U Kyaw Kyaw Maung, the Governor of the CBM, are listed as direct contacts, indicating high-level political will and engagement. The activity is strongly aligned with national development priorities, specifically the government's Myanmar Financial Inclusion Roadmap (2015-2020), which targets increasing access to financial services for the poor, further signaling governmental commitment. While the project is fully financed by an IDA Credit of US$100 million with no financing gap, the Government of Myanmar (GOM) also demonstrates investment by utilizing its own budget lines for operational expenditures and staff salaries of the MOPF through Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs). A substantial portion of the project's budget is allocated to internal capacity building within both MOPF and CBM, including modernizing the CBM and its financial infrastructure, establishing a training center, and IT investments, which reflects a long-term commitment to institutional strengthening.Dedicated Project Coordination Units (PCUs) will be established within both MOPF and CBM, staffed with experts in procurement, M&E, and financial management, with resources provided by the project to ensure effective management and coordination. MOPF will also on-lend US$25 million to CBM, indicating an internal financial and administrative responsibility.Regarding other stakeholders, the International Development Association (IDA) is actively aiding the activity by providing full financing, dedicated task team leaders, and technical support including training for PCU staff and defining responsibilities through project and subsidiary agreements. However, significant challenges are present: the project's overall procurement risk is assessed as High due to the absence of a centralized procurement legal framework and the MOPF and CBM's lack of comprehensive procurement procedures, expertise, and staff experienced with World Bank guidelines. Myanmar is also noted as a fragile country with low procurement implementation capacities. These internal capacity constraints could deteriorate activity outcomes without the robust mitigation measures planned, which include hiring specialist staff (procurement, IT, M&E) within the PCUs and providing training by the Bank, as well as establishing explicit coordination arrangements between the autonomous MOPF and CBM.
ACTIVITY RISKS: Relevant risks to the Myanmar Financial Sector Development Project not achieving overall success include: outbreaks of violence, primarily focused on the country's Muslim minorities; an underdeveloped financial sector, infrastructure gaps, and limited capacities to manage economic shocks; high Political and Governance risk; moderate Macroeconomic risk; moderate Technical Design of Project or Program risk; high Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability risk; high Fiduciary risk; and moderate Conflict and Violence risk.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The project has clear objectives aligned with Myanmar's national financial inclusion roadmap, targeting increased access for the poor. It will modernize financial infrastructure for efficiency and safety. Governance is ensured by annual work plans, DLI monitoring, a legal Subsidiary Agreement, and a comprehensive results framework. Risk mitigation includes broad stakeholder consultation, macroeconomic stability strategies, dedicated PCUs with trained staff, and streamlined World Bank procurement. The project fosters inter-agency coordination (MOPF/CBM), has no negative social effects, and includes a contingent emergency response mechanism."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P154482
ACTIVITY TITLE: Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-05-31
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2025-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Regional

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 117,991,714

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Location: Salamat Region; Ouaddai Region; Wadi Fira Region; Republic of Senegal; Kolda; Casamance; Province du Houet; Dourou; Burkina Faso; Wilaya du Trarza | Recipient countries: BF 16.67%; ML 16.67%; MR 16.67%; NE 16.67%; SN 16.67%; TD 16.67%

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project (SIIP), identified by Project ID P154482, aims to improve stakeholders' capacity to develop and manage irrigation and to increase irrigated areas using a regional 'solutions' approach in six participating countries across the Sahel. This initiative is categorized under the lead practice area of Water, with contributing practice areas in Agriculture, Environment & Natural Resources. The project addresses a significant challenge where areas suitable for irrigated agriculture are largely underdeveloped and underexploited, with only 37% of over 2 million hectares equipped for irrigation and 60% of that equipped area actually irrigated. Past efforts have seen slow growth and outcomes below expectations due to factors like insufficient local engagement, inadequate commercial viability, technical design flaws, poor construction quality, non-transparent land allocation, limited access to finance, unclear operation and maintenance responsibilities, and poor stakeholder coordination.

The SIIP, managed by GWA07 with a team led by Francois Onimus, Elisee Ouedraogo, and Pierrick Fraval, adopts a regional approach to scale up irrigation development through institutional strengthening and selected irrigation land development. This represents a paradigm shift based on balancing public interventions across different irrigation systems, adopting a market-oriented production system, and engaging stakeholders directly in planning and implementation. This strategy is expected to enhance impact on agricultural productivity and incomes, thereby reducing food insecurity and poverty. The regional approach is designed to facilitate coordinated investment planning, build knowledge and cross-learning, attract private sector participation, and promote regional policy adoption, enabling solutions at scale despite inherent complexities and political economy risks. The project benefits from strong political commitment from the six Sahel countries (Burkina Faso, Chad, Mali, Mauritania, Niger, and Senegal), leveraging the World Bank's convening capacity. It builds on experiences from the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program and the Regional Sahel Pastoralism Support Project, and aligns with the broader Sahel Irrigation Initiative (2iS) launched by the Dakar Declaration, which targets 1 million hectares irrigated in the Sahel by 2020.

The project is structured into three components: Component A focuses on Modernizing the institutional framework (US$ 19.60 Million), Component B on Financing irrigation investment solutions (US$ 141.20 Million), and Component C on Knowledge management and coordination (US$ 37.50 Million). According to one document, the total project cost is US$ 198.25 Million with US$ 170.00 Million in total Bank Financing, and a financing gap of US$ 0.00 Million. Financing sources include US$ 10.70 Million from BORROWER/RECIPIENT, US$ 79.17 Million from International Development Association (IDA), US$ 90.83 Million from IDA Grant, US$ 5.85 Million from Global Partnership on Output-based Aid, and US$ 10.70 Million from LOCAL BENEFICIARIES, totaling US$ 197.25 Million. An Integrated Safeguards Data Sheet presents slightly different figures, indicating a total project cost of US$ 192.46 Million with US$ 173.00 Million in total Bank Financing, and sources comprising US$ 6.64 Million from BORROWER/RECIPIENT, US$ 173.00 Million from IDA, US$ 5.85 Million from Global Partnership on Output-based Aid, and US$ 6.97 Million from LOCAL BENEFICIARIES, also with a US$ 0.00 Million financing gap. The lending instrument is Investment Project Financing, and the environmental category is A - Full Assessment.

Expected annual disbursements in US$ Million are: 0.00 (2017), 9.00 (2018), 15.00 (2019), 35.00 (2020), 40.00 (2021), 40.00 (2022), 25.00 (2023), and 11.85 (2024), reaching a cumulative disbursement of US$ 175.85 Million by 2024. Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) will be conducted at both regional (by CILSS, responsible for design, coordination, training, and data quality control) and national levels (by participating countries, with local stakeholders collecting data). Project interventions will be georeferenced using earth observation tools and smartphones, with information publicly accessible. A baseline survey will collect irrigation, social, and environmental data, and figures will be reviewed at the Mid Term Review (MTR). Sustainability is central to the project's strategic positioning, aiming to establish a solid framework for leveraging resources and improving sector performance through integrated solutions and institutionalization. This involves strengthening CILSS's coordinating role, building capacity in technical centers like AGRHYMET and INSAH for learning and knowledge management services, and embedding national Project Management Units (PMUs) within line ministries or specialized agencies. A financing mechanism will be devised before MTR for national projects to contribute to regional service costs, progressively taking over CILSS financing from IDA in the latter half of the project. This strategic approach, strong political backing, and a comprehensive M&E framework, combined with a focus on institutional strengthening and market-oriented solutions, are key factors for forecasting the overall success of the Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The targeted outcomes include improving stakeholders' capacity to develop and manage irrigation and increasing irrigated areas using a regional 'solutions' approach. This aims to enhance agricultural productivity and incomes, reduce food insecurity and poverty, create jobs, and foster transparent policy and planning. Specific targets include revitalizing/developing approximately 23,000 hectares and conducting feasibility studies for an additional 50,000 hectares, directly benefiting 80,000 households and 480,000 beneficiaries. Overall success depends on achieving these objectives through institutional strengthening, a comprehensive M&E framework, and market-oriented solutions.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The Sahel region faces significant external challenges that impact the probability of the project's success. It is exposed to a unique set of climatic and environmental risks, including high and sustained population growth exerting pressure on natural resources, high variability in precipitation, irregular and short rainy seasons, continuous erosion, deforestation, and unsustainable agricultural practices. These lead to widespread land degradation, low crop yields, regular food shortages, and reliance on food imports, exacerbated by climate change which is expected to amplify droughts and floods. Past irrigation efforts in the region have seen slow growth and outcomes below expectations due to systemic external factors such as insufficient local engagement, inadequate commercial viability, non-transparent land allocation, limited access to finance, unclear operation and maintenance responsibilities, and poor stakeholder coordination. Despite these challenges, there are favorable external conditions: agriculture remains a high-level priority and the backbone of the Sahelian economy, crucial for food security and job creation. The region possesses relatively abundant water resources and vast tracts of land suitable for irrigated agriculture along major rivers and in inland valley areas. Furthermore, the project benefits from strong political commitment from the six participating Sahel countries and aligns with the broader regional Sahel Irrigation Initiative (2iS) launched by the Dakar Declaration, which targets 1 million hectares irrigated in the Sahel by 2020. The World Bank's convening capacity also represents a positive external factor.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project is highly complex due to its regional scope across six countries, aiming to scale up irrigation development with institutional strengthening. It addresses significant historical challenges like technical design flaws, poor construction, and inadequate coordination. The regional approach itself increases project complexity and political economy risks, managing diverse irrigation system types (from small-scale to large-scale requiring complex management structures), and necessitating paradigm shifts in planning, design, and construction.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project (SIIP) is a large regional program coordinating irrigation development across six Sahel countries. It is deeply integrated into a broader strategic framework, building on experiences from prior regional programs (West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program and Regional Sahel Pastoralism Support Project) and aligning with the overarching 'Sahel Irrigation Initiative (2iS)' launched by the Dakar Declaration, which targets 1 million hectares irrigated.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project's total cost is US$198.25M. Sub-activities & budgets: Component A (Modernizing institutional framework): US$19.60M. Component B (Financing irrigation investment solutions): US$141.20M, comprising B.1 (Preparing investment proposals & mobilizing financing): US$9.4M IDA for studies on 50,000ha; and B.2 (Designing & implementing irrigation solutions): US$109.6M IDA for revitalization, modernization, new construction on 23,000ha, with infrastructure, training, and beneficiary contributions. Component C (Knowledge management & coordination): US$37.50M."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: CILSS (regional implementing agency) has a solid track record and a plan for sustained financing. National PMUs in line ministries and local beneficiaries also implement. Borrowers and local beneficiaries show skin-in-the-game through financial contributions and formal commitments to manage irrigation schemes. SOFITEX (private firm) has strong safeguards in place. Political commitment from six Sahel countries and an experienced World Bank team aid outcomes. The regional approach directly addresses past issues like poor local engagement and coordination.
ACTIVITY RISKS: The risk to development outcome is rated high due to: (i) the high political and governance risk as well as high security risks in project implementation areas; (ii) the high risk related to the institutional capacity for implementation given the complex nature and geographical extent of this project; and (iii) the cumulative impact of several other substantial risks. Political and governance risk is high, with several countries (Burkina Faso, Chad, Mali, Niger, and to a lesser extent Burkina Faso and Mauritania) currently facing direct terrorist threats and internal conflicts. These risks may lead to below expectations outcomes. Sector strategies and policies risk is rated substantial, as many countries already have irrigation strategies and policies in place, however, they are often not reflected in the reality in the field. Technical design risk is substantial, as the project will primarily develop micro- and small-scale schemes, and unintended impact will only be achieved if there is significant improvement in the quality of design and construction while bringing unit costs down. Institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability risk is high, given the project will only be effective if there is a high level of coordination among the various partners and willingness of the six countries to implement the policy changes derived from stakeholder consultations. Sustainability risk is further affected by the focus of the project on irrigation services with less attention given to agricultural intensification. Weaknesses in the agricultural extension services or input supply chains may render the project outputs irrelevant if farmers do not receive incremental income from their improved irrigation systems. Fiduciary risk is rated substantial, with overall fiduciary environment having substantial weakness in the integrity of the procurement and financial management systems among the CILSS and the six countries. This could result in delays in the acquisition of key project commodities and lead to disjointed implementation of key interventions.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "[\"The project adopts a regional solutions approach facilitating coordinated investment, knowledge sharing, and private sector participation. It benefits from strong political commitment from six Sahel countries and leverages the World Bank's convening capacity, building on experiences from prior regional programs. A comprehensive M&E framework includes georeferencing and public accessibility. Sustainability is central, with a focus on institutional strengthening, capacity building, and a financial sustainability plan where national projects contribute to regional costs. Stakeholders and beneficiaries are included in decision-making through a consultative and participatory process.\", \"Risks are mitigated through several key strategies. ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Strengthening Systems for Social Protection and Civil Registration Project" aims to improve the coverage of social security allowances (SSA) and civil registration (CR), and enhance the delivery of social security allowances. The total project cost and total Bank financing are both USD 150.00 million, provided by the International Development Association (IDA). The project is structured around four components: Component 1 focuses on improving CR and SSA coverage by establishing a national population register of individuals and household/family folders, to be updated through regular CRs and targeted outreach, housed in an enhanced Management Information System (MIS) at the Department of Civil Registration (DOCR), with technical assistance from the World Health Organization (WHO). Component 2 aims to modernize social security delivery systems by supporting the roll-out of e-payments in 25 of 75 districts, strengthening other aspects of SSA benefit delivery through increased citizen engagement and IT use in all 75 districts, with technical assistance from the United Nations Capital Development Fund (UNCDF). Component 3 is dedicated to institutional strengthening through cross-cutting activities to build government capacity for managing CR and SSA functions at central and local levels. Finally, Component 4 supports project management, monitoring, and evaluation by establishing a Project Management Unit (PMU), district-level project implementation units (DL-PIUs), building staff capacity, and financing evaluation studies.

The Department of Civil Registration (DOCR) will be the implementing agency, mainstreaming most project activities into its day-to-day work. A PMU, led by the DOCR Director-General as Project Director, will oversee implementation with support from existing Directors, consultants for specialized areas, and DL-PIUs in all 75 districts. A project steering committee (PSC) chaired by the MOFALD Secretary, including joint secretaries from MOFALD and other ministries, and a technical committee will provide oversight. District-level project coordination committees (DPCCs) chaired by Local Development Officers will ensure inter-agency coordination. The project does not trigger any environmental or social safeguard policies, indicating no anticipated negative environmental or social impacts requiring mitigation under these policies.

The project is designed to address significant challenges in Nepal, including weak state presence in remote locations, limited state capacity to provide public services and resolve disputes, a largely informal rural economy with poverty reduction driven by remittances, and vulnerability to natural disasters. A key issue is the exclusion of millions from public services due to a lack of official documentation, compounded by fragmented, poorly coordinated, and manually managed social protection programs with high under-coverage (30-40% for senior citizen and single woman pensions) and suspicions of duplication and leakage. The existing CR system is underdeveloped, decentralized, paper-based, and suffers from low registration rates and limited use for vital statistics. There is also a lack of a comprehensive national population register and a non-robust identity ecosystem with no nationally unique identification numbers for citizenship or CR documents, leading to duplication and fraud, and poor coordination between ministries. However, the government has recognized the need for stronger safety nets, a population registry, and consolidating SP programs. Incremental improvements, building on demonstrated successes from previous pilots, are considered viable. MOFALD has already established DOCR to strengthen CR and SSA, developed an MIS (now in 25 districts with plans for all 75) capable of recording both CR and SSA data, and piloted commercial banks as payment service providers, indicating a foundation and institutional commitment for the proposed system-wide overhaul.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The targeted outcomes of the "Strengthening Systems for Social Protection and Civil Registration Project" are to improve the coverage of social security allowances (SSA) and civil registration (CR), and to enhance the delivery of social security allowances. More specifically, the project aims to: 1. Improve CR and SSA coverage by establishing a national population register of individuals and household/family folders, to be regularly updated through CRs and targeted outreach, housed in an enhanced Management Information System (MIS) at the Department of Civil Registration (DOCR). This includes establishing a Civil Registration and Vital Statistics (CRVS) system that allows individuals easy access to CR, enables regular monitoring of registrations, generates timely vital statistics from a central database, and links with programs like SSA. 2. Modernize social security delivery systems by supporting the roll-out of e-payments in 25 of 75 districts to improve operational efficiency and accountability of the SSA program. This also involves strengthening other aspects of SSA benefit delivery through increased citizen engagement and IT use in all 75 districts. 3. Build government organizational and institutional capacity at both central and local levels for managing CR and SSA functions, ensuring the sustainable institutionalization of the project's improvements. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: 1. A significant increase in the coverage of social security allowances and civil registration, implying a substantial reduction from the current 30-40% under-coverage for pensions and improved civil registration rates. 2. ACTIVITY CONTEXT: Nepal faces significant challenges: weak state presence in remote areas, limited state capacity, and an underdeveloped, fragmented, and poorly coordinated social protection (SP) and civil registration (CR) system. A non-robust identity ecosystem lacks unique national IDs, causing duplication and fraud. Public awareness and access to CR are low. While the government recognizes the need for stronger safety nets and a population registry, this recognition has yet to translate into concrete policy actions, posing a risk to sustained reform. ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity involves a system-wide overhaul of national civil registration and social protection, digitizing paper-based systems, establishing a national population register, rolling out e-payments across 75 districts, and institutional strengthening. IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The Department of Civil Registration (DOCR) is the primary implementing agency, with the Ministry of Home Affairs also listed as an implementer. The DOCR demonstrates significant skin-in-the-game and prior commitment to the activity. Potential factors that could deteriorate activity outcomes include the existing weak state presence and limited capacity, particularly in remote areas. The current fragmented, uncoordinated, and manually managed social protection and civil registration systems, along with a non-robust identity ecosystem lacking unique national IDs, pose significant challenges that the project aims to overcome.
