  "summary": "The Livestock Development Project is structured as an Investment Project Financing (IPF) initiative, funded by an IDA credit of US$100 million over six years. Its primary objective is to improve the productivity, competitiveness, and efficiency of smallholder short-cycle livestock producers, while enhancing the livelihoods and resilience of pastoral and agro-pastoral producers in target areas. For this project, efficiency is defined as the capacity to convert natural resources into animal products, measured by feed conversion ratio, GHG emissions per unit of product, and synthetic N and P used per unit of animal product. Key anticipated results include a reduction in losses from prevalent parasitic and infectious animal diseases such as Foot and Mouth Disease, Contagious Bovine Pleuropneumonia, Peste des Petits Ruminants, Newcastle Disease, and African Swine Fever; increased areas of rangelands under sustainable management practices; enhanced yield and efficiency across cattle, small ruminant, pig, poultry, and bee production systems for direct beneficiaries; and an increase in the value of marketed products and overall output of target animal production. The project is aligned with Cameroon's 2010-2014 Country Assistance Strategy, focusing on increasing competitiveness and improving service delivery within the livestock sector and its value chains.\n\nThe project is organized into four main components. Component 1, allocated US$10 million IDA, focuses on strengthening the animal health system to more rapidly detect and control parasitic and infectious diseases and prevent natural disasters. This involves fortifying veterinary services, including epidemio-surveillance and early detection systems for major animal diseases, reinforcing private veterinary practices, linking them with public authorities, and supporting Lanavet to increase vaccine production by 10 percent yearly. It also includes implementing surveillance and control plans for priority animal diseases and a contingency emergency response mechanism. Component 2, with US$25 million IDA, supports natural resources management, transhumance, and herder security by increasing the productivity and resilience of pastoral systems, improving transhumance routes, and financing community-managed Sub-Projects. This includes supporting pastoral land development to improve rangelands productivity and resilience to drought, securing access to rangelands and feed resources, and improving transhumance planning, rehabilitation, and demarcation of corridors to mitigate conflicts, particularly in the North, Far North, East, and Center regions, alongside building capacity for conflict prevention.\n\nComponent 3, receiving US$50 million IDA, is dedicated to the intensification of selected cattle, goat, sheep, pig, poultry, and bee farming systems, alongside value chain development. This involves establishing “Productive Partnerships” between producer organizations (POs) and buyers (agro-industries, SMEs), supporting access to rural financing, and financing PO Sub-Projects through matching grants, financial institution credits, and PO contributions. These investments cover collective actions like feed production, processing, improved animal husbandry, small-scale processing units, waste management systems to minimize greenhouse gas emissions and pollution, energy supply, and technical assistance aimed at increasing production, productivity, and improving post-harvest facilities and quality. The component will also work to improve breed productivity by increasing the provision of improved breeds to farmers. Component 4, with US$15 million IDA, aims to strengthen livestock institutions, policy capacity, and project coordination and monitoring. This includes establishing a Project Coordination Unit (PCU) at national and regional levels for project management, fiduciary oversight, and monitoring and evaluation, as well as identifying and financing long-term training and education needs to increase the pool of specialized experts in the country. The PCU will operate under the ministry in charge of livestock.\n\nQuantitatively, the project anticipates significant increases in livestock production by 2020 compared to 2014 and a 'without project' scenario. For instance, dairy cattle milk production is projected to increase from 13,978 tons (2014) to 95,466 tons (with project 2020), other cattle (beef) from 102,572 tons to 138,413 tons, sheep (mutton) from 12,833 tons to 20,979 tons, market swine (pork) from 28,150 tons to 36,981 tons, poultry (chicken) from 126,378 tons to 174,535 tons, goats from 25,341 tons to 44,685 tons, eggs from 70,846 tons to 196,916 tons (with an increase in eggs per layer per cycle from 240 to 260), and honey from 1,174 tons to 2,880 tons. Furthermore, the planned interventions are expected to result in a net GHG sink of 242.7 million tons of CO2 equivalent, primarily through improved livestock management practices, enhanced productivity, and resilience of pastoral production systems, and the intensification of various farming systems. In contrast, the 'without-project' scenario would lead to combined GHG emissions and carbon sequestration of 681,019,514 tCO2-º. The project ultimately aims to contribute to the World Bank Group's twin goals of improved food security, livelihoods, and poverty reduction for extremely poor beneficiaries, with shared prosperity measured by employment monitoring. This demand-driven approach will leverage public-private-partnerships and productive alliances, scaling up successful models like PACA's support for pig and poultry farming, to integrate smallholders into competitive value chains. The project triggers several safeguard policies including Environmental Assessment OP/BP 4.01, Natural Habitats OP/BP 4.04, and Forests OP/BP 4.36."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P154947
ACTIVITY TITLE: Regional Infrastructure Development Fund
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-03-31
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-11-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Single location

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 201,494,417

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: ID 100.00% | Location: Republic of Indonesia

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 4925

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Regional Infrastructure Development Fund (RIDF) activity aims to increase access to infrastructure finance at the subnational level through a financially sustainable financial intermediary. This proposed operation aligns with the Government of Indonesia's (GoI) development priorities as defined in the 2015-2019 National Medium Term Development Plan (RPJMN), which highlights the massive need for infrastructure delivery, particularly through subnational spending, and the acceleration of infrastructure development for growth and equity, specifically mentioning mechanisms like a municipal development fund for urban infrastructure. It is also well-aligned with the World Bank Group's Country Partnership Framework (CPF) for Indonesia for FY16-FY20, supporting sustainable urbanization and cities, including infrastructure financing options in key sectors such as urban transport, water supply and sanitation, drainage, urban flood and disaster risk management, slum upgrading, solid waste management, and urban pollution. Furthermore, the activity contributes to the World Bank Group's twin goals of ending extreme poverty and promoting shared prosperity by improving infrastructure and local service delivery, as improved quality of infrastructure is globally linked to economic growth and reductions in income inequality.

The activity addresses the challenge that subnational governments in Indonesia currently lack the technical, institutional, and financial capacity to carry out strategic infrastructure investments and keep pace with rapidly expanding demand for local services, despite subnational expenditure growing significantly and accounting for 60 percent of consolidated public infrastructure spending. The market for subnational borrowing also currently lacks options for medium to long-term financing. Lessons from previous projects, such as the Urban Sector Development Reform Project (USDRP), suggest that a financial intermediary solution would be more effective than direct on-lending to municipal governments, and that long-term operations and maintenance costs should be considered in initial planning. RIDF is designed as a retail domestic financial intermediary located within PT. Sarana Multi Infrastruktur (PT. SMI), focusing on providing long-term debt for economically viable infrastructure projects to creditworthy subnational governments at provincial and district levels, particularly fast-growing medium and large *kota* governments across all island groups. It is structured around principles of financial sustainability to leverage market-based sources of finance in the medium-term and aims to institutionalize municipal lending to crowd in market finance, complementing existing initiatives like the Indonesia Infrastructure Finance Facility (IIFF) and Indonesia Infrastructure Guarantee Fund (IIGF).

The Project comprises two components: Capital Support for RIDF and an RIDF Project Development Facility (PDF). The initial fund size for RIDF is US$ 400 million for the first four years, sourced from a combination of equity from MoF/PT. SMI and long-term debt from the World Bank and AIIB, with an initial debt-to-equity ratio of 1:1. The debt component, comprising US$ 100 million from IBRD and US$ 100 million from AIIB, will be channeled through a two-stage loan process where the GoI (represented by MoF) borrows in foreign currency and on-lends the proceeds to PT. SMI in IDR, with MoF assuming the exchange rate risk. A phased approach is envisioned, with additional financing of US$ 300 million anticipated for a second phase, bringing the aggregate borrowing to US$ 500 million. The PDF will support the development of a subproject pipeline and channel technical assistance to subnational governments in areas of project identification, design, construction supervision, and related advisory services. Subproject investments will be designed and appraised based on Indonesian standards and technical guidelines, with international standards used where local ones are unavailable. Financial management, procurement, and safeguards are outlined in the Project Operations Manual. The main beneficiaries of the Project are residents of the areas served by the infrastructure subprojects funded by sub-loans under Component 1.

For forecasting overall activity success, the achievement of the Project Development Objective will be measured through several results indicators. These include the number of subnational governments receiving RIDF loans, the average loan size approved by RIDF (in IDR billions), and the average tenor of loans approved by RIDF (in years), all to indicate increased access to infrastructure finance at the subnational level. Financial performance and health of RIDF will be tracked by the return on RIDF assets (after-tax profits/average assets percent), non-performing loans (NPLs) as a percentage of outstanding principal, and the proportion of the total loan portfolio concentrated in a single sector, with target values specified as minimums or maximums. Projections indicate a strong financial outlook, with RIDF's loan book expected to grow from US$ 26 million in year 1 to US$ 791 million in year 5, and total assets reaching over US$ 1.1 billion by year 10. Profits, driven by interest income, are projected to grow strongly in line with the loan portfolio, and key financial ratios such as net profit ratio, return on assets, and return on equity are expected to remain healthy throughout the period. Sensitivity analyses suggest profitability with a net interest margin of at least 0.65 percentage points and an NPL ratio remaining below 15 percent, while the debt service coverage ratio (DSCR) is projected to be comfortably above 1 in all drawdown scenarios. All data for these indicators will be collected annually from the RIDF Annual Report, with PT. SMI responsible for data collection.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The targeted outcomes of the Regional Infrastructure Development Fund (RIDF) activity are to increase access to infrastructure finance at the subnational level through a financially sustainable financial intermediary. To be considered an overall success, the activity must achieve specific targets related to both increased access to finance and the financial health of RIDF. Success will be measured by: (i) an increase in the number of subnational governments receiving RIDF loans, (ii) an increase in the average loan size approved by RIDF (in IDR billions), and (iii) an increase in the average tenor of loans approved by RIDF (in years). Simultaneously, RIDF's financial performance and sustainability must demonstrate: (iv) a healthy return on RIDF assets, (v) a low ratio of non-performing loans (NPLs), and (vi) an appropriate concentration of the total loan portfolio in a single sector.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: - Indonesia faces rapid urbanization (68% urban by 2025) and a massive local infrastructure deficit across critical services (water, sanitation, transport, housing), creating significant economic opportunity costs. The Regional Infrastructure Development Fund (RIDF) directly aligns with the Government of Indonesia's (GoI) 2015-2019 National Medium Term Development Plan and the World Bank's Country Partnership Framework for FY16-FY20, ensuring strong political and strategic support. There's a substantial untapped subnational borrowing potential (US$3.8 billion in 30 largest cities) within a sound regulatory framework, filling a critical 'missing middle' in long-term infrastructure finance. RIDF is also integrated into a broader World Bank-supported sustainable urbanization platform, leveraging additional sector investment and technical assistance programs, enhancing its ecosystem of support.
- Despite subnational governments now leading infrastructure spending, they often lack technical, institutional, and financial capacity for strategic investments. The private sector's appetite for long-term local infrastructure finance is constrained by asset-liability mismatches and risk aversion. Previous GoI initiatives to expand subnational debt for infrastructure (RDI, RDA, SLA) had limited success and significant arrears, indicating past challenges in implementation. Overall, infrastructure investment in Indonesia significantly declined post-1997 Asian financial crisis (from 7% to 3-4% of GDP), lagging regional competitors and highlighting a large backlog and the need for sustained recovery. The success of RIDF will depend on overcoming these historical capacity and market limitations.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity exhibits substantial complexity as a financial intermediary, addressing subnational capacity gaps and carrying 'Substantial' risks in Political and Governance, Technical Design, and Sector Strategies. It requires developing detailed operating procedures, robust appraisal criteria, and a comprehensive framework for corporate systems, including a Project Development Facility for technical assistance.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is highly integrated, aligning with Indonesia's RPJMN (2015-2019) and the World Bank Group's CPF (FY16-FY20). It complements existing initiatives like the IIFF and IIGF, functioning as a retail domestic financial intermediary within PT. SMI to institutionalize municipal lending, rather than being an independent program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "Sub-activities: Capital Support for RIDF (US$400M) for subnational loans, and RIDF Project Development Facility (PDF) (US$6M) for technical assistance. Total budget: US$406M. Initial funding from IBRD (US$100M), AIIB (US$100M), Borrower (US$203M), MDTF (US$3M). The initial US$400M fund, plus anticipated US$300M in phase two, is small compared to the US$11.1B subnational infrastructure financing gap, indicating likely under-funding relative to need. RIDF projects strong financial health, which is positive, but its limited scale will constrain overall success in fully addressing the vast demand."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: PT. Sarana Multi Infrastruktur (PT. SMI) is the implementing agency, demonstrating significant investment by contributing equity to the RIDF. As a state-owned enterprise, it operates with independent management, allowing for autonomous appraisal and credit decisions, supported by robust technical and financial capacity, including a clear mandate for subnational infrastructure lending. RIDF within PT. SMI has dedicated staffing, KPIs, and a strong governance structure (Board of Commissioners and Directors) ensuring accountability. The Government of Indonesia (GoI), particularly the Ministry of Finance (MoF), significantly aids outcomes by providing additional equity, assuming exchange rate risk, managing loans, and establishing a supportive regulatory environment. The World Bank and AIIB provide substantial debt financing. While subnational governments have capacity gaps, the Project Development Facility (PDF) within RIDF will provide technical assistance to mitigate this risk and improve outcomes. The activity also aims to crowd in market finance, suggesting future private sector aid.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- Political and Governance Risk: Substantial risks may arise from competing interests at the local level, which may result in delays to individual subprojects. Subnational government borrowing requires approval from local legislatures.
- Macroeconomic Risk: Sluggish global growth and associated uncertainty have put pressure on Indonesia's economy. The Government has faced significant fiscal pressures since mid-2013.
- Sector Strategies and Policies Risk: Substantial sector-related policy risks exist, including that subnational governments may encounter delays in obtaining necessary concurrence from MoHA for proposed borrowing. Regulatory uncertainty exists related to revisions of Framework Law 33/2004 on the intergovernmental fiscal framework, where hastily developed provisions on subnational borrowing could unduly constrain the market.
- Technical Design of Project or Program Risk: There is a substantial risk of slow implementation and disbursement should the subproject pipeline turn out to be weak, as specific subprojects are not confirmed before Project approval.
- Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability Risk: PT.SMI institutional capacity risk.
- Fiduciary Risk.
- Environment and Social Risk.
- Stakeholders Risk: Substantial risks may arise from competing interests at the local level, which may result in delays to individual subprojects.
- Credit Risk: A substantial risk exists that subnational governments will fail to honor repayment commitments to RIDF.
- Climate and Disaster Risk.
- Financial performance risk if the net interest margin falls below 0.65 percentage points.
- Financial performance risk if the non-performing loans (NPLs) ratio exceeds 15 percent.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P155038
ACTIVITY TITLE: Andhra Pradesh 24X7 Power for All
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-11-30
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2022-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 363,318,042

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: IN 100.00% | Location: West Godāvari; Visakhapatnam; Vijayawada; Srīkākulam; Nellore; Nandyal; Medak; Kurnool; Kistnapatam; Krishna

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 2696

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Andhra Pradesh 24X7 Power for All" activity is designed to increase electricity delivery to customers and enhance operational efficiency and system reliability in selected areas of Andhra Pradesh's distribution network. This project aligns with the broader goal of supporting poverty reduction and shared prosperity in India by ensuring affordable, reliable electricity, and specifically aims to improve operational efficiency in the state's transmission and distribution sector. The project targets existing and new customers of power distribution companies, particularly those in rural areas, including the less developed Rayalaseema region (Anantapur and Kurnool districts), who will benefit from augmented grid-based electricity supply. Andhra Pradesh faces an annual energy demand growth exceeding 8.5%, with a projected peak demand of 13,400 MW by FY 2019, necessitating substantial investments in its intra-state transmission (~US$1.1 billion) and distribution network (~US$1.17 billion) over the next five years. To achieve these goals, the project includes several components: US$200 million (IBRD $140 million) for urban distribution investments focusing on network augmentation (33kV and below), smart grids with smart metering, underground cabling, Ariel Bunched Cables, and ICT interventions in urban and coastal towns vulnerable to natural hazards, to reduce technical and commercial losses, improve operational efficiency, and boost reliability. An additional US$215 million (IBRD $150 million) is dedicated to rural distribution, strengthening the network (33kV and below), implementing the High Voltage Distribution System (HVDS), and feeder segregation in identified areas, especially to provide better quality power and reduce losses in districts like Anantapur and Kurnool, which currently have poor infrastructure and high AT&C losses (18.3% and 10.8% respectively). US$50 million (IBRD $30 million) is allocated for deploying solar agri-pumpsets in rural areas, and US$10 million (IBRD $10 million) supports technical assistance for capacity building, including improving institutional structures, business processes, and ICT integration for distribution companies (e.g., ERP systems), alongside deploying Third Party Quality Assurance Consultants and developing social and environmental management frameworks such as a Resettlement Policy Framework, Tribal People Plan Framework, and a Gender Action Plan. Key performance indicators for success include increasing electricity delivery (GWh), reducing AT&C losses in select districts, and decreasing distribution transformer failure rates, with a plan to establish baselines for SAIDI and SAIFI during project implementation for subsequent measurement. While Andhra Pradesh's distribution companies are advanced in ICT deployment, and inter-regional transmission constraints are expected to be resolved by 2018, the state's power system is described as delicately balanced, and challenges like varying AT&C loss levels across rural districts and vulnerability to natural calamities in coastal areas are explicitly addressed through targeted investments. This project, being one of the first World Bank engagements under India's 24x7 Power for All agenda, is also expected to provide valuable lessons for future projects.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: Targeted outcomes include increasing electricity delivery and system capacity, improving operational efficiency and reliability, meeting growing demand, reducing AT&C losses and distribution transformer failure rates, providing 24x7 power to households and access for unconnected consumers, and reducing poverty and GHG emissions. Success criteria are: electricity supply reaching 74,000 GWh; AT&C losses reducing to 9.25% in Kurnool and 15.5% in Anantapur; increased transmission capacity; decreased transformer failure rates; 20.0% economic rate of return; and 0.9 million tons CO2e reduction.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The external context for the Andhra Pradesh 24X7 Power for All activity indicates a highly supportive and reformed environment for the power sector, significantly contributing to the probability of its overall success. Andhra Pradesh was the third state in India to enact power sector reform legislation (February 1999) and the first to enact anti-theft legislation (July 2000), demonstrating a proactive and progressive approach to energy governance. The state's power system operates within a mature institutional structure, which includes the unbundling of the former Andhra Pradesh State Electricity Board (APSEB) into APGENCO (generation), APTRANSCO (transmission and bulk supply), and four distribution companies (DISCOMs) for retail supply across different regions. The Andhra Pradesh Electricity Regulatory Commission (APERC), established in April 1999, is an independent and autonomous body with a strong track record of transparent regulatory processes, stakeholder involvement, independent decision-making, and setting regulatory precedents in the country. This stable and credible regulatory framework, along with the financial autonomy granted to DISCOMs since 2002, provides a robust foundation for project implementation. The state has also implemented measures to control electricity theft and improve operational efficiency. Although the state was bifurcated in 2014, leading to the formation of Telangana, the power sector entities in Andhra Pradesh have been restructured (APGENCO, APTRANSCO statewide, and two distribution companies for the remaining districts), indicating adaptation to significant administrative changes. The activity aligns with India's broader 24x7 Power for All agenda, suggesting strong national-level political commitment. This is further supported by the involvement of the Government of Andhra Pradesh as the responsible agency and borrower, and co-financing from the AIIB. Economically, Andhra Pradesh faces an annual energy demand growth exceeding 8.5%, with a projected peak demand of 13,400 MW by FY 2019, indicating a clear and growing need for increased electricity delivery and enhanced system reliability. Moreover, inter-regional transmission constraints are expected to be resolved by 2018, which should further ease power availability. Operationally, APGENCO's Plant Load Factor (PLF) has consistently been higher than the national average over the past decade, and the state's distribution companies are advanced in ICT deployment. Geographically, the state is coastal and vulnerable to natural hazards, a factor explicitly addressed by the project's targeted investments in ICT interventions and underground cabling in vulnerable towns. The large rural population (70.4%) underscores the relevance of rural distribution and solar agri-pumpset components. This comprehensive and supportive external environment significantly enhances the probability of the activity's success.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity involves significant technical complexity, encompassing network augmentation, smart grids with smart metering, underground cabling, and ICT interventions in urban areas; strengthening rural distribution networks with High Voltage Distribution Systems and feeder segregation; and deploying solar agri-pumpsets. It also includes comprehensive technical assistance for capacity building, institutional structures, business processes, and ERP systems. The project addresses a "delicately balanced" power system, varying AT&C loss levels, and vulnerability to natural calamities, requiring targeted investments and advanced solutions like SCADA and automated substations to improve efficiency and reliability. Its pioneering nature as one of the first World Bank engagements under India's 24x7 Power for All agenda further highlights its complex implementation.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is a major project that is directly and cohesively integrated into "India's 24x7 Power for All agenda" (also referred to as the "state's Power for All Program"). The activity specifically supports the broader goal of ensuring affordable, reliable electricity and improving operational efficiency in Andhra Pradesh, aligning with the larger national program's objectives. The "24x7 Power for All agenda" can be considered a larger program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "Total project cost US$570M, no financing gap. Financed by IBRD (US$240M), AIIB (US$160M), Borrower (US$170M). Sub-activities & budgets: Power Transmission System Strengthening (US$100M), Smart Grid Development Urban Areas (US$210M), Distribution System Strengthening – Rural (US$250M), Technical Assistance (US$10M), Front-end fees (US$1M). Project is fully funded. However, its US$570M addresses only part of state's 5-year needs (~US$1.1B transmission, ~US$1.17B distribution). US$50M for solar agri-pumpsets is allocated but not itemized in detailed breakdown. Activity success well-financed; distribution companies' financial health projected to improve by FY 2020 via efficiency and strong government (UDAY) support."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The implementing organizations (GoAP, APTRANSCO, APEPDCL, APSPDCL) show significant skin-in-the-game. GoAP contributes $175M, and IAs must repay the World Bank loan. Abilities include advanced ICT, with project focus on operational reforms and capacity building to optimize ICT use. The World Bank provides $400M, technical assistance, and Project Management Consultants. Citizen engagement, including a Gender Action Plan, aids outcomes by ensuring participation and grievance redressal. Potential deterrents like natural calamities, varying AT&C losses, and construction impacts are addressed by targeted investments and mitigation plans.
ACTIVITY RISKS: Relevant risks to the Andhra Pradesh 24X7 Power for All activity not achieving overall success include: 1. Financial viability of the sector due to complex issues of political economy and governance, as distribution companies suffered financial losses from high short power purchases to meet growing energy deficits. The gap between Aggregate Revenue Requirement (ARR) and Approved Cost of Supply (ACS) increased, not met by government support or tariff adjustments, leading to short-term debts and high interest payments. 2. Challenges in managing scarce power supply between different consumer categories (agriculture, small domestic, industrial) due to policies like free/subsidized power for agriculture, impacting financial health of distribution companies and economic growth. 3. Distribution utilities faced financial stress from non-recovery of cost of supply, including large Government receivables and unapproved/pending Fuel Surcharge Adjustment write-offs, leading to cumulative losses and reliance on short-term loans. 4. Inadequate investment in transmission and distribution infrastructure resulting from financial stress, leading to network bottlenecks, high transformer loading, high distribution transformer failure rates (~5% annually), and components reaching their rated economic life. 5. Substantial stakeholder risk, as the Project's full success depends on effective collaboration between all stakeholders, including the Government of Andhra Pradesh, state regulator, and implementing agencies.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The project has several positive indicators and reasons for overall success or ability to overcome inherent risks. Andhra Pradesh's distribution companies are advanced in ICT deployment, and inter-regional transmission constraints are expected to be resolved by 2018. The Project implementing agencies have adopted an environmental and social management framework (ESMF) to ensure environmental and social sustainability. The utilities involved are experienced in the operations of the assets, and their O&M practices will be strengthened through the technical assistance component. The Project will also indirectly assist distribution companies in financial recovery and long-term sustainability by improving commercial and procurement processes, and IT systems for better management reporting. The Government of India (GoI) and the Government of Andhra Pradesh (GoAP) are fully aware of the financial situation and are committed to working on these issues through the Ujjwal Discom Assurance Yojana (UDAY), which is a financial turnaround and revival package. Andhra Pradesh has also prepared a business plan to understand key business drivers and steps to improve financial performance. Stakeholder risk is addressed by the establishment of a steering committee. The technical design risk is rated as 'Moderate,' as Andhra Pradesh utilities are well-versed in the planning, design, development, and operation of a robust T&D system and have a strong track record of introducing advanced technology. Safeguards management is central to the project design, addressing exclusion and vulnerability through capacity building, information, education, communication, consultations, negotiations, transparency, and accountability. The ESMF is prepared based on World Bank-approved projects and incorporates India's 'The Right to Fair Compensation in Land Acquisition, Resettlement and Rehabilitation Act, 2013' and the Indian Constitution's Fifth Schedule provisions to protect tribal interests, including a Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and a Tribal People Development Planning Framework (TPPF). The Project recognizes and plans for gender benefits, promoting gender equality and women's empowerment through specific gender actions at both project and community levels, with a Gender Action Plan (GAP) to maximize benefits and minimize vulnerabilities. The state is committed to citizen engagement, with inbuilt mechanisms like annual consultations, consent in tribal areas, external oversight, sharing of plans, multi-layered grievance redressal arrangements, and full adoption of the Right to Information Act, fostering legitimacy, inclusion, and accountability. Most identified environmental impacts are short-term, modest, site-specific, and reversible, with manageable mitigation measures. The ESMF provides adequate guidelines for preparing required environmental safeguards, and PIUs will integrate environmental management requirements into contract documents and conduct monthly on-site monitoring. Training modules for capacity building and stakeholder consultations have been held, with the ESMF and generic ESMP disclosed publicly."

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Madhya Pradesh Urban Development Project aims to enhance the capacity of the Madhya Pradesh Urban Development Company (MPUDC) to improve coverage of key urban services and increase the revenue of participating urban local bodies. The project has a total financing of USD 166.00 million, comprising USD 49.80 million from the Borrower and USD 116.20 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development, with expected disbursements from 2017 to 2023. An amendment to the Loan Agreement, dated June 28, 2021, revised the project's Results Framework and removed activities related to the Bhopal-Indore Super Corridor region (BISCO), also deleting its definition from the project appendix. This amendment refined Part A.2 of Schedule 1 to specifically include technical and project management assistance to MPUDC for strengthening its institutional and management capacity for project implementation.

The project is structured into two main components: Institutional Development, with a cost of USD 26.50 million, and Urban Investments, costing USD 139.20 million, along with a USD 0.30 million front-end fee. The project is aligned with India's twelfth development plan (2012–2017) and supports the AMRUT and SCM schemes, contributing to urban infrastructure, financial sustainability of Urban Local Bodies (ULBs), and the 2013–2017 Country Partnership Strategy for India by focusing on urban transformation and poverty reduction in low-income states. The lead practice area for the project is Social, Urban, Rural and Resilience Global Practice, with Governance and Water as contributing areas.

The project is expected to benefit approximately 0.85 million urban residents in participating ULBs, with at least 45% being women, through improved access to basic municipal services such as water, wastewater, sewerage, and solid waste management. MPUDC and participating ULBs will also benefit from strengthened capacity and support for revenue base and financial management systems. The revised Results Framework outlines key performance indicators and their end targets: 60 projects to be completed by MPUDC, 30 participating ULBs reporting an average annual growth rate in own-source revenue of at least 10%, 280,000 people in urban areas provided with access to improved water sources, and 340,000 people provided with access to improved sanitation.

For forecasting overall activity success, the project's clear objectives, specific measurable targets, and substantial funding are positive indicators. Its strong alignment with national development priorities and focus on both infrastructure investment and institutional capacity building (for MPUDC and ULBs) are crucial for sustainable outcomes. However, the Systematic Operations Risk-Rating Tool (SORT) identifies an overall "Substantial" risk. Notably, "Environment and Social" risk is rated "High," while "Technical Design of Project or Program," "Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability," and "Fiduciary" risks are all rated "Substantial." "Political and Governance" and "Macroeconomic" risks are "Low," and "Sector Strategies and Policies" and "Stakeholders" risks are "Moderate." The successful mitigation of these identified high and substantial risks, particularly concerning environmental and social impacts, technical design, institutional implementation capacity, and fiduciary management, will be critical for achieving the project's ambitious development objectives and ensuring its overall success.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The activity targets enhancing MPUDC's capacity to improve urban service coverage and increase revenue of participating ULBs. Outcomes include: 60 projects completed by MPUDC; 51 ULBs with >=10% annual own-source revenue growth; 280,000 people with improved water access; 340,000 people with improved sanitation. Overall success requires achieving these targets and crucially mitigating identified 'High' Environment and Social risk, and 'Substantial' risks in Technical Design, Institutional Capacity, and Fiduciary management, ensuring sustainable outcomes.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: - The project operates amidst India's rapid urbanization, with 50% urban population projected by 2030, driving massive infrastructure investment needs (US$600-822 billion nationally). "Sector Strategies and Policies" and "Stakeholders" risks are "Moderate," suggesting general alignment but requiring careful management of diverse interests. However, sensitive water bodies and historic structures in project locations demand meticulous planning for environmental and social impacts.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is technically complex, with an overall implementation risk assessed as "Substantial." Specific risks include "High" for Environmental and Social, and "Substantial" for Technical Design of Project or Program, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Fiduciary. Technical complexities involve potential political economy issues in revenue generation and managing innovative build-operate contracts. FINANCING DETAILS: "The project has an updated total financing of USD 156.90 million. Sub-activities are grouped into two main components: Institutional Development (USD 26.50 million) and Urban Investments (USD 139.20 million), plus a USD 0.30 million front-end fee. It also serves as the Project Management Unit (PMU) for overall project management and execution. The project specifically aims to enhance MPUDC's institutional and management capacity through technical and project management assistance, addressing the 'Substantial' risk identified for 'Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability.' The PMU within MPUDC will be staffed by a Project Director from the state government and various specialists (Financial, Procurement, Environmental/Social Safeguards, M&E, Municipal Engineer), supported by 13 regional Project Implementation Units (PIUs) for ground-level supervision.The implementing organization, MPUDC, demonstrates 'skin-in-the-game' through the Borrower's substantial financial contribution of USD 49.80 million (out of USD 166.00 million), making the state government (which oversees MPUDC) a significant financial partner. ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-06-15
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2023-12-31

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 235,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: IN 100.00% | Location: Vaishali; Purnia; Purba Champaran; Gopalganj; Araria; Buxar; Katihar; Banka

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 2696

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Bihar Rural Roads Project (P155522) is an Investment Project Financing activity by The World Bank, approved on December 19, 2016, with a closing date of December 31, 2022. It is not a regionally tagged project and involves no Bank/IFC collaboration. The project is categorized as B - Partial Assessment for environmental assessment. The Republic of India is the Borrower, with the Rural Works Department, Bihar, serving as the Implementing Agency, funded via an IDA Credit.

The Proposed Development Objective (PDO) for this project is to improve rural road connectivity in project districts and enhance the management of rural roads in Bihar. The project is designed with two main components: Rural Roads Improvement, costing USD 320.00 Million, and Asset Management and Institutional Effectiveness, costing USD 15.00 Million.

The project is fully aligned with the World Bank Group's goal of reducing poverty and promoting shared prosperity, and India's Country Partnership Strategy 2013-2017 priorities of supporting low-income states through integration, transformation, and inclusion. It aims to better integrate rural populations, businesses, and industries in Bihar's remote and poorer districts with the national and state economy via improved transport connectivity and integration with strategic transport corridors. Improved road access is expected to have a transformational impact on rural poverty by enhancing agricultural productivity, creating higher non-farm employment, increasing rural wages, and facilitating better inclusion of marginalized communities through improved access to markets, jobs, growth opportunities, and services. Furthermore, it supports the Government of India's development priorities, including the Twelfth Five-Year Plan (2012-17), which emphasizes rural road development for inclusive growth, economic development, and market access, and the current priority of doubling farmers' incomes. The project also contributes to Bihar's efforts in achieving several Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), specifically Goal 1 (Ending Poverty), Goal 2 (Food Security), Goal 3 (Health and well-being), Goal 4 (Inclusive, Equitable and Quality Education), Goal 8 (Sustainable and Inclusive Economic Growth and Employment), and Goal 10 (Reduce Inequality).

The project is expected to provide all-weather road access to economic opportunities and social services for approximately 1.2 million people, primarily from poorer and vulnerable sections of society, with 30 percent below the poverty line and 48 percent being females. A maintenance program supported by the project will further benefit about 30 million people in the state by ensuring a good quality road network and improved access to rural transport and other social services. Capacity building initiatives will benefit around 2,500 Rural Works Department (RWD) staff, 1,000 contractors, and 600 classified consultants, alongside fostering the development of many small contractors.

For forecasting overall activity success, key Project Development Objective Indicators target by Year 6 and End Target include increasing road connectivity to habitations to 62000.00, raising roads in good and fair condition as a share of total classified roads to 50.00, achieving 50.00 for improved effectiveness of project expenditure through cost-effective designs, and establishing and using an asset management system in 10 districts. Intermediate Results Indicators for success include the construction of 2500.00 Rural Roads, achieving 85.00 for improved quality of built infrastructure certified through independent reviews, extending the length of roads subject to planned maintenance contracting to 10000.00, establishing an effective citizen feedback mechanism rating of 85.00, and undertaking 2000.00 road safety improvement scheme-related audits on priority.

The project is designed as a holistic solution to leverage the entire rural road sector in Bihar, complementing traditional World Bank funding for MMGSY with knowledge support to enhance management. While funding only 10 percent of MMGSY, it aims to leverage project benefits to improve the entire MMGSY by enhancing its systems, procedures, and compliance, using existing MMGSY implementation arrangements with minor variations for easy integration. Enhancements will focus on improved planning processes for project selection, use of climate-resilient and cost-effective engineering designs (incorporating road safety, social, and environmental issues), improved procurement and contract management to avoid time and cost-overruns, improved quality assurance for longer-life roads, enhanced verification and oversight mechanisms, citizen participation, and improved monitoring and management tools. The project will also support effective asset management, enhance the capacity of the RWD and the construction industry, and transform the entire road agency into a modern entity through a Road Sector Modernization Plan. Cost-effective designs are expected to save approximately 25 percent in the unit cost of rural roads, and improved asset management could minimize about US$400 million in annual potential losses in road asset value and save an equal amount in vehicle operating costs. Component 1, Rural Roads Improvement, will support the construction and improvement of the state core network to enhance road connectivity for target habitations under MMGSY (populations 250-499 in 2012) in project districts. This component includes civil works for about 2,500 km of rural roads and standalone bridges, design, implementation and management support, and pilot initiatives for innovative bridge construction, retrofitting road safety, and climate-resilient measures in the existing rural road network, demonstrating new technologies for cost-effective, modern, climate-resilient, and environment-friendly road construction.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The targeted outcomes are to improve rural road connectivity in project districts and enhance rural road management in Bihar, providing all-weather access for 1.2 million people and benefiting 30 million with improved networks. Success entails: road connectivity to habitations reaching 62,000; 50% of roads in good/fair condition; 50% improved expenditure effectiveness via cost-effective designs; asset management systems used in 10 districts; 2,500 km of rural roads constructed with 85% quality; 10,000 km under planned maintenance; 85% effective citizen feedback; and 2,000 road safety audits.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The project benefits from strong external alignment and support, increasing its success probability. It's fully aligned with World Bank Group goals, India's Country Partnership Strategy, Government of India's Twelfth Five-Year Plan (emphasizing rural road development and doubling farmers' incomes), and Bihar's SDGs. It leverages the ongoing MMGSY program, integrating existing implementation arrangements. The World Bank provides continuous technical support for climate-resilient designs, institutional modernization via RWD's RSMP, and road safety initiatives, backed by IDA Credit. This high-level commitment and integration with established frameworks are key enablers.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is technically complex, involving a holistic solution to leverage the entire rural road sector in Bihar. It aims to enhance systems for planning, design (climate-resilient, cost-effective, incorporating safety, social, environmental aspects), procurement, contract management, quality assurance, verification, oversight, citizen participation, monitoring, and asset management. It includes civil works for 2,500 km of roads and bridges, innovative construction, retrofitting safety, and climate-resilient measures, supported by World Bank and international experts, with the goal of modernizing the Rural Works Department.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The Bihar Rural Roads Project is integrated directly and cohesively into a larger program, the MMGSY (Mahatma Gandhi Gramin Sadak Yojana), which itself is a larger program. The project aims to improve the entire MMGSY by enhancing its systems, procedures, and compliance, while funding approximately 10 percent of MMGSY. The project's two components total USD 335.00 Million, indicating a substantial scale.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Bihar Rural Roads Project has a total cost of US$335 million, fully financed with US$235 million from IDA and US$100 million from the Government of Bihar (GoB). There is a US$0.00 financing gap. Sub-activities are under two main components: 1. Rural Roads Improvement (US$320 million; IDA: US$224M, GoB: US$96M): Includes construction/improvement of ~2,500 km of roads/bridges, design/management support, and pilot initiatives for innovative/climate-resilient construction using local materials to reduce costs. 2. Asset Management and Institutional Effectiveness (US$15 million; IDA: US$11M, GoB: US$4M): Covers HR development, RWD infrastructure support, business process modernization, road safety management, and establishing an asset management system. GoB's US$100 million counterpart funding explicitly covers land acquisition, compensation, and regulatory clearances, which are often causes of funding issues. GoB has committed to annual O&M funding (INR405 million) from 2022-2036. The project employs cost-effective designs, expected to save ~25% in unit costs. No under-funded aspects are identified; clear financing commitments and cost-saving strategies suggest strong financial viability."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The Rural Works Department (RWD) of Bihar and the Bihar Rural Road Development Agency (BRRDA) are the primary implementing organizations. Their likely performance is robust due to several factors: they are leveraging existing systems like a custom-designed Management Information System (MIS) for MMGSY and an operational mobile application ("Meri Sadak") application for citizen feedback. The project aims for significant modernization through a Road Sector Modernization Plan, streamlining and computerizing RWD's key business processes. They are committed to quality, engaging technical examiners, contract staff, a Project Management Consultant, and independent safeguard monitoring consultants. Extensive capacity building for 2,500 RWD staff, 1,000 contractors, and 600 consultants is planned. The organization shows strong skin-in-the-game through the project's alignment with national and state development priorities and explicit commitments to maintaining adequate maintenance funding, introducing performance-based contracts, and integrating climate-resilient designs. Transparency and accountability are high, with public disclosure of technical examiner reports and a formal grievance redressal mechanism (GRM). The project also demonstrates a strong commitment to gender and social inclusion, with specific actions to ensure women's participation.Other stakeholders are integral to the project's success. Local communities are actively involved in planning, design, and feedback. Local administration, forest, revenue, and rural development departments provide support. External experts, including technical examiners, the Project Management Consultant, and independent consultants, ensure quality assurance and problem-solving. Academic and research institutions contribute to new technologies and designs. Capacity building extends to contractors, and local police and transport departments will receive training for road safety management. There are no indications that other relevant stakeholders would deteriorate activity outcomes; instead, their involvement is structured to provide aid through collaboration, oversight, and feedback, enhancing the overall likelihood of success.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- The overall implementation risk of achieving the PDO is rated substantial.
- The technical design of the project and institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability pose substantial risks.
- Climate resilient engineering designs and other innovative activities under the Road Sector Modernization Plan (RSMP) are relatively new to RWD, requiring significant augmentation to its implementation capacity and knowledge base.
- RWD needs to effectively cope with the big demands of MMGSY and PMGSY and to deliver on prescribed standards, as well as focus on maintenance of the existing rural road network.
- The construction industry in Bihar is relatively less developed, contributing to implementation delays and quality issues in designs and built infrastructure.
- RWD needs to improve both the pace and quality of delivery of its road programs by enhancing engineering designs, asset management, value for money, procurement performance, and by minimizing time and cost overruns.
- RWD's original institutional structure and business procedures are primarily suited for small works and have not kept pace with current demands and latest developments in the rural road sector.
- The capacity of the local construction industry needs strengthening, especially in construction management practices and adoption of modern technologies.
- The unit cost of rural road construction in Bihar is relatively high due to long haulage of aggregates, extra flood protection works, and design procedures ignoring local materials.
- RWD needs to undertake a climate vulnerability assessment of the existing rural road network and incorporate suitable remedial measures.
- Road safety is a critical issue due to geometric deficiencies, narrow roadways, missing protection structures, and negligible awareness.
- There is a need for more efficient and reliable transport services in rural areas, which is currently under-developed.
- Poor and marginalized groups are unable to access job opportunities due to lack of adequate skills, while road programs suffer from non-availability of technical staff and skilled workers.
- Project activities, if not properly planned, managed, and mitigated, could have adverse environmental impacts such as affecting natural drainage, felling of trees, impacts on religious properties, soil erosion, construction phase pollution, and safety concerns.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Project aligns strongly with WB poverty reduction and national development goals, promising transformational impact on rural poverty, productivity, and employment. Robust PDO/Intermediate Results Indicators are in place. Holistic design enhances existing systems, incorporating climate-resilient, cost-effective engineering and road safety plans. Strong analytical underpinnings and GoB's commitment to maintenance/reforms are key. Operational sustainability secured via quality engineering and audits. Gender integrated. An Environment Management Framework and grievance redress mechanisms exist. 'Other' risk is low."

The Republic of Albania is the borrower, with the Ministry of Economic Development, Tourism, Trade and Entrepreneurship serving as the implementation agency. The financing for this activity is an IBRD Loan amounting to EUR 65.8 million. The DPL is structured around three core pillars: (i) Attracting and Retaining Investment, (ii) Making it easier to do Business, and (iii) Facilitating Trade. The overall risk rating for the operation is substantial, and there are no short or long-term climate and disaster risks identified as relevant.

Under Pillar I: Attracting and Retaining Investment, the DPL supports the development of a comprehensive investment regime and the creation of a mechanism to address investor grievances. This is expected to lead to a strong and transparent investment policy framework that will help spur local business development, attract foreign investors, and harness spill-overs from FDI. The government aims to consolidate investment laws, develop a systematic mechanism for identifying and addressing investor grievances, and create a single, comprehensive investment law to improve clarity for all investors and provide greater protection. Efforts are also focused on reducing the high number of international investment disputes, which incur significant reputational costs, by establishing a formal mechanism to ensure regulatory compliance across government agencies and promote timely problem-solving. Result indicators for this pillar include increasing the number of investment leads generated annually by AIDA by 20 percent and retaining US$60 million in investment through the Investor Grievance Mechanism, both by 2018.

Pillar II: Making it easier to do Business, aims to upgrade Albania's systems and processes in areas such as construction permitting, electricity, business licensing, and national quality infrastructure to reduce the burden on businesses. The DPL supports reforms targeted at reducing administrative burdens associated with construction permits, electricity connections, and business licensing, while also addressing gaps in the national quality infrastructure. The Ministry for Economic Development, Tourism, Trade and Entrepreneurship leads this effort, having established an inter-ministerial working group that has drafted an Action Plan to improve Albania's performance across all Doing Business topics within the next two years. Key result indicators for this pillar include an improved business environment, evidenced by an increase in the DB DTF for Overall Doing Business to 70, for Construction permits to 66, and for Electricity to 48. Additionally, inspections are to commence for at least two groups of non-food consumer products.

Pillar III: Facilitating Trade, addresses Albania's trade volumes, which are substantially below its peers and potential, largely due to difficulties in efficient cross-border trade and weak management at the port of Durres. The DPL supports the government's efforts to streamline border clearance processes, introduce risk management for food products, and reform the port of Durres, building on the momentum of the Joint Action Plan to Facilitate Trade and Transport in the Western Balkans. Expected outcomes for this pillar include a 15 percent reduction in imported cargo physically inspected by NFA inspectors at border inspection points, the annual value of imports under local clearance procedures reaching $70 million, and a 20 percent reduction in cargo dwell time at the port of Durres.

The proposed DPL has been developed jointly by the World Bank Group, leveraging an extensive technical assistance program covering investment policy and promotion, trade facilitation, and the Doing Business indicator reform program. The World Bank Group will jointly monitor and support the implementation of future reforms. This DPL is also complementary to two other policy-based interventions supporting Albania's reform agenda: the Financial Sector DPL II (US$100 million), focusing on reducing Non-Performing Loans and strengthening the financial sector, and the Public Finance Policy-Based Guarantee (US$227 million), which supports reforms in public financial management, tax, pension, and energy sectors to improve fiscal sustainability. These three interventions collectively form a coherent package to address critical growth and distributional issues over the medium term. The government has demonstrated strong commitment to this DPL-supported reform program.

The World Bank projects Albania's growth to rise to 3.2 percent in 2016 and 3.5 percent in 2017, driven by domestic demand and private investment in projects like the Trans Adriatic Pipeline and a hydropower plant. While government spending will remain limited due to fiscal consolidation, investment climate bottlenecks, particularly in business regulations, are identified as primary constraints to private sector development, which the DPL directly addresses. Though the current account deficit is projected to widen due to FDI-related imports, external financing is deemed sustainable. The medium-term outlook for Albania's economic recovery pathway, and thus the potential success of the DPL, is heavily dependent on the pace and depth of additional structural reforms in the energy and financial sectors, as well as improvements in public investment management. The DPL's reforms are expected to contribute significantly to improving fiscal balances and sustaining high revenue collections as part of ongoing fiscal consolidation measures. The World Bank has been closely engaged through technical assistance, advising agencies like the National Agency for Territorial Development (NATD) on streamlining construction permits and the Albanian Investment Development Agency (AIDA), indicating strong institutional support and readiness for reform implementation.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The Albania Competitiveness Development Policy Loan (DPL) targets enhancing Albania's competitiveness by improving its investment regime, making it easier to do business, and facilitating trade.The activity will be considered an overall success if the following specific outcomes are achieved by 2018:Under Pillar I: Attracting and Retaining Investment, the targeted outcomes include developing a comprehensive investment regime, creating a mechanism for investor grievances, establishing a strong and transparent investment policy framework to spur local business and attract foreign investors, consolidating investment laws into a single, clear framework, and reducing international investment disputes.Specific success criteria for this pillar are: (1) an increase in the number of investment leads generated annually by AIDA by 20 percent (from a baseline of 15 to a target of 18), and (2) US$60 million in investment retained through the Investor Grievance Mechanism.Under Pillar II: Making it easier to do Business, the activity aims to upgrade systems and processes in construction permitting, electricity, business licensing, and national quality infrastructure to reduce administrative burdens.Specific success criteria for this pillar are: (1) an improved business environment evidenced by an increase in the Doing Business Distance to Frontier (DB DTF) for Overall Doing Business to 70, (2) a streamlined construction process evidenced by an increase in the DB DTF for Construction permits to 66, (3) reduced time and procedures for new electricity connections evidenced by an increase in the DB DTF for Electricity to 48, and (4) the commencement of inspections for at least two groups of non-food consumer products.Under Pillar III: Facilitating Trade, the DPL aims to streamline border clearance processes, introduce risk management for food products, and reform the port of Durres to increase trade volumes.Specific success criteria for this pillar are: (1) a 15 percent reduction in imported cargo physically inspected by NFA inspectors at border inspection points, (2) the annual value of imports under local clearance procedures reaching $70 million, and (3) a 20 percent reduction in cargo dwell time at the port of Durres.Overall success of the DPL also depends on broader economic growth, with projections of Albania's growth rising to 3.2 percent in 2016 and 3.5 percent in 2017. The DPL's reforms are expected to contribute significantly to improving fiscal balances and sustaining high revenue collections as part of ongoing fiscal consolidation. Crucially, the medium-term economic recovery and the potential success of the DPL are heavily dependent on the pace and depth of additional structural reforms in the energy and financial sectors, as well as improvements in public investment management.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The external context for the Albania Competitiveness Development Policy Loan (DPL) presents a mixed picture, with several factors influencing its probability of overall success. Albania's attainment of EU candidate status in June 2014, following extensive reforms, provides a strong political anchor and external driver for the DPL-supported reforms, indicating a sustained commitment to integration and reform. The government has demonstrated strong commitment to the DPL-supported reform program. Economically, after a period of stabilization since 2013 through fiscal and financial sector reforms, Albania's GDP growth is projected to rise to 3.2% in 2016 and 3.5% in 2017. However, the country faces a critical need to accelerate growth and create jobs, particularly in the private sector, to address stagnating poverty levels (around 14%) and high youth and women unemployment. The economy needs to diversify its growth sources beyond consumption and construction by integrating more effectively into the global economy, as evidenced by low export levels (35% of GDP) compared to high-income small countries. Fiscal stability has improved since 2013, with fiscal results in H1 2016 on track, but public debt remains high at an estimated 72.5% of GDP in 2016, exposing Albania to interest rate risks. Although a new organic budget law mandates a decline to 45% of GDP, and Standard & Poor's confirmed a 'B+' credit rating, this high debt level remains a significant external vulnerability. The financial sector, while resilient to global crises, is constrained by low credit portfolio quality and high Non-Performing Loans (NPLs), which peaked at 25% in 2014 and stood at 20% in H2 2016, limiting credit expansion, especially to businesses. Albania's external position is vulnerable due to declining remittances (from 10.8% of GDP in 2009 to 6.9% in 2015) and volatile Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), which fell by 23% in H1 2016. A major external economic vulnerability is the high concentration of exports, both in terms of destination (Italy accounts for 50%, top five for 70%) and product composition (minerals constitute almost 50%), making the economy highly susceptible to country-specific and product-specific shocks. The DPL's success is heavily dependent on the pace and depth of additional structural reforms in the energy and financial sectors, and improvements in public investment management. However, it benefits from being part of a coherent reform package, complemented by two other World Bank policy-based interventions (Financial Sector DPL II and Public Finance Policy-Based Guarantee), and extensive World Bank Group technical assistance, indicating strong institutional support and readiness for reform implementation.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The DPL has a "substantial" risk rating, involving extensive legislative, institutional, and administrative reforms across three pillars: investment regime, ease of doing business (construction permits, electricity, licensing, quality infrastructure), and trade facilitation (border clearance, port reforms). This requires significant legal, regulatory, and operational changes, leveraging extensive technical assistance and depending on broader structural reforms in energy and finance, indicating high complexity.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This DPL is a "stand-alone Development Policy Lending operation" but is explicitly "complementary to two other policy-based interventions (Financial Sector DPL II, Public Finance Policy-Based Guarantee)." These three form a "coherent package to address critical growth and distributional issues." Thus, it's cohesively integrated into a larger, overarching strategic program of economic and structural reforms for Albania, not an isolated activity.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Albania Competitiveness DPL is an IBRD Loan of EUR 65.8 million, provided as general budget support, not allocated to specific sub-activities. Sub-activities include: (I) Investment regime, investor grievance, law consolidation; (II) Construction permits, electricity, business licensing, quality infra, product inspections; (III) Border clearance, food risk, Durres port reform. No specific sub-activities are noted as under-funded; Albania faces fiscal constraints and PFM weaknesses. Financing is substantial (EUR 65.8M DPL, plus >EUR 700M from other DPLs/IMF); external financing sustainable, gov committed. Success hinges on structural reform pace and improved public investment management."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: Implementing organizations (Ministries of Finance, Economic Dev., Urban Dev.) demonstrate strong commitment and abilities through completed prior actions, establishment of the Investor Grievance Mechanism Secretariat, inter-ministerial working groups, and adoption of legal frameworks for construction permits and risk management. Past success includes significant improvement in Doing Business rankings. High skin-in-the-game is shown by the Prime Minister heading a High-Level Council for grievances. Aiding stakeholders: World Bank (TA, monitoring), EBRD (Business Ombudsman), EU, USAID, GIZ. No specific deteriorating stakeholders are noted, only systemic investment climate bottlenecks.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- The DPL's success is vulnerable to high public debt (72.5% of GDP in 2016 with 55% floating interest) exposing Albania to interest rate risks and a structural current account deficit primarily financed by subdued remittances. It also faces risks from delays in fiscal consolidation, changes in exchange rates, and slower-than-projected GDP growth.
- Potential success is heavily dependent on the pace and depth of additional structural reforms in the energy and financial sectors, improvements in public investment management, and effectively addressing persistent investment climate bottlenecks, particularly in business regulation design and enforcement, which currently constrain private sector development and investment.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The DPL benefits from joint development and extensive technical assistance from the World Bank Group, which will also monitor and support future reforms. It is part of a coherent package of three complementary policy-based interventions. The government shows strong commitment, accelerating structural reforms and conducting public outreach. Positive economic growth is projected, and the DPL directly addresses primary constraints to private sector development. Reforms are expected to improve fiscal balances. Close World Bank engagement and institutional support are present. Macroeconomic risks are mitigated by a prudent fiscal consolidation path, growth-enhancing structural reforms, and significant financial support from the World Bank, IMF, and EU. Institutional capacity risks are addressed by targeting areas with strong commitment and providing complementary technical assistance, alongside wide stakeholder consultation and coordination with development partners."
