ACTIVITY TITLE: Guinea Urban Water Project
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2016-06-23
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-03-29

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 30,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: GN 100.00% | Location: Grandes Chutes; Conakry; Matoto Sub-Prefecture; Ratoma Sub-Prefecture; Dixinn Sub-Prefecture; Matam Sub-Prefecture; Kaloum

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 1717

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Guinea Urban Water Project, with a total project cost of US$30.00 million, is entirely financed by an IDA Grant, resulting in no financing gap. Expected disbursements are US$4.00 million in fiscal year 2018, US$10.00 million in 2019, US$10.00 million in 2020, US$4.00 million in 2021, and US$2.00 million in 2022, reaching a cumulative US$30.00 million by 2022. The project's lead practice area is Water, supported by the Social, Urban, Rural and Resilience Global Practice. The objectives are to increase access to improved water services in the Greater Conakry area and enhance the operational efficiency of the urban water utility. This intervention is critical for addressing high WASH-related disease rates in Guinea, aiming to reduce exposure to unsafe drinking water and hygiene, lower health-related costs, reduce missed work hours, and improve school attendance, particularly benefiting females who are disproportionately responsible for water fetching. The project aligns with the World Bank's Country Partnership Strategy 2013–2017's Human Development pillar and Guinea's socioeconomic development plan 2016–2020, which targets increased access to improved water supply from 61% to 75.6% and sanitation from 30% to 38%. The project's beneficiaries include people in Conakry's metropolitan and periphery areas who will gain more connections, increased water supply hours, adequate pressure levels, fewer interruptions, and closer proximity to water sources. The utility (SEG), the Government of Guinea (GoG), and the City of Conakry will also benefit from capacity building and support for reforms. The expected outcomes include at least 730,000 direct beneficiaries (51.7% female) with improved services and an improvement in SEG's operational efficiency, measured by a working ratio under 1, though a precise baseline is currently unavailable and may be adjusted at midterm review. The project's progress will be measured by the number of people in urban areas provided with access to improved water sources and enhanced water supply services, and the water utility's working ratio. The project consists of four components: Urban Water (US$26.2 million) for increasing water production and rehabilitating distribution networks; Urban Sanitation (US$1.0 million) for a citywide baseline survey, sanitation strategy, and knowledge exchange; Institutional Strengthening and Project Management (US$2.8 million) for supporting water sector institutions, urban water sector reform, and a Project Implementation Unit (PIU); and a Contingent Emergency Response (US$0.0 million). Key risks are rated Substantial for Political and Governance, Macroeconomic, Sector Strategies and Policies, Technical Design, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Overall risk. Fiduciary risk is rated High, while Environment and Social, Stakeholders, and Climate Change and Disaster risks are Moderate. The project meets regional criteria for readiness for implementation. A crucial point for forecasting outcomes is the initial budget constraint, which precludes funding for an SEG management contract and sanitation infrastructure rehabilitation. However, additional IDA financing is envisaged, conditional on the project meeting required implementation criteria, suggesting that full project impact is contingent on early performance. The acknowledgment of institutional capacity risks at MEH and SEG, and the plan to mitigate them through a robust PIU with performance-based contract renewals, highlight areas requiring diligent monitoring. The legal covenant mandates the preparation and adoption of a Project Implementation Manual within three months of the effective date to define governance, implementation, financial management, and procurement procedures."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The activity targets increasing access to improved water services in Greater Conakry and enhancing the urban water utility (SEG)'s operational efficiency. Expected outcomes include at least 730,000 direct beneficiaries (51.7% female) with enhanced water supply services, and 20,000 with access to improved water sources by FY22. SEG's operational efficiency will improve, aiming for a working ratio under 1, and Non-Revenue Water will reduce from 42% to 30%. Water production capacity is targeted to increase by 86,000 m³/day. The project also aims to reduce WASH-related diseases, lower health costs, reduce missed work hours, and improve school attendance, especially for females."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "Guinea faces substantial external challenges for success: a low GDP per capita ($1717), negative economic impacts from the Ebola epidemic and falling commodity prices, rapid urbanization (2.7% annual growth) overwhelming infrastructure, and weak water/sanitation institutions. Political & Governance, Macroeconomic, Sector Strategies, and Institutional Capacity are rated substantial risks. While aligning with GoG recovery plans and benefiting from abundant water resources, success hinges on addressing these systemic weaknesses and securing future IDA financing."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The Guinea Urban Water Project faces substantial technical and institutional complexities, aiming to increase water production, rehabilitate extensive distribution networks, and improve operational efficiency for a utility with high unaccounted water. Institutional capacity, fiduciary, and overall risks are rated high, with initial budget constraints requiring diligent monitoring.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The Guinea Urban Water Project is an independent project with distinct components, entirely financed by an IDA Grant. It is cohesively integrated into larger national and World Bank strategic frameworks, aligning with Guinea's socioeconomic development plan and the World Bank's Country Partnership Strategy, addressing critical water and sanitation needs.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Guinea Urban Water Project (US$30M) is IDA Grant-financed, with no initial gap. Activities: Urban Water (US$26.2M) for production/network; Urban Sanitation (US$1.0M) for surveys/strategy; Institutional Strengthening/Project Management (US$2.8M); Contingent Emergency Response (US$0.0M). Initial budget excludes SEG management contract/sanitation infrastructure; full impact requires additional, conditional IDA financing. Consumption aims for 85 L/capita/day (current 57 L). Non-revenue water targeted from 42% to 30%. Maintenance cost 3% of investment. Financially viable, but high fiduciary, procurement, and overall risks challenge full success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: "The Guinea Urban Water Project involves the Guinea Water Company (SEG) and the National Directorate of Territory Administration, Urban Planning, Roads and Infrastructures (DATU) as implementing partners, with the Ministry of Energy, Hydraulics and Hydrocarbons (MEH) as the overall coordinator and accountable organization. The project explicitly states the MEH and SEG have \"limited capacity\" and identifies \"Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability\" as a substantial risk. Fiduciary risk is rated high. To mitigate these, a robust Project Implementation Unit (PIU) will be established within the MEH, with key staff on performance-based contract renewals, providing a direct incentive for individual performance. Technical assistance (AMO) will reinforce MEH and SEG capacity, especially for the critical urban water supply component (87% of the project), and twinning arrangements with regional utilities are planned for knowledge transfer. The project is 100% IDA Grant-financed, meaning no direct financial contribution from the Government of Guinea (GoG) in the initial budget. However, additional IDA financing for an SEG management contract and sanitation infrastructure is \"conditional on the project meeting required implementation criteria,\" providing a strong incentive for implementers to perform well to unlock further funds, acting as a form of skin-in-the-game. The project's goals of improving SEG's operational and financial sustainability by reducing reliance on subsidies also reflect a long-term investment by SEG in its own improved performance. Other stakeholders aiding outcomes include the World Bank providing extensive implementation support and technical assistance (PPIAF), and coordination with other active donors in the sector (JICA, IsDB, Arab Bank, Kingdom of Morocco). Citizen engagement mechanisms are also built in to ensure accountability. Factors that could deteriorate outcomes include substantial risks related to political and governance, macroeconomic conditions, sector strategies, technical design, and the initial budget constraint precluding a full management contract for SEG and sanitation infrastructure rehabilitation, which limits immediate impact."
ACTIVITY RISKS: "Overall risk is Substantial, with Fiduciary risk rated High. Key risks include institutional capacity weaknesses at MEH and SEG (unfamiliarity with World Bank procurement, lack of SEG procurement department, expired SEG performance contract, weak AREEG capacity). An initial budget constraint prevents funding for an SEG management contract and sanitation infrastructure rehabilitation, making full project impact contingent on conditional additional IDA financing. Financial viability is at risk from a 30% increase in production/treatment costs or a 30% deviation from NRW targets. There is a significant urban water supply deficit (141,000 m³/day) and inadequate pressure levels. Other risks are the lack of a reliable urban water information system, limited groundwater data, and the legal covenant requiring a Project Implementation Manual within three months."

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Full IDA grant funding eliminates financing gap. Project is economically and financially viable (FIRR 51%, EIRR 30%), with clear objectives to increase water access for 730,000 beneficiaries and enhance utility efficiency, addressing critical WASH-related diseases. Strong government political will for water sector reform and strategic alignment with national/World Bank plans. Comprehensive risk mitigation includes robust PIU staffing, training, implementation manuals, twinning, and World Bank oversight for institutional, procurement, and fiduciary risks. Benefits are conservatively estimated, suggesting greater actual impact."

The total project cost is US$60 million, with a financing gap of zero, indicating a borrower contribution of US$12 million. The Project Appraisal Document was issued on January 30, 2018.

The project's implementation is slated to begin on February 28, 2018, with an expected effectiveness date of May 25, 2018, and an expected closing and implementation end date of June 30, 2023. The fiscal year for the project runs from April 1 to March 31. The lead practice area for this project is Environment & Natural Resources, with contributing practice areas including Agriculture, Gender, Macroeconomics, Trade and Investment. The project's development objective is to strengthen community-led landscapes management in selected landscapes in the state of Meghalaya.

The project is structured around three main components: "Strengthening Knowledge and Capacity for Natural Resource Management" budgeted at US$13.00 million, "Community-led Landscape Planning and Implementation" at US$30.00 million, and "Project Management and Governance" at US$5.00 million. The overall risk rating for the project is 'Substantial', with specific 'Substantial' ratings for Fiduciary and Environment and Social risks, while Political and Governance, Macroeconomic, Sector Strategies and Policies, Technical Design of Project or Program, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Stakeholders are all rated 'Moderate'.

The Government of Meghalaya is the responsible agency, with the Meghalaya Basin Management Agency (MBMA) vested with the overall responsibility for implementation. Key institutional arrangements include the MBMA maintaining a State Project Management Unit (SPMU) led by a Project Director, District Project Management Units (DPMUs) within each district, and Block Project Management Units (BPMUs) to support villages. The project also requires the constitution of Village Natural Resource Management Committees (VNRMCs) in selected villages. A critical condition for disbursement of Village Grants under sub-components 2A and 2B is the preparation and adoption of a Community Operations Manual by the MBMA. Other important legal covenants include continuous monitoring, reporting, evaluation, and the maintenance of a multi-layered grievance redress mechanism. The project triggers several safeguard policies, including Environmental Assessment (OP/BP 4.01), Natural Habitats (OP/BP 4.04), Forests (OP/BP 4.36), Physical Cultural Resources (OP/BP 4.11), and Indigenous Peoples (OP/BP 4.10), necessitating adherence to an Environmental Management Framework (EMF) and Social Management Framework (SMF). ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Myanmar Flood and Landslide Emergency Recovery Project (P158194) is an Investment Project Financing activity designed to support recovery in priority areas affected by the 2015 floods and landslides in Myanmar, and to provide immediate and effective response in the event of another eligible crisis or emergency. The project, with an overall cost of US$200 million, is financed by two International Development Association (IDA) credits, each for SDR 71.3 million (US$100 million equivalent), with one credit drawing from IDA Crisis Response Window Resources. The Republic of the Union of Myanmar is the borrower, with the Ministry of Agriculture, Livestock and Irrigation (MOALI) and the Ministry of Construction (MOC) serving as responsible agencies. The project's implementation began on July 14, 2016, with an expected effectiveness date of October 14, 2016, and is slated to conclude on June 30, 2021, with a closing date of December 31, 2021.

The project is structured around five components: Resilient Rehabilitation of National Roads (US$105.00 million), Resilient Rehabilitation of Rural Roads and Livelihoods Support (US$70.00 million), Provision of Eligible Goods (US$15.00 million), Project Management and Knowledge Support (US$10.00 million), and a Contingent Emergency Response component with zero initial allocation. Led by the Social, Urban, Rural and Resilience Global Practice, with contributions from Agriculture, Transport & ICT, the project addresses cross-cutting topics such as Climate Change, Fragile, Conflict & Violence, Gender, and Jobs. Key sectors include Health and other social services (20%), Transportation (45%), and Water, sanitation and flood protection (35%), with major themes centered on social protection and risk management (natural disaster management) and rural development (non-farm income generation and rural services and infrastructure).

For forecasting overall activity success, several factors are noteworthy. The project's overall risk rating is Substantial. Specific risks rated High include Political and Governance, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Fiduciary aspects. Substantial risks also apply to Technical Design of Project or Program, Stakeholders, and Disaster and Climate, while Macroeconomic, Sector Strategies and Policies, and Environment and Social risks are rated Moderate. Given these high and substantial risk ratings, careful attention to institutional capacity, governance, and financial management will be crucial for success. The project triggers several safeguard policies including Environmental Assessment, Natural Habitats, Forests, Physical Cultural Resources, Indigenous Peoples, and Involuntary Resettlement, necessitating continuous adherence to environmental and social safeguard provisions and reporting. Legal covenants mandate continuous maintenance of project implementation support teams, adherence to the Project Operational Manual, and yearly submission of annual work plans and budgets. Disbursement conditions for the Contingent Emergency Response component require the occurrence of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency and the completion of related safeguards, while other components require the prior adoption of the Project Operational Manual. The project team is led by Henrike Brecht, Michael Bonte-Grapentin, and Zuzana Stanton-Geddes.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The targeted outcomes are to support recovery in priority areas affected by the 2015 floods and landslides in Myanmar, and to provide immediate and effective response in the event of another eligible crisis or emergency. Success would require careful attention to institutional capacity, governance, and financial management, continuous adherence to environmental and social safeguard provisions, maintenance of project implementation support teams, adherence to the Project Operational Manual, and timely submission of annual work plans and budgets.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The external context for the Myanmar Flood and Landslide Emergency Recovery Project is characterized by a high susceptibility to natural disasters, particularly floods and landslides, as evidenced by the 2015 events and a Substantial 'Disaster and Climate' risk. The project operates within a 'Fragile and/or Capacity Constraints' environment, facing a High risk from 'Political and Governance' issues, indicating potential instability or governance challenges. Macroeconomic and Sector Strategies and Policies present a Moderate risk, while Stakeholder dynamics pose a Substantial risk. ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The activity, titled "Strengthening Critical Infrastructure against Natural Hazards," is designed to bolster the Recipient's disaster risk management capacities, enhance the resilience of its critical infrastructure against natural hazards, and improve its overall disaster response capability. These objectives will be achieved by fostering a deeper understanding of disaster risks, promoting disaster risk-informed planning, guiding the design and reconstruction of critical infrastructure—including bridges, flood protection, and riverbank erosion-prevention infrastructure—and enhancing the recipient's ability to respond promptly and effectively to emergencies. This initiative serves as a foundational element for the Government of Tajikistan's (GoT) long-term disaster risk management (DRM) program, responding to a high demand for comprehensive DRM support and critical infrastructure reconstruction under the "building back better" principle. Initially, the project targets areas in GBAO and the Khatlon Oblast, which were severely impacted by floods and mudflows in July 2015, by financing infrastructure reconstruction to reduce long-term disaster risks given the region's exposure to various natural hazards. Ultimately, it aims to strengthen the GoT's capacity to address a broad spectrum of countrywide disaster risks, including floods, mudslides, rock falls, avalanches, landslides, and earthquakes.

The project carries a total cost of 50.00 USD Million, entirely funded by bank financing with no financing gap. This funding comprises 25.00 USD Million from the International Development Association (IDA) and an additional 25.00 USD Million from an IDA Grant, totaling 50.00 USD Million. Expected cumulative disbursements are planned over several fiscal years: 0.00 USD Million in 2017, 2.38 USD Million in 2018, 6.86 USD Million in 2019, 14.79 USD Million in 2020, 26.82 USD Million in 2021, 39.20 USD Million in 2022, and reaching the full 50.00 USD Million in 2023 and 2024. The project's lead institutional oversight falls under the Social, Urban, Rural and Resilience Global Practice, with contributing support from the Agriculture, Climate Change, and Transport & ICT practice areas.

The project is structured into four main components. Component 1, "Strengthening Disaster Risk Management Capacity," is allocated 4.00 USD Million and aims to enhance the GoT's DRM capabilities through targeted activities in disaster risk identification, preparedness, and financial protection. This component includes strengthening the capacity of the Committee of Emergency Situations and Civil Defense (CoESCD) for crisis management and emergency response, the Institute of Seismology, Earthquake Engineering, and Engineering Seismology (IoSEE) for understanding seismic hazards in Dushanbe, and the Ministry of Finance (MoF) for strategizing financial responses to disasters by combining and improving various ex-ante financial instruments. This component will be implemented in coordination with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP). A specific subcomponent, "Modernizing the Crisis Management Centers and Systems for Improved Disaster Preparedness," will improve and modernize the country's crisis management system, including Crisis Management Centers (CMCs) and unified emergency communication systems, to enhance hazard monitoring, interagency coordination, implement international best practices, issue timely early warnings, decentralize the system, and reduce emergency response times. This subcomponent will finance the necessary work to build or renovate a facility for the national CMC, along with capacity building for its sustainable operation and maintenance. The largest component, Component 2, "Making Critical Infrastructure Resilient against Natural Hazards," is budgeted at 38.00 USD Million. Component 3, "Contingent Emergency Response Component," has an allocation of 6.00 USD Million, and Component 4, "Project Management," is assigned 2.00 USD Million.

The project is anticipated to benefit approximately 650,000 people living and working in districts where infrastructure subprojects will be implemented. An estimated 260,000 people annually affected by emergencies will indirectly benefit from improved emergency response services delivered through modernized CMCs and systems. Direct beneficiaries include roughly 110,000 people across thirty-seven villages in the Khatlon Oblast, who will benefit from flood protection and riverbank erosion-prevention infrastructure, and approximately 12,000 people in twenty-three villages in GBAO, where bridges will be strengthened. The project will also contribute significantly to the restoration of the M41 route, which is the sole access to Khorugh, the capital of GBAO (home to around 30,000 people), and a vital China–Tajikistan trade route, thereby enhancing the country's overall capacity to respond to natural disasters. Approximately 51 percent of the beneficiaries are expected to be women and girls. Furthermore, key institutional stakeholders, including representatives from the CoESCD, MoF, Ministry of Transport (MoT), Agency for Land Reclamation and Irrigation (ALRI), and IoSEE, will receive technical and institutional capacity building in planning, implementation, and monitoring. Gender-specific indicators will be collected throughout project implementation, with updated social assessment data incorporating gender-disaggregated information on project-affected populations, and an examination of the link between road usage, flood management, and gender to mainstream gender considerations into natural hazard resilience efforts.

Progress toward the development objectives will be measured quarterly using several key performance indicators. These include the operationalization of crisis management centers and systems, with the national crisis management center and mobile command and communication vehicles fully functional as regional/local centers for hazard monitoring, communication, and emergency response decision-making. Other indicators involve an improved understanding of seismic hazards through systematically updated seismic hazard maps, the number of bridges reconstructed or strengthened based on multi-hazard disaster and climate change risk designs (encompassing floods, landslides, mud flows, rock falls, erosion, earthquakes, and climate change scenarios), the number of people whose disaster risks are reduced by more resilient flood protection and riverbank erosion-prevention infrastructure, and an overall strengthened capacity to coordinate and respond to emergencies. Data for these indicators will be gathered through Project Progress Reports, with various Project Implementation Units (PIUs) from the MoF, MoT, ALRI, CoESCD, and IoSEE responsible for data collection. This activity is well-aligned with the Country Partnership Strategy's (CPS) cross-cutting theme of climate change, specifically in enhancing disaster risk management. Information useful for forecasting overall activity success includes clear and detailed objectives, specified components with significant budget allocation to infrastructure resilience, identification of specific target areas and beneficiaries including a gender breakdown, robust institutional capacity-building efforts for various government entities, established partnerships and coordination with organizations like UNDP, adherence to a "building back better" principle, a focus on addressing systemic issues such as risk identification and financial protection, comprehensive consideration of multi-hazard and climate change risks in designs, the presence of detailed and measurable PDO-level indicators with defined monitoring responsibilities, explicit commitment to gender mainstreaming, full financing with no gaps, and a phased disbursement schedule.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: ["The activity aims to strengthen the Recipient's disaster risk management (DRM) capacities, enhance critical infrastructure resilience against natural hazards, and improve overall disaster response. This involves fostering a deeper understanding of disaster risks, promoting risk-informed planning, and guiding resilient design/reconstruction of critical infrastructure (bridges, flood protection, riverbank erosion-prevention). It will also modernize crisis management centers/systems for improved hazard monitoring, interagency coordination, timely early warnings, and reduced emergency response times. Capacity building for CoESCD, MoF, MoT, ALRI, and IoSEE is key. It seeks to benefit approximately 650,000 people, directly impacting 122,000 in Khatlon/GBAO, with 51% women/girls.", "Overall success requires crisis management centers and systems to be 100% operational, including the national CMC and mobile command vehicles, functioning for hazard monitoring and emergency response. It also requires updated seismic hazard maps and improved understanding of seismic risks (via national assessment and Dushanbe micro-zoning). Success is further measured by the number of bridges reconstructed/strengthened based on multi-hazard/climate risk designs, and the number of people whose disaster risks are reduced by resilient flood protection/erosion-prevention infrastructure. A strengthened capacity to coordinate and respond to emergencies, effective institutional capacity building for government entities, and mainstreamed gender considerations are crucial. Full financing and adherence to 'building back better' are foundational."]

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The probability of overall success for the "Strengthening Critical Infrastructure against Natural Hazards" activity is significantly influenced by several external contextual factors. Tajikistan ranks as the most vulnerable country to climate change in the Europe and Central Asia Region, with low adaptive capacity. The country is highly prone to numerous natural hazards including floods, earthquakes, landslides, mudflows, avalanches, droughts, and heavy snowfalls, whose frequency and severity are projected to increase due to climate change (e.g., mean annual temperatures projected to be 2

C warmer by 2050, increased dry days, stronger glacier melting, more frequent droughts/floods/heat/cold waves). Past disasters (1992-2016) caused over US$1.8 billion in economic losses and affected almost 7 million people, with projected annual losses from floods at 1.4% of GDP and earthquakes at 5% of GDP. Major events could lead to losses of 7.6% (flood) to 34% (earthquake) of GDP, highlighting the immense scale of the risks. The country's economy is vulnerable to external shocks, with growing government debt (41.4% of GDP in 2016) and "scarce budgetary resources" for competing development projects and ex-ante risk reduction, potentially hindering sustained long-term investment. High poverty rates persist, especially in target regions like Khatlon Oblast and GBAO (among the poorest), where populations are highly dependent on vulnerable land/water resources. Tajikistan is Central Asia's least accessible and isolated country, with aging, Soviet-era infrastructure suffering from insufficient maintenance and repeated hazard exposure. Critical routes, such as the M41 in GBAO, are frequently disrupted by hazards, impacting connectivity and trade. On the positive side, the Government of Tajikistan (GoT) is transitioning from disaster response to risk mitigation, has established a clear institutional framework (CoESCD, SCES) for DRM, and collaborates with international partners (UNDP, REACT). The GoT's adoption of the Sendai Framework (2015) and a National Disaster Risk Management Strategy (2010-15) signals political will and a supportive policy environment, which are crucial for the project's implementation and long-term success.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity exhibits significant technical and implementation complexity due to its multi-faceted approach to disaster risk management. It involves strengthening DRM capacities, enhancing critical infrastructure resilience against multiple natural hazards (floods, mudslides, rock falls, avalanches, landslides, earthquakes, climate change), and improving emergency response. ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is not an independent or isolated program; it is directly and cohesively integrated into a larger program. It is explicitly described as serving "as a foundational element for the Government of Tajikistan's (GoT) long-term disaster risk management (DRM) program." The GoT's long-term disaster risk management (DRM) program itself can be considered a larger program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The activity has four components: 1. Strengthening Disaster Risk Management Capacity ($4.00M), includes enhancing DRM capabilities, CoESCD, IoSEE, MoF, and modernizing Crisis Management Centers. 2. Making Critical Infrastructure Resilient against Natural Hazards ($38.00M), includes $19.00M for Strengthening Bridges and $19.00M for Flood Protection/Riverbank Erosion-Prevention Infrastructure. 3. Contingent Emergency Response ($6.00M). 4. Project Management ($2.00M), covering operational costs, administration, procurement, M&E. The total cost is $50.00M, fully funded by IDA ($25.00M Credit, $25.00M Grant) with a zero financing gap. No under-funded aspects. The activity is very well-financed, with robust support, clear disbursements, and flexible funding, positively impacting success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The activity will be implemented by a consortium of Government of Tajikistan (GoT) entities, spearheaded by the Ministry of Finance (MoF) Project Implementation Unit (PIU), which holds overall responsibility for financial management, disbursement, and consolidation of reports. The MoF PIU will directly implement Component 1 (Strengthening Disaster Risk Management Capacity) and Component 3 (Contingent Emergency Response Component), receiving technical inputs and supervision from the Committee of Emergency Situations and Civil Defense (CoESCD) and the Institute of Seismology, Earthquake Engineering, and Engineering Seismology (IoSEE). The Agency for Land Reclamation & Irrigation (ALRI) Project Management Unit (PMU) and the Ministry of Transport (MoT) Project Implementation Group (PIG) will implement subcomponents of Component 2 (Making Critical Infrastructure Resilient against Natural Hazards), focusing on flood protection and transportation infrastructure, respectively. Both ALRI and MoT units will manage safeguards, procurement, contract signing, technical supervision, and contract management for their assigned areas.The GoT demonstrates strong investment, framing the activity as a "a foundational element for the Government of Tajikistan's (GoT) long-term disaster risk management (DRM) program," indicating high demand and political will. The Minister of Finance serving as the "Recipient's Representative" further underscores high-level commitment. Staff from the MoF PIU, MoT PIG, and ALRI PMU have acquired experience under existing World Bank projects, suggesting a baseline of capabilities and established processes for handling international development initiatives. The activity also explicitly includes "robust institutional capacity-building efforts" for all key implementing entities (CoESCD, MoF, MoT, ALRI, IoSEE) in planning, implementation, and monitoring, signaling a commitment to enhance long-term organizational abilities.While the financial management (FM) assessment identified inadequate staffing numbers and a need to modify accounting systems, these are acknowledged and planned to be addressed through the contracting of dedicated FM consultants and building controls, mitigating potential risks.Other relevant stakeholders significantly aiding activity outcomes include the International Development Association (IDA), providing full funding (USD 50.00 Million) with no financing gap, and the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), which will coordinate with the MoF PIU on Component 1 activities, bringing external expertise. There is no information suggesting stakeholders that would deteriorate activity outcomes. ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Malawi Agricultural Commercialization Project (P158434), supported by The World Bank as an Investment Project Financing instrument, was approved on May 23, 2017, and is set to close on May 31, 2023, with an Environmental Assessment Category of B - Partial Assessment. This project involves complementary or interdependent Bank/IFC collaboration, requiring active coordination. The Project Development Objective is to increase the commercialization of agriculture value chain products selected under the project, with the Ministry of Finance, Economic Planning and Development serving as the borrower for Malawi.

The project is structured around four main components: Component 1: Building Productive Alliances, allocated US$62.00 million; Component 2: Support Investment Enabling Services, with US$23.00 million; Component 3: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC), with US$0.00 million; and Component 4: Project Coordination and Management, receiving US$10.00 million.

The project's strategic approach focuses on enabling smallholder producer organizations to market farm and agribusiness products to address significant rural poverty and high subsistence farming in Malawi. It aims to improve productivity, develop market linkages, and integrate small-scale and emerging producers with off-takers through Productive Alliances and the provision of infrastructure and services. The initiative will establish a PCG Fund to enhance producers' access to finance for investments in technologies, including climate-smart agriculture, and create a conducive environment for agribusinesses to participate in value chains. Key interventions include investing in smallholder productivity and market access improvements, enhancing agricultural productivity and agribusiness-related infrastructure and services, improving access to agricultural finance, and promoting the adoption of climate-resilient agricultural technologies. This is expected to lead to increased capacity of smallholder producer groups, improved agricultural technologies, better access to finance, and improved agribusiness regulations and services, ultimately resulting in farmers integrated into competitive supply chains, diversified commercial agriculture, a conducive agri-business environment, and resilient farming systems.

The project addresses critical challenges in Malawi, where agricultural production often fails to meet domestic and export demands due to low productivity, limited use of improved agricultural technologies, poor access to credit and markets, and inadequate marketing infrastructure. Constraints also include unorganized farmers, weak business and investment climate, poor utility infrastructure, and climate change risks, all contributing to high levels of subsistence farming. The rationale for this project stems from the need to transform subsistence agriculture into diversified commercial agriculture, enhancing food security, reducing poverty, and promoting shared prosperity through broad-based agricultural growth.

Several performance indicators are used to track the project's progress. While some indicators like "Increase in Yield of commodities by supported Producer Organisations" and "Producer groups that meet market specifications defined by off-takers" were marked for deletion, and "Value of gross sales from agriculture value chain products supported by the Project" was also marked for deletion, others have been introduced or revised. A new indicator, "Increase in sales of producer organizations within productive alliances," starts at a baseline of 0.00 metric tons/year, with an intermediate target of 3,000.00 and an end target of 15,000.00, reflecting an increased level of commercialization. Another new indicator tracks "Beneficiaries of the warehouse receipt system," aiming to increase from a baseline of 3,511.00 to an end target of 4,100.00 beneficiaries. Additionally, the project aims for "Number of smallholder households with secure land tenure" to rise from a 0.00 baseline to an end target of 12,000.00. Under Component 4, revised indicators include "Smallholders/farmers/beneficiaries satisfied with services provided by the project," with a baseline of 0.00% and an end target of 80.00%, and specifically "Female farmers satisfied with services provided by the project," also targeting 80.00% satisfaction from a 0.00% baseline. The project also aims to reach 40,000.00 farmers with agricultural assets and services from a baseline of 0.00. The emphasis on new and revised indicators, particularly those directly reflecting commercialization, satisfaction, and reach, provides a clearer framework for forecasting project success in achieving its development objective.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: ["Targeted outcomes aim to increase agricultural commercialization. Key targets include: 15,000 MT/year increase in producer organization sales, US$10M supplemental sales, 5 value chain products with >50% commercialization increase, 40,000 farmers reached with assets/services (incl. 16,000 female farmers), 391,000 emergency response beneficiaries, 50% female farmer satisfaction, 20 agribusiness reforms, and 12,000 smallholder households with secure land tenure. This should lead to farmers in competitive supply chains, diversified commercial agriculture, a conducive agribusiness environment, and resilient farming systems.", "Overall success requires achieving the Project Development Objective to increase commercialization of agriculture value chain products. This means meeting or exceeding all specific end targets for sales, farmer reach, satisfaction, reforms, and emergency response beneficiaries. Furthermore, success involves transforming subsistence agriculture into diversified commercial agriculture, enhancing food security, reducing poverty, and promoting shared prosperity through broad-based agricultural growth, reflecting farmers integrated into competitive supply chains with resilient systems."]

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: - Malawi faces high macroeconomic (volatile growth, high inflation, currency depreciation, PFM irregularities) and political/governance risks. Poverty is high, with prevalent subsistence farming. A weak investment climate, poor infrastructure, high interest rates, trade barriers, and severe climate vulnerability (drought, floods) are major external challenges. Significant gender and youth employment gaps persist. However, the project benefits from strong alignment with national policies (e.g., National Agriculture Policy, National Export Strategy, National Land Policy) and coordinated World Bank Group efforts, including new land legislation and climate-smart agriculture focus, providing a supportive policy framework.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is a large, comprehensive program with four main components, not an independent or isolated activity. It is structured as an Investment Project Financing instrument by The World Bank and involves complementary, interdependent Bank/IFC collaboration, requiring active coordination to address systemic agricultural commercialization in Malawi.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Malawi Agricultural Commercialization Project has a total budget of US$95.00 million, excluding physical and price contingencies of US$1.20 million and US$1.80 million respectively. The project is structured around four main components with detailed sub-activities and budgets:\n\nComponent 1: Building Productive Alliances (US$62.00 million)\n*   Subcomponent 1.1: Horizontal Alliances (PO Formation) - Budgeted at US$10.30 million. This activity focuses on organizing producer organizations.\n*   Subcomponent 1.2: Productive Alliances - Budgeted at US$33.30 million. This includes matching grant financing, technical assistance (TA), and credit to enable POs and off-takers to form alliances. Matching grants will cover up to 70% of investment costs, with the remainder expected to be self-financed by POs through cash, in-kind contributions, or financial institutions. The Project Partial Credit Guarantee Fund (PCGF) under Part 1.2(c)(i) is allocated SDR 1,700,000, designed to reduce risk for financial institutions lending to POs (originally stated as US$3 million for the PCG Fund in descriptive text). Matching Grants for PO Sub-projects under Part 1.2(b) are budgeted at SDR 17,100,000. Consulting services under Part 1.2(c)(ii) are allocated SDR 200,000.\n*   Subcomponent 1.3: Last-mile Infrastructure for Productive Alliances - Budgeted at US$18.40 million. This includes investments for improving access to electricity (capped at US$200,000 per subproject, generally US$10,000-20,000) through step-down transformers, low-voltage extension lines (up to 3km, max US$20,000, only if other alternatives exhausted), and off-grid solutions. It also covers investments for improving access to water for POs and PAs (connecting processing facilities to potable water, boreholes, submersible pumps, solar panels, and tanks). Last Mile Sub-projects under Part 1.3 are allocated SDR 10,600,000.\n\nComponent 2: Support Investment Enabling Services (US$23.00 million)\n*   Subcomponent 2.1: Access to Agricultural Financing - Budgeted at US$7.30 million. This will finance TA to strengthen the warehouse receipt system, assess challenges, identify engagement models for farmers/SMEs, and explore insurance/indemnity funds. Consulting services under Part 2.1 are allocated SDR 200,000.\n*   Subcomponent 2.2: Access to Land for Commercial Agriculture - Budgeted at US$7.30 million. This involves piloting new land laws to provide more secure land tenure.\n*   Subcomponent 2.3: Support for Business Enabling Services - Budgeted at US$8.40 million. This promotes dialogue between the government and the private sector to improve the investment and trade environment.\n\nComponent 3: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) (US$0.00 million)\n*   While the main project cost table shows US$0.00 million for this component, the disbursement table allocates a substantial SDR 14,000,000 for Emergency Expenditures, indicating a significant reserve for unforeseen events.\n\nComponent 4: Project Coordination and Management (US$10.00 million)\n*   This component covers project regional offices, social accountability mechanisms, corruption prevention, community awareness, internal and external audits, accounting, reporting, and overall project administration. A general category for Goods, works, non-consulting services, consulting services, Training and Operating Costs for Parts 1.1, 1.2(a), 1.2(c)(ii), 2.2, 2.3 and 4 of the Project is allocated SDR 24,500,000, which includes costs for this component.\n\nFunding and Financing Considerations:\n*   **Potential Under-funding:** A key aspect likely to be under-funded or challenging is the requirement for smallholder producer organizations (POs) to self-finance or secure external loans for at least 30% of their investment costs under the matching grant program. Given the target beneficiaries are often face \"poor access to credit and markets,\" this could hinder their ability to fully leverage the grants, especially since some subprojects needing productive inputs (seed, fertilizer) if POs lack funds. High interest rates charged by commercial banks for warehouse receipt financing could also deter farmers from participating, potentially limiting the success of the US$7.30 million allocated to \"Access to Agricultural Financing.\"\n*   **Financing Impact on Success:** The project's success is heavily reliant on the POs' capacity to meet their co-financing obligations. The PCG Fund aims to mitigate lending risks for financial institutions, which is crucial for improving access to finance for POs. However, the sustainability of the PCG Fund relies on fee structures that might affect its attractiveness. The significant allocation for Emergency Expenditures (SDR 14,000,000) provides a robust safety net for unforeseen events, which is critical for activity success in an agricultural project exposed to climate risks. The restrictive caps and conditions on financing for electricity and water infrastructure (e.g., US$20,000 cap for certain electricity investments, and only if other alternatives are exhausted) could limit the reach and impact of these last-mile interventions if other viable alternatives are not readily available or affordable."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The activity is primarily implemented by the Republic of Malawi through the Ministry of Agriculture, Irrigation, and Water Development (MoAIWD), in close collaboration with the Ministry of Industry, Trade, and Tourism (MoITT), Ministry of Finance and Economic Affairs, and Ministry of Trade. The Ministry of Finance, Economic Planning and Development serves as the borrower.A Project Implementation Unit (PIU) will be established under MoAIWD, staffed by experienced, competitively recruited professionals responsible for day-to-day oversight and coordination. A high-level Project Steering Committee (PSC), comprising representatives from numerous ministries (including MoAIWD, MoITT, Ministry of Lands, Housing and Urban Development, Ministry of Finance, Economic Planning, and Development) at the Principal Secretary level, along with representatives from the Malawi Confederation of Chambers of Commerce and Industry and Farmers Union of Malawi, will provide strategic and policy guidance. MoAIWD will chair the PSC, with MoITT as co-chair. A Project Technical Committee (PTC) provides technical oversight.The Malawian government demonstrates significant skin-in-the-game through several commitments: the obligation to provide funding, personnel, and other resources; the establishment and maintenance of the PIU, PSC, and PTC as foundational structures (PIU establishment is an effectiveness condition); the adoption of a Project Implementation Manual; and the active involvement of ministries in implementing new land laws and translating dialogue into policy reforms. The requirement for annual work plans and budgets with proposed financing plans further indicates financial commitment.Other relevant stakeholders are poised to aid activity outcomes through extensive collaboration. The multi-ministerial involvement at strategic and technical levels ensures broad governmental buy-in and coordination. Public-private dialogue (PPD) forums, agribusiness taskforces, and the inclusion of private sector entities like the Malawi Confederation of Chambers of Commerce and Industry, Farmers Union of Malawi, Financial Institutions, and Producer Organizations directly integrate market needs into project design and implementation. Capacity building support for various government entities (e.g., MoLHUD, MITC, MBS) also strengthens the enabling environment. However, the complexity of coordinating numerous actors and addressing existing capacity constraints within some entities or unorganized farmer groups could pose challenges, which the robust management structure and capacity building components aim to mitigate.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- Overall Project Risk is High.
- Institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability is substantial: Malawian institutions have low capacity to implement and coordinate the project among various entities, including sector ministries, state/non-state institutions, private agribusinesses, and producer organizations.
- Fiduciary risks are high: The main risks relate to weak financial management, particularly within the public sector.
- Environmental and social risks are moderate: These risks stem from the provision of infrastructure to support commercialization and adverse impacts on domestic agricultural production caused by weather.
- Stakeholder risks are moderate: Some stakeholders could oppose the project, and it may prove difficult to reach others, such as women and youth.
- Project Procurement Risk is Substantial.
- Political and governance risks are substantial: Political economy relationships may slow reforms to promote commercialization, especially for new entrants. The 2019 presidential and parliamentary elections could inject uncertainty into reform continuity.
- Macro-economic risks are high: A weak macroeconomic environment (inflation, exchange rate instability, and continued depreciation) and high costs of borrowing undermine private sector development.
- Sector strategies and policies risks are substantial: The key risk is that national policies and strategies for commercial agriculture will not be implemented; challenges exist in piloting the 2016 land acts to ensure tenure security for project beneficiaries.
- Technical design of the project risks are high: The project's complexity, involving coordination of multiple stakeholders and solutions across various sectors, makes it potentially challenging to implement. Residual risks for the PCG include failure to identify a suitable manager, underutilization by financial institutions, and financial unsustainability due to high claims.
- Existing agricultural sector challenges: Malawi faces low agricultural productivity, limited use of improved technologies, poor access to credit and markets, inadequate marketing infrastructure, unorganized farmers, and a weak business and investment climate.
- High trade costs: Trade in agricultural commodities is hampered by high tariffs, non-tariff barriers, regulatory costs, border challenges, and high transportation costs.
- Poor access to finance: The financial sector is small, offers a narrow range of products, and has very high interest rates (40-80% p.a. for commercial banks, microfinance, and informal banking systems).
- Gender and youth issues: Significant and costly gender gaps remain in agriculture, and high youth unemployment (15% in 2011) limits prospects and skills for jobs or entrepreneurship, particularly in rural areas.
- Climate-related production risks: Changing and more variable climate patterns, including later onset/cessation of rains, longer dry seasons, and shorter growing seasons, pose serious concerns for agricultural development.
- Other key risk factors: Significant returns are sensitive to key risk factors such as adoption rates, crop prices, and project delays.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The project demonstrates strong financial viability with an Economic Net Present Value of US$372 million, a benefit-cost ratio of 4.7, and an Economic Internal Rate of Return of 51 percent, indicating significant returns and potential to increase annual farm income by 40-100 percent. It includes robust last-mile infrastructure investments in irrigation, feeder roads, electricity, and clean water to ensure Productive Alliances (PAs) become sustainable, profitable, and less vulnerable to risks like drought. The design incorporates an independent PIU, qualified technical assistance, and a six-year timeframe for effective implementation. Mitigation measures address political, macroeconomic, and institutional risks through clear criteria, lower-interest loans, land tenure security piloting, and dedicated expert staff for financial management and safeguards. The project is expected to constitute a net carbon sink due to improved crop management and climate-smart agriculture practices, with moderate environmental and social impacts that are mitigable, supported by existing government safeguard capacity. Targeted efforts include a special window for youth and women POs/PAs, with preference in matching grants. Clear performance indicators track increased sales for producer organizations (target 15,000 metric tons/year), a rise in warehouse receipt system beneficiaries (target 4,100), and secure land tenure for 12,000 smallholder households, alongside high satisfaction rates (80%) for beneficiaries, including female farmers."

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "CI-DPO on Fiscal Management, Education and Energy Reforms" is the first single-tranche operation in a programmatic series of two consecutive Development Policy Operations (DPOs), financed by an IDA credit of EURO 68.5 million (US$75 million equivalent) on blend IDA terms, with a 25-year maturity including a 5-year grace period, implemented by the Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) with the Government of Côte d'Ivoire as the borrower. The Program Development Objectives (PDOs) are to support the Government's ability to (i) strengthen domestic resource mobilization and efficiency, (ii) enhance allocative efficiency of public spending in the education sector, and (iii) improve the management of fiscal risk stemming from the electricity sector and from Public and Private Partnerships (PPPs), with mutually reinforcing complementarities among these three pillars. This operation (P158463) aligns with the Government's 2016-2020 National Development Plan (NDP) which seeks to make Côte d'Ivoire an emerging economy by 2020, targeting 8.7% GDP growth per year and halving the poverty rate, and addresses the critical context of narrowing domestic fiscal space, rising non-concessional public debt, and significant fiscal risks in the energy sector and from PPPs, alongside persistent inequality and poverty, and external and internal risks including volatile commodity prices and security concerns.

The DPO series is designed to tackle challenges in implementing the NDP, focusing on three strategic pillars: quality of institutions and good governance, accelerating the development of human capital and social well-being, and structural transformation of the economy through industrialization. The programmatic approach is justified by the cascading nature of reforms in areas like education, procurement, and tax mobilization, which require time and behavioral changes for full effectiveness. The operation is expected to have positive social and poverty impacts, including increased fiscal space for social programs, improved education quality and equity for poor households, enhanced energy access and reliability, and better allocation of resources to the poor through reduced PPP risks and improved public finance management, with a specific positive impact on gender equality by facilitating girls' and women's access to social services. No adverse poverty or social impacts are expected, and no short or long-term climate or disaster risks relevant to the operation have been identified, with an overall risk assessed as moderate.

Key results indicators for 2018, with 2014 or 2015 baselines, include: an increase in taxpaying firms and individuals from 86,945 to 105,000; an increase in VAT revenue collected from 1.7% to 2.0% of GDP; a reduction in public procurement completion time from 159 to 120 days; a decrease in the primary school repetition rate from 15.6% to less than 10%; an increase in lower secondary education completion rates for children from the poorest families (boys from 25% to 33%, girls from 13% to 22%); an improvement in CI-Energies' net income from a CFAF 63 billion deficit to a CFAF 70 billion surplus; a reduction in electricity losses from 22% to 20%; and the Government signing at least two power purchase agreements with Independent Power Producers, including one for renewable energy. The Government's strong track record under IMF-supported programs, close collaboration between the World Bank and IMF on budget support, and the comprehensive and participatory development of the NDP, which secured US$34 billion in commitments from partners (including US$5 billion from WBG), all contribute to a strong foundation for forecasting overall activity success.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The targeted outcomes are to strengthen domestic resource mobilization and efficiency, enhance allocative efficiency of public spending in the education sector, and improve fiscal risk management from the electricity sector and PPPs. Success would be indicated by: taxpaying firms/individuals increasing to 105,000 (from 86,945); VAT revenue rising to 2.0% of GDP (from 1.7%); public procurement time reducing to 120 days (from 159); primary school repetition rate falling to <10% (from 15.6%); lower secondary completion rates for poorest families increasing (boys to 33%, girls to 22%); CI-Energies’ net income reaching CFAF 70 billion surplus (from CFAF 63 billion deficit); electricity losses reducing to 20% (from 22%); and signing at least two power purchase agreements with Independent Power Producers, including one for renewable energy.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: External context includes a robust economic recovery (8.5% avg growth 2012-15), supported by a favorable external environment and active donor community engagement (US$34bn for NDP). An improving socio-political environment is marked by peaceful 2015 presidential elections and a 2016 constitutional referendum, bolstering stability. However, significant external risks persist, including exposure to volatile commodity prices, slowdowns in trading partners, higher international borrowing costs, potential negative shocks from slower growth in emerging markets, and security threats like the March 2016 terrorist attacks. The government's strong track record with IMF-supported programs also bodes well.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is the first in a programmatic series of two DPOs, addressing complex challenges in fiscal management, education, and energy reforms. It involves cascading reforms requiring significant behavioral changes for effectiveness and focuses on strengthening domestic resource mobilization, enhancing public spending efficiency in education, and improving fiscal risk management from the electricity sector and PPPs. This multi-pillar approach with mutually reinforcing complementarities, as well as a balance of cross-cutting and specific actions, contributes to its inherent complexity.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is the first in a programmatic series of two consecutive Development Policy Operations (DPOs). This DPO series is directly and cohesively integrated into the Government's 2016-2020 National Development Plan (NDP), addressing its strategic pillars and challenges. The NDP itself can be considered a larger national development program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "[\"The CI-DPO on Fiscal Management, Education and Energy Reforms is financed by an IDA credit of EURO 68.5 million (US$75 million equivalent) provided as general budget support; no specific amounts are budgeted per sub-activity. The DPO supports three main pillars: (i) strengthening domestic resource mobilization and efficiency, (ii) enhancing allocative efficiency of public spending in education, and (iii) improving fiscal risk management from the electricity sector and PPPs. Sub-activities include public debt management reforms, electricity tariff adjustments, and PPP rationalization, among others. Energy sector asset renewals and grid expansion require CFAF 1.6 trillion (2016-2020), financed by tariffs.\", \"The activity faces significant financial challenges, with a Balance of Payments financing gap projected at CFAF 146 billion (2016) and CFAF 183 billion (2017), indicating potential under-funding despite budget support. Côte d'Ivoire remains at moderate debt distress risk, stemming from lower fiscal/export revenue and rising non-concessional debt. While the NDP secured US$34 billion in commitments, the success of the DPO hinges on prudent debt management, careful monitoring of external debt, and effective management of contingent liabilities from PPPs and SOEs to avoid increased vulnerabilities. The DPO helps address these risks by supporting reforms.\"]"

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The MEF, implementing with the Government of C		u00f4te d'Ivoire, shows strong investment. The activity aligns with their comprehensive 2016-2020 NDP, backed by $34B partner commitments. A "strong track record" under IMF programs and proven DPO management via an MEF-appointed team demonstrate high capacity. Government commitment to fiscal discipline shows skin-in-the-game. Aiding stakeholders: World Bank (funding, TA), IMF, EU, AFD, AfDB, and existing WB projects ensure robust support. Risks (political, institutional capacity, macroeconomic) are substantial but mitigated by strong collaboration and government actions.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- External and internal risks have risen, including volatile commodity prices, slowdown in trading partners, higher international borrowing costs, and tightening international financial conditions impacting export revenues. Recent terrorist attacks (March 2016) could jeopardize peace and stability, divert financial resources, and raise fiscal deficits. Root causes of past conflicts like land tenure security and high youth unemployment remain unaddressed.
- Domestically, fiscal space is narrowing, with the fiscal deficit expected to reach 4% of GDP in 2016 and rising non-concessional public debt. Significant fiscal risks stem from the energy sector's fragile financial conditions (State-owned enterprises) and extensive recourse to Public and Private Partnerships (PPPs), potentially leading to debt accumulation. The financial situation of certain public enterprises and drought also pose risks. Political, governance, and institutional capacity risks are substantial. Upcoming legislative elections could lead to expenditure overruns.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Côte d'Ivoire shows sustained economic recovery, poverty reduction (46.3% in 2015), and improved infrastructure/services. Strong external sector performance includes rising FDI and trade surplus. Successful international capital market access (oversubscribed Eurobonds, low yield, resilient debt market). Sound fiscal management with sustained revenue growth and narrowing deficit. Business environment significantly improved (35 places up in Doing Business, most competitive in Africa). Government's strong structural reform program, observance of borrowing ceilings, and IMF/EU collaboration mitigate risks. Overall risk is moderate."

This specifically covers consolidating and extending the MV/LV Electricity Infrastructure, financing goods and materials up to US$ 2.5 million for EDD's repair and maintenance stock, funding Project-Financed Last Mile Connection Materials, and implementing street lighting in areas such as Dogley, Warabley 1 and Warabley 2 in Balbala North, and Layabley, Layabley bis, PK12 Zone Cinema and Nassib in Balbala South, with other areas to be added as agreed. Subcomponent 1.2 finances additional costs related to the MV/LV Electricity Infrastructure, including land parcels and environmental and social studies, and covers all connection costs for Last Mile Connections, excluding the Project-Financed Last Mile Connection Materials.

Component 2, "Technical assistance, capacity building and program management," consists of Subcomponent 2.1 which finances costs for Project management, implementation, and oversight, including specialist and key expert remuneration for the PIU, audits, and the midterm review. It also funds the drafting of a National Sustainable Electrification Program and relevant technical studies, such as a distribution grid master plan and an action plan, to support the Government of Djibouti's goals of achieving a 100% electrification rate by 2035, reaching 100% renewable energy supply, and reducing urban migration. ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P158713
ACTIVITY TITLE: China: Liaoning Safe and Sustainable Urban Water Supply Project
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2017-06-30
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 300,453,175

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: CN 100.00% | Location: Shenyang; Tanghe Shuiku; Dahuofang Shuiku; Anshan; Fushun; Shimenzi Shuiku; Fuxin; Gaizhoudao

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 13303

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The China: Liaoning Safe and Sustainable Urban Water Supply Project aims to improve water quality and operational efficiency of selected water supply utilities in the project areas, benefitting 5.69 million people across five cities/counties (Shenyang, Anshan, Fuxin, Fushun, and Gaizhou), with approximately 50% women beneficiaries. The project's development objectives contribute to expanding safe water supply and improving service quality and efficiency in more than 50 small towns and cities. The project encompasses two main components: Water supply service infrastructure improvement and Water supply service management improvement. The first component, costing US$287.14 million (of which US$185.06 million is from an IBRD Loan), supports infrastructure investments to improve water supply services, reduce Non-Revenue Water (NRW), and increase energy efficiency through civil works construction and rehabilitation. These activities include the construction, upgrade, repair, and/or rehabilitation of water reservoirs, treatment plants, pump stations, booster pumps, transmission and distribution pipelines, and control valves, as well as the installation and/or replacement of water meters for district, bulk, commercial, industrial, and household customers, and household water service connections. The second component, costing US$57.08 million (with an IBRD Loan of US$48.63 million), aims to enhance water supply service management through measures such as NRW reduction, water quality monitoring, energy savings, and effective asset management, including mapping and modeling of all water distribution systems. The overall project cost, including fees, is US$386.29 million, with IBRD financing representing 60% of the total counterpart fund of US$372.89 million. The project adopts a comprehensive and innovative approach by introducing the latest technologies in network replacement, rehabilitation (e.g., selected pipe materials, efficient pumps, advanced meters), and state-of-the-art SCADA systems and telemetry platforms for real-time monitoring and decision-making, along with institutional capacity building. It also promotes active citizen engagement through social media for reporting complaints and problems, and contributes to climate change adaptation and mitigation through efficient water resource/energy use and GHG emission reduction. \n\nThe financial benefits of the project include cost savings from system rehabilitation, leakage reduction, and technical upgrades (covering energy, raw water, chemicals, repair, and maintenance), increased water sales due to reduced commercial NRW, and incremental water supply to existing connections. The project is expected to reduce leakages from current levels and decrease energy consumption through reduced leakages and rehabilitated pumping stations and wells, with carbon benefits also incorporated. A financial analysis, prepared using a with/without methodology over 25 years starting from 2017 (the first year of implementation) and based on actual results from several base years, projects positive financial internal rates of return (FIRR) for all participating cities: Shenyang at 17%, Anshan at 18% (excluding government financial support), Fuxin at 20%, Fushun at 9% (excluding government financial support), and Gaizhou at 4% (excluding government financial support). Even in a sensitivity case (a combination of a one-year project delay, 5% increase in project costs, and 5% decrease in financial benefits), the FIRRs remain positive, ranging from 3% to 18%. All projected FIRRs exceed the weighted average cost of capital, which is below 3%. Water demand development assumptions include an increase in the volume of water supplied in Shenyang, Fuxin, Fushun, and Anshan from implementation onwards, with a projected 2% per annum growth in water demand from existing connected residential consumers over the first 10 years, where current demand is approximately 100 litres per capita per day (LCD). For Gaizhou specifically, residential water demand is expected to decline from around 140 LCD to below 110 LCD due to improved metering. The relatively low FIRR for Gaizhou is considered acceptable due to a technical imperative to finance a new water source and production, necessitated by the impending closure of its current main source due to environmental issues, specifically seawater intrusion from over-exploitation of groundwater and associated ground settlement. Without the project, the water companies would face increased costs of repair and maintenance, with historical annual increases of 3-4% in breakage-related efforts over the last three years, and would struggle to maintain supply capacity. For Gaizhou, the absence of the project would mean the compulsory closure of its main water source, severely reducing its capacity to serve water demands."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve water quality and operational efficiency for 5.69 million people (49% women) in Shenyang, Anshan, Fuxin, Fushun, and Gaizhou. To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve 100% water quality health standard compliance; significantly reduce Non-Revenue Water (e.g., Fushun to 22.1%, Gaizhou to 35.1%) and energy consumption (e.g., Gaizhou to 0.36 kWh/m³); improve working ratios; achieve 75% customer satisfaction; complete 300 km of rehabilitated pipelines and install 115,000 water meters; establish hydraulic network models in 3 cities and computerized Asset Management systems in 5 cities; train 754 staff; and resolve 100% of grievances on time."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "The project benefits from a strong external context including alignment with China's 12th and 13th Five Year Plans, which prioritize infrastructure, green growth, water conservation, and energy efficiency. National policies and regulations, such as water loss targets (12% by 2017, 10% by 2020) and the 13th Five Year Plan for Building Water Saving Society, demonstrate high government commitment. It addresses critical external challenges like worsening water scarcity, groundwater depletion, climate change impacts, and severe water pollution in Liaoning, where water quality compliance is low (33% in project cities) and per capita water resources are scarce. The project leverages a robust, longstanding partnership with the World Bank in the water sector, introducing global knowledge (e.g., IWA Water Loss Framework) and building upon the success of previous Bank-financed projects in Liaoning. China's substantial 13th Five Year Plan investment in water (US$385 billion nationally, US$9.47 billion in Liaoning) provides a strong financial and political backdrop. However, Liaoning Province's economic downturn (including negative GDP growth in 2016) and historical challenges in infrastructure maintenance pose potential external risks, though central government prioritization for revitalization may mitigate this."

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity is technically complex, involving the introduction of latest technologies in network replacement and rehabilitation (e.g., selected pipe materials, efficient pumps, advanced meters), and state-of-the-art SCADA systems and telemetry platforms for real-time monitoring and decision-making. It also includes extensive civil works construction and rehabilitation across multiple cities for water reservoirs, treatment plants, pump stations, and pipelines, along with institutional capacity building and promotion of active citizen engagement.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: This activity is a large, comprehensive program designed to improve water quality and operational efficiency across five cities/counties in Liaoning, China. With a total cost of US$386.29 million and a seven-year duration, it encompasses two main components: water supply service infrastructure improvement and water supply service management improvement. It is a standalone, significant program rather than being integrated into a larger overarching program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The China: Liaoning Safe and Sustainable Urban Water Supply Project has a total cost of US$386.29 million, including fees. The International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) provides US$250.00 million in financing, while counterpart funds account for US$136.29 million. It also covers the installation and/or replacement of water meters for district, bulk, commercial, industrial, and household customers, and household water service connections.\n    *   Budget allocation per beneficiary water company:\n        *   Shenyang Water Supply Group: US$92.80 million (US$58.75 million from IBRD).\n        *   Anshan Water Supply Co.: US$50.32 million (US$32.83 million from IBRD).\n        *   Fushun Water Supply Co.: US$85.19 million (US$53.83 million from IBRD).\n        *   Fuxin Water Supply Co.: US$29.54 million (US$19.56 million from IBRD).\n        *   Gaizhou Water Supply Co.: US$29.29 million (US$20.09 million from IBRD).\n\n2.  Water Supply Service Management Improvement: Total cost is US$57.08 million (IBRD Loan: US$48.63 million; Counterpart: US$8.45 million).\n    *   Activities aim to enhance management through Non-Revenue Water (NRW) reduction, water quality monitoring, energy savings, and effective asset management, including mapping and modeling of water distribution systems.\n    *   Budget allocation per beneficiary water company:\n        *   Shenyang Water Supply Group: US$23.11 million (US$20.14 million from IBRD).\n        *   Anshan Water Supply Co.: US$8.67 million (US$7.13 million from IBRD).\n        *   Fushun Water Supply Co.: US$9.97 million (US$7.95 million from IBRD).\n        *   Fuxin Water Supply Co.: US$10.14 million (US$8.94 million from IBRD).\n        *   Gaizhou Water Supply Co.: US$5.19 million (US$4.46 million from IBRD).\n\n3.  Project Implementation Support and Institutional Strengthening: Total cost is US$16.94 million (IBRD Loan: US$15.69 million; Counterpart: US$1.25 million).\n    *   This includes non-consulting services, consultants' services, training and workshops, and incremental operating costs.\n    *   Budget allocation per beneficiary water company:\n        *   Shenyang Water Supply Group: US$10.67 million (US$9.88 million from IBRD).\n        *   Anshan Water Supply Co.: US$1.02 million (US$0.94 million from IBRD).\n        *   Fushun Water Supply Co.: US$1.15 million (US$1.06 million from IBRD).\n        *   Fuxin Water Supply Co.: US$1.54 million (US$1.42 million from IBRD).\n        *   Gaizhou Water Supply Co.: US$2.57 million (US$2.38 million from IBRD).\n\nOther Project Costs:\n*   Front-end Fee: US$0.625 million (100% IBRD financed).\n*   Financial Charges During Implementation (interest during construction and commitment fees): US$24.50 million (100% Counterpart financed).\n\nCost Details, Typical Costs, and Discrepancies:\nThe project introduces latest technologies in network replacement, rehabilitation (e.g., selected pipe materials, efficient pumps, advanced meters), and state-of-the-art SCADA systems and telemetry platforms for real-time monitoring and decision-making, aiming for improved efficiency and water quality. Without the project, water companies would face increased costs of repair and maintenance, with historical annual increases of 3-4% in breakage-related efforts over the last three years. The project is expected to reduce these operational costs through leakage reduction and rehabilitated infrastructure. A significant cost detail is for Gaizhou, where the relatively low financial internal rate of return (FIRR) of 4% (without government support) is considered acceptable due to the technical imperative to finance a new water source. This new source is necessitated by the impending closure of its current main source due to environmental issues (seawater intrusion from over-exploitation of groundwater and associated ground settlement). Without the project, Gaizhou would face the compulsory closure of its main water source, severely reducing its capacity to serve water demands, making the project's costs a critical investment to prevent a major service disruption.\n\nFinancing and its Effect on Activity Success:\nTotal project financing is US$386.29 million, with US$250.00 million from an IBRD loan and US$136.29 million from counterpart funds. The financial analysis projects positive FIRRs for all participating cities: Shenyang at 17%, Anshan at 18% (excluding government financial support), Fuxin at 20%, Fushun at 9% (excluding government financial support), and Gaizhou at 4% (excluding government financial support). All projected FIRRs exceed the weighted average cost of capital (below 3%), indicating that the investments are financially viable. Even under sensitivity scenarios (one-year project delay, 5% increase in project costs, and 5% decrease in financial benefits), FIRRs remain positive, ranging from 3% to 18%, suggesting the project is robustly financed against unforeseen challenges. However, despite improvements in working ratios, the financial projections indicate that, with current tariff assumptions, none of the companies are projected to be profitable in net profit terms. The project promotes active citizen engagement through social media for reporting complaints and problems. The project demonstrates strong financial viability, with projected positive financial internal rates of return (FIRRs) for all participating cities ranging from 4% to 20%, all exceeding the weighted average cost of capital (below 3%).
