ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P162760
ACTIVITY TITLE: Electricity Service Access Project
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2017-05-31
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2024-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Sub-national: Multi-first-level administrative areas

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 36,185,600

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: ZM 100.00% | Location: Western Province; Southern Province; North-Western Province; Northern Province; Mkushi District; Luapula Province; Eastern Province; Copperbelt Province; Central Province; Muchinga Province

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 1235

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Electricity Service Access Project (P162760) aims to increase electricity access in Zambia's targeted rural areas. Its Project Development Objective (PDO) is to increase electricity access, with an end target of providing new or improved electricity service to 114,400 people. This target is calculated based on grid-connected households multiplied by an average rural household size of 5.2, and progress will be measured quarterly via reports from REA and ZESCO. The project specifically targets households, businesses, community and public facilities, and farmers in rural areas, encompassing approximately 22,000 low-income households and 1,000 Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs), totaling around 115,000 direct beneficiaries. Many of these beneficiaries will receive electricity for the first time, replacing traditional fuels like kerosene, diesel, wood, and dry cell batteries. The project also intends to reduce disparities in electricity services between urban and rural households, thereby improving opportunities for rural socioeconomic development. Furthermore, key institutions such as REA, ZESCO, the Development Bank of Zambia (DBZ), and the Department of Energy (DoE) will benefit from capacity-building and implementation support activities. The project represents the first stage of a two-stage effort by the World Bank Group to support the Zambian government's renewed commitment to scaling up rural electrification.

The project is necessitated by the Zambian government's (GRZ) recognition that the existing model for increasing rural electricity access is not achieving intended results, putting access targets at risk. A key part of the GRZ's strategy involves repositioning the Rural Electrification Authority (REA) from an implementer to a facilitator, tasked with providing incentives to ZESCO and private developers for both on-grid and off-grid rural electrification. This shift is supported by analysis under the 'SE4ALL' Initiative, which also advocates for clearer roles between REA and ZESCO, greater private sector engagement, and an enhanced Rural Electrification Fund (REF) financing model to be administered by REA. To enable comprehensive and inclusive national electrification, the project underscores the critical need for a National Electrification Strategy (NES). This strategy will provide a planning framework, remove regulatory impediments, ensure sustainable financial support, and improve institutional coordination across urban and rural areas, incorporating insights from regularly updated geospatial planning and household surveys. The NES is expected to clearly define roles and responsibilities for all stakeholders, including the private sector, and identify incentives and mechanisms to remove bottlenecks for private sector participation, and will be developed through extensive consultation.

The project comprises three main components. Component A focuses on On-grid Electricity Access Expansion, leveraging the successful OBA (Output-Based Aid) subsidy approach, previously tested in urban areas, for consumer connections in rural areas. This component will finance critical distribution network reinforcements and extensions using low-cost grid electrification techniques to support 'last mile' connections, enabling ZESCO to connect new users and complementing other cooperating partners' efforts. This component has a total funding of SDR 17.3 million (approximately US$23.7 million equivalent), including contributions from IDA, SIDA/GPOBA Trust Fund, consumers, and REA/ZESCO, and will be implemented through two subcomponents, initially focusing on areas requiring minimal extension works. Component B, Off-grid Electricity Access Expansion, initially funds upstream activities such as identifying and scoping off-grid sites, addressing regulatory impediments, and building institutional capacity. Upon successful completion of these preparatory activities, it will pilot two financial mechanisms: a Smart Grant Subsidy Facility and a Loan Facility, designed to support private sector-led electrification through renewable energy mini-grids and stand-alone solar systems, structured to leverage private financing and participation. This component is supported by IDA SDR 4.3 million (approximately US$5.9 million equivalent), and its design is based on lessons from previous off-grid efforts and regional experience. Component C will fund the development of the comprehensive NES and provide support for timely and efficient project implementation, through undertaking diagnostic and analytical activities.

Several factors contribute to a positive forecast for the project's overall success. The GRZ's explicit recognition of past shortcomings and its commitment to a revised, comprehensive strategy, including legislative amendments based on reviews of REA's role and mandate, signals strong political will and institutional alignment. The adoption of a proven OBA subsidy approach, previously successful in urban areas, to rural on-grid electrification offers a tested model for consumer connections. The strong emphasis on developing a comprehensive National Electrification Strategy (NES), informed by geospatial planning and regular surveys, is crucial for long-term, coordinated, and sustainable scale-up, ensuring that interventions are well-targeted and adapt to changing conditions. The project's two-stage approach allows for the establishment of workable models and a conducive environment in the first stage, which is designed to attract larger development partner funding and private sector interest in the subsequent stage, addressing a key constraint identified. Furthermore, the focus on building the capacity of key institutions like REA, ZESCO, DBZ, and DoE, alongside active private sector engagement through designed financial mechanisms (Smart Grant Subsidy Facility and Loan Facility), is vital for sustainable implementation and leveraging additional resources. The extensive consultation process for the NES, involving local government, communities, and the private sector, indicates a participatory approach that can enhance ownership and relevance of the strategy. The clear, measurable target of 114,400 people provided with new or improved electricity service, with quarterly monitoring by REA/ZESCO, provides a robust metric for tracking progress and accountability.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: Targeted outcomes: Increase electricity access for 114,400 people (22,000 low-income households, 1,000 MSEs) in Zambia's rural areas, replacing traditional fuels, reducing urban-rural disparities, and improving rural socioeconomic development. It also aims to build capacity in REA, ZESCO, DBZ, DoE, and develop a comprehensive National Electrification Strategy (NES) for sustainable scale-up, removing regulatory impediments. Success factors include achieving the 114,400 beneficiary target (monitored quarterly), successful OBA subsidy application, and the NES's development and adoption, creating a conducive environment for future private sector investment.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The external context offers a cautiously optimistic outlook. Strong GRZ political will for sector reform, including revised strategies, legislative amendments, and commitment to financial sustainability (e.g., tariff adjustments), is a key positive. Post-2016 election stability and improving macroeconomic forecasts (GDP growth, Kwacha stability) also contribute positively. However, deep-seated challenges remain: the power sector's historical financial unsustainability, pervasive rural poverty affecting affordability, and inherent difficulties of low-density rural electrification.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The Electricity Service Access Project is highly complex due to its multi-faceted approach to increasing electricity access in Zambia's rural areas. It addresses a challenging operational environment where electricity demand has outpaced generation capacity and the power sector is not financially sustainable. The project involves significant institutional reforms, including repositioning the Rural Electrification Authority (REA) from an implementer to a facilitator. Key complexities include developing a comprehensive National Electrification Strategy (NES) based on geospatial planning and household surveys, integrating various technical approaches (on-grid network reinforcements and extensions, off-grid renewable energy mini-grids, and stand-alone solar systems), and designing intricate financial mechanisms (Output-Based Aid subsidies, Smart Grant Subsidy Facility, and a Loan Facility). Furthermore, it requires extensive capacity building for institutions like REA, ZESCO, DBZ, and DoE, establishing and maintaining Project Implementing Units with diverse expertise, and engaging external consultants and independent verification.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The Electricity Service Access Project is the first stage of a two-stage effort by the World Bank Group, demonstrating its direct and cohesive integration into a larger program aimed at scaling up rural electrification in Zambia. The project itself could be considered a larger program given its comprehensive scope encompassing policy development (National Electrification Strategy), institutional reform, diverse technical interventions (on-grid and off-grid), financial mechanisms, and capacity building across multiple provinces. This initial stage is designed to establish workable models and a conducive environment to attract larger development partner funding and private sector interest in the subsequent stage of the broader national electrification initiative.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Electricity Service Access Project totals US$36.8M. Component A (On-grid) US$23.7M covers connections/grid extension. Component B (Off-grid) US$5.9M supports smart grants/loan facility. Component C (Capacity Building/Support) US$7.2M funds TA/implementation. Financing includes IDA (US$26.5M), SIDA/GPOBA (US$7M, in principle), REA/ZESCO (US$2.7M), Consumers (US$0.6M). Key risks: SIDA/GPOBA funding is not secured, potentially impacting components A.1 and C. Off-grid funds (B.2) may reallocate to on-grid if delays occur. Broader Zambian power sector's financial unsustainability (US$300-400M annual losses) poses long-term risks."

An Independent Verification Entity (IVE) will enhance accountability. These elements create a complex implementation environment with strong government backing but notable financial and capacity risks requiring diligent management and external support."]

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P162849
ACTIVITY TITLE: India Energy Efficiency Scale-up Program
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2017-10-19
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2023-09-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Single location

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 1,496,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: IN 100.00% | Location: New Delhi

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 2696

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The India Energy Efficiency Scale-up Program is designed to scale up energy savings in the residential and public sectors, bolster EESL's institutional capacity, and improve its access to commercial financing. Success will be gauged through PDO-level outcome indicators that track market transformation in energy-efficient appliances and equipment, the development of sustainable business models in emerging EE market segments, increased access to commercial financing, and enhanced institutional strength. For the IPF component, the quantum of commercial financing leveraged by the IBRD guarantee will be precisely quantified, measured, and reported. The program is structured with four key Results Areas under the PforR, with achievement measured by six Disbursement Linked Indicators (DLIs): energy savings and EE market transformation in both the residential sector and public street lighting, the development of sustainable business models in new EE market segments, and institutional strengthening to facilitate sustainable EE scale-up.

Regarding its environmental and social implications, the Program Environmental and Social Systems Assessment (ESSA) anticipates substantial environmental and social benefits. These benefits are expected to be sustainable beyond the project period, provided proper planning is undertaken and negative impacts or adverse environmental issues are effectively managed through the involvement of various stakeholders. No sensitive, diverse, or unprecedented adverse effects on the environment and people are foreseen, and the Program carries a "moderate" social risk. Crucially, planned efforts and a strong focus on institutional capacity development are deemed essential for ensuring that the Program interventions yield sustainable social and environmental benefits. While key provisions of EESL's environmental systems generally align with national and state environmental legislations, effectively addressing most impacts from its programs, there are identified opportunities for enhancing and strengthening how EESL manages environmental and social impacts and risks, particularly in areas like waste management, hazards and issues during installation and maintenance, and product quality. The comprehensive measurement framework, coupled with a focus on institutional capacity building and proactive environmental and social risk management, suggests a strong foundation for the program's overall success.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: Targeted outcomes: Scale up energy savings in residential and public sectors; bolster EESL's institutional capacity; improve EESL's access to commercial financing; market transformation in EE appliances/equipment; develop sustainable business models in emerging EE segments; achieve substantial and sustainable environmental/social benefits including reduced carbon intensity. Success requires: tracking market transformation in EE products/models; increased commercial financing access; enhanced institutional strength; quantifying IBRD guarantee leveraged financing; effective management of environmental/social impacts, including strengthened waste management, installation safety, and product quality; and achieving energy savings and market transformation targets across residential and public lighting.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: India's high economic growth and rising energy demand, plus strong Government of India (GoI) commitment to energy efficiency (EE), climate goals, and universal electricity access, establish a favorable policy environment. Yet, vast EE potential is untapped due to market failures: high financing costs, limited awareness, and technical barriers. Residential and public sectors face additional hurdles from subsidized tariffs and high transaction costs. The ESSA predicts substantial, sustainable environmental and social benefits with a moderate social risk. Overall program risk is moderate.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity addresses significant technical and institutional complexities. These include overcoming barriers in the residential and public sector for energy efficiency (like non-cost-reflective tariffs, lack of awareness, limited technical know-how, and high cost of financing). It also tackles market development challenges for Energy Service Companies (ESCOs), particularly in scaling business, knowledge gaps, and access to finance. Further complexity arises from the need for EESL to improve access to commercial capital and develop innovative models for raising debt. Institutionally, the program addresses inadequate mechanisms for environmental and social considerations, requiring strengthening of EESL's capacity in waste management, installation hazards, and product quality.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The "India Energy Efficiency Scale-up Program" is itself a large-scale program. It is directly integrated into and supports the broader Energy Efficiency Services Limited (EESL) investment program (totaling INR 511 billion/USD 7.2 billion) and ongoing Government programs such as "Unnat Jyoti by Affordable LEDs for All" (UJALA) and the "Street Lighting National Program" (SLNP). Furthermore, EESL, the implementing agency, is a key entity tasked with the implementation of the National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE), making this activity cohesively part of national energy efficiency goals.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The India Energy Efficiency Scale-up Program's IBRD-supported scope (FY2018-FY2022) is budgeted at USD 1.5 billion. Sub-activities include investments in UJALA and SLNP, new business model development, and institutional strengthening, targeting residential and public sector energy efficiency. Total financing is $1496 million. EESL's inefficient access to commercial capital is a critical concern, risking a bottleneck for market transformation and indicating potential under-funding in this area. Program success hinges on mobilizing private resources, diversifying financiers, and leveraging the IBRD guarantee to secure commercial financing for scale-up."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: EESL, a state-owned ESCO and joint venture of four major PSUs, exhibits strong performance with successful large-scale EE programs (UJALA, SLNP). It demonstrates significant investment by leveraging its own capital for upfront financing and has mobilized diverse commercial funding. The program aims to strengthen EESL's institutional capacity and improve its access to commercial financing, addressing identified bottlenecks. Government backing and IBRD's guarantee are key aiding factors. While EESL’s environmental and social systems are largely aligned, targeted improvements in waste management and installation practices are crucial for sustainable outcomes, preventing potential deterioration.
ACTIVITY RISKS:
- Risk of limited availability and high cost of financing for EE investments due to high upfront costs, weak balance sheets of EE service providers, dispersed nature of projects, and high-risk perceptions by financial institutions lacking familiarity with EE projects, services, and business models.
- Risk from residential and public sector specific barriers including non-cost-reflective tariffs, lack of awareness, limited technical know-how, perceived risks by households, low availability and high cost of financing for residential EE interventions, and limited competition among EE product manufacturers resulting in expensive options.
- Risk of inadequate institutional capacity within EESL for managing environmental and social impacts and risks, particularly concerning waste management, installation/maintenance hazards, and product quality, as current institutional mechanisms for environmental and social considerations are deemed inadequate, and if EESL staff capacity for guidance and compliance is not strengthened.
- Risk from market development challenges faced by the nascent ESCO industry, including targeted companies' lack of knowledge regarding potential energy savings and benefits of ESCO services, leading to an inability to scale their business significantly.
- Risk that negative environmental impacts or adverse environmental issues are not effectively managed, potentially undermining the program's sustainable benefits, and if proper planning is not undertaken and adverse issues are not effectively managed through stakeholder involvement, and if institutional capacity development is not strong enough.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The program anticipates substantial, sustainable environmental and social benefits, with moderate social risk and minor adverse environmental impacts. EESL has a strong track record of mobilizing significant financing, mitigating upfront customer risk, and raising commercial funds. Robust institutional capacity building and proactive environmental/social risk management, aligned with national targets, provide a strong foundation. EESL's established EHSS, EMF, EMP, and IPPF systems further ensure effective management and success, leveraging an IBRD Guarantee for broader commercial financing."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P162858
ACTIVITY TITLE: Second Fiscal Sustainability, Competitiveness, and Migration DPF
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2017-12-15
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2020-05-21

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Single location

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 750,000,000

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: CO 100.00% | Location: Bogota

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 7913

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Colombia Second Fiscal Sustainability, Competitiveness, and Migration Development Policy Financing" is a single-tranche operation, the second in a series of two Development Policy Financing operations, designed to support Colombia's efforts to maintain fiscal sustainability and enable accelerated productivity growth, particularly in the non-extractive sector, within a less favorable external environment. This DPF series recognizes and supports the Colombian authorities' reform efforts, aligning with the country's National Development Plan (NDP) 2014-2018, which emphasizes Green Growth for peace and sustainable development, and the newer 2018-2022 NDP's focus on legality and entrepreneurship. The operation complements other related DPFs, specifically supporting the CPF's crosscutting area of constructing peace, and is linked to the Territorial Development Development Policy Loan (P158520) and the Sustainable Development and Green Growth DPLs (P150475 and P161642) through measures like a carbon tax and energy efficiency reforms.

The Program Development Objectives (PDOs) for this activity are to: (i) support fiscal sustainability measures and improved contingent liabilities management; (ii) foster productivity and growth in non-extractive sectors by strengthening the policy framework for trade facilitation, business regulation, innovation, and green growth; and (iii) support the regularization and integration of migrants. The reforms under these objectives are expected to mutually reinforce each other, aiming to raise investor confidence, de-risk the economy, reduce private sector costs, enhance resilience to shocks, and foster medium-term growth.

The total financing for this activity is US$750 million. This comprises an IBRD loan of US$718.5 million with a variable spread, a 20.5-year maturity period including a 16.5-year grace period, and US$31.5 million in concessional financing from the Global Concessional Financing Facility on a non-reimbursable basis. The Republic of Colombia is the borrower, with the Ministry of Finance and Public Credit and the National Department of Planning acting as implementing agencies. The operation's ID is P162858.

The DPF's components address key priorities. Fiscal consolidation remains a central government priority due to additional spending needs for implementing the peace process and a mandate from the Fiscal Rules to reduce the central government deficit by 3 percentage points by 2022 (from 2.9% of GDP in 2016 to 1% by 2022 for the structural balance). The DPF supports critical revenue, expenditure, and contingent liabilities reforms, recognizing the authorities' commitment to stringent expenditure controls, tax policy and administration reforms. To foster productivity and competitiveness, the DPF backs structural reforms to strengthen the policy framework for trade facilitation, business regulation, innovation, and green growth, specifically targeting non-extractive sectors. Furthermore, Colombia is recognized as a regional leader in proactively managing the Venezuela migration crisis, and this operation supports the CONPES 3950 policy framework's objectives: identifying a roadmap for migrants to access health, education, early childhood development, water, sanitation, and housing services, and integrate into the labor market, while also strengthening institutions responsible for addressing the needs of the Venezuelan population.

Success will be measured by several result indicators, with baselines (B) typically from 2015 and targets (T) for 2019 unless specified otherwise. For fiscal sustainability, targets include a reduction in central government personnel expenditures and general operating expenditures as a share of GDP (B: 3.1%; T: 2.8%), an increase in the use of FONPET savings to finance subnational contributions to the subsidized health regime (B: 0; T: COP3.7 trillion cumulatively 2016-19), and an increase in non-oil central government tax revenue (excluding equity tax and surcharge CREE) as a share of GDP (B: 12.5%; T: 13%). Indicators also aim for a change in VAT evasion, measured by the estimated VAT gap (B: 22.2%; T: 23.1%), and an increase in new income tax taxpayers (B: 0; T: 420,000 cumulatively 2016-19). ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Climate Resilient Agriculture and Productivity Enhancement Project (P162956) aims to promote the adoption of improved technologies, leading to increased productivity and enhanced climate resilience of agricultural production systems in targeted areas of Chad. Specifically, it focuses on improving access to and accelerating the adoption of improved technologies adapted for smallholder farmers, and strengthening the resilience of agricultural systems to climate variability and change, primarily in the Sudanian AEZ regions of Salamat, Moyen-Chari, and Mandoul. The project targets the intensification of key rain-fed cereal value chains like sorghum, millet, corn, rice, and associated crops such as peanut, cowpea, and sesame, while also promoting diversification into livestock production (small ruminants, pigs, poultry), agro-forestry, gum arabic, and peri-urban milk production. It is aligned with Chad's National Development Plan (2017-2021), the World Bank Group Strategic Goals, and the 2016 WBG Africa Climate Business Plan, contributing to objectives like improved agricultural returns, strengthened value chains, enhanced food and nutritional security, and climate adaptation and mitigation. The project is expected to reach 360,000 direct beneficiaries, with a focus on smallholder families farming between one and five hectares of land, and emphasizes women and youth empowerment (targeting 40% women and 50% youth below 35 years) through gender and youth sensitive extension, increased access to support, subsidies, labor-saving technologies, and mobile-phone based agricultural advice. Financially, the project demonstrates strong viability, with income-generating activities showing internal rates of return between 27-29% and positive net present values across various scenarios. Economic analysis forecasts a net present value ranging from US$14.7 million to US$129.0 million and an economic internal rate of return from 14.2% to 97.1%, depending on the valuation of environmental benefits, including a projected 4.26 million tons reduction in CO2e emissions over 20 years. Technical feasibility is based on approaches proven successful in prior World Bank-financed projects like the West Africa Agriculture Productivity Program and the Niger Climate-smart Agriculture Support Project. Furthermore, the project includes a US$2.0 million component for strengthening sectoral strategies, focusing on reforms, capacity building in quality control and certification of agricultural inputs, and policy monitoring. Key targets include 360,000 farmers (108,000 female) reached with agricultural services, 21,400 hectares under improved technology, and 10,700 hectares under climate-smart agriculture. The project also establishes linkages with the Rural Mobility and Connectivity Project for road access and the Regional Sahel Irrigation Initiative Project for irrigation schemes, and is aligned with the Sahel Alliance initiative."

ACTIVITY TARGETS: "The Climate Resilient Agriculture and Productivity Enhancement Project targets promoting improved technologies, increasing productivity, and enhancing climate resilience in Chad's agriculture. Outcomes include improving smallholder farmers' access to and adoption of technologies, strengthening climate resilience, intensifying key crop value chains, and diversifying production. Success implies reaching 360,000 farmers (108,000 female) with services, 21,400 hectares under improved technology, 10,700 hectares under climate-smart agriculture, a 25% yield increase for beneficiaries, and 80% overall beneficiary satisfaction (90% for female beneficiaries)."

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: "Chad faces a high overall risk due to a highly volatile external context. The economy is underperforming due to low oil prices and a deep recession, with GDP growth at 0.2% in 2017, severely limiting government revenue and public spending. Regional insecurity from ongoing conflicts in neighboring countries (Sudan, Nigeria, CAR, Libya) and Boko Haram violence diverts scarce institutional and financial resources from development to security priorities and disrupts cross-border trade. The country is extremely vulnerable to climate change, experiencing land degradation, erratic climatic conditions, droughts, floods, poor rainfall distribution, and extreme heat, which exacerbates chronic food insecurity affecting 1.4 million people. Chad is one of the poorest nations globally, with 47% of the population below the poverty line, rapid population growth, high youth unemployment, and reliance on subsistence farming. Deep-seated gender inequalities mean women farmers have limited access to markets, services, and inputs. The agriculture sector is in a \"deep crisis,\" struggling to modernize with traditional practices and low yields, far below regional averages. Farmer-herder conflicts are also a risk in the Sudanian AEZ. Despite these challenges, the government prioritizes agriculture for economic diversification and has identified significant untapped agricultural potential."

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Guangxi Poverty Reduction Program for Results (PforR), identified as P163138, is a Program for Results operation designed to enhance the impact and efficiency of the Guangxi Consolidated Poverty Reduction Program (CPRP) in targeted counties, thereby contributing to Guangxi's broader poverty reduction targets through more efficient program budgeting and enhanced monitoring and evaluation (M&E). The CPRP itself is a subset of the Guangxi Rural Poverty Reduction Program (GRPRP), covering 54 designated national and provincial-level poverty counties and pooling over 40 earmarked funds. The PforR specifically aims to connect planned activities more closely to poor households and villages, building upon institutional advancements such as the National Poverty Registration System (NPRS) and the decentralization of poverty reduction efforts to county governments. A key aspect is reducing the gender gap in stockbreeding skills, ensuring equal benefits for both male and female farmers.

The program's financing period spans 2018-2021, with a total expected financing of US$5.53 billion. The majority, US$5.13 billion (93%), will be financed by the Government, and US$400 million (7%) will come from an IBRD loan. The PforR explicitly excludes activities with potentially significant adverse environmental and social impacts, as well as high-value procurement above specific thresholds for works (US$115 million), goods (US$75 million), non-consulting services (US$75 million), and consulting services (US$30 million). Key objectives for the targeted counties include increasing the production income of registered poor households from a baseline of 11,041 RMB to 15,000 RMB, raising the percentage of registered households with access to rural roads from 96.00% to 98.00%, and increasing access to safe drinking water from 78.60% to 85.40%. The program also targets the production of three Integrated Consolidated Fund Program-based Performance Monitoring Reports.

The PforR implements its objectives through several mechanisms. It focuses on strengthening poverty reduction leading enterprises (PRLEs) and farmer cooperatives, which are expected to generate income linkages for the poor. A PRLE accreditation process, supported by Disbursement Linked Indicators (DLIs), is designed to leverage substantial private sector investments, aiming for a 1-to-10 factor from PforR funds by encouraging "high quality investments" that demonstrate clear poverty outreach and benefit-sharing mechanisms through various incentives. The program emphasizes an annual mechanism of bottom-up budgeting in poor counties, coupled with transparent and accountable financing and M&E support from higher government levels. For M&E, a poverty data platform will be established and curated at the Information Technology office of the Regional Poverty Reduction Office (RPRO) to monitor performance across various program components, with verification protocols involving the Guangxi Audit Office and a Third-Party Verification Agency to ensure functionality, sustainability, and adherence to international best practices.

Forecasting overall activity success, the program benefits from a strong foundation. China has a globally recognized track record in poverty reduction, having already achieved the MDG of halving poverty ahead of schedule, and the government has made an ambitious commitment to eliminate rural poverty by 2020 through its Thirteenth Five-Year Plan. The PforR directly enhances established institutional innovations like the NPRS and the decentralization of poverty reduction efforts. Substantial government financial commitment, coupled with an IBRD loan and a strategy to leverage private sector investment, provides significant resources. Guangxi also possesses a good track record in implementing previous Bank-funded agricultural and poverty reduction projects. However, potential challenges include the ambitious nature of the PforR, spanning multiple sectors, and existing human resource capacity constraints at the county government level for program development, implementation, and effective monitoring, some project management steps being manual and labor-intensive. The success will largely depend on strengthening these capacities, improving linkages between inputs and outcomes, and the effective implementation of the integrated, locally-driven approaches.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The Guangxi PforR targets: increasing registered poor household production income from 11,041 RMB to 15,000 RMB; raising rural road access from 96% to 98%; increasing safe drinking water access from 78.6% to 85.4%; and producing three Integrated Consolidated Fund Program-based Performance Monitoring Reports. It also aims to reduce the gender gap in stockbreeding skills. Overall success hinges on strengthening county-level human resource capacity, improving linkages between program inputs and outcomes, and the effective implementation of integrated, locally-driven approaches, while achieving these specific quantitative targets.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The activity benefits from China's exceptional track record in poverty reduction, having lifted ~790 million people out of poverty and achieved MDG targets ahead of schedule. The government's ambitious 13th Five-Year Plan commits to eliminating rural poverty by 2020, ten years ahead of the WBG's target. This is supported by established institutional innovations like the National Poverty Registration System (NPRS) and decentralization of poverty reduction efforts to county governments. Substantial government funding (93% of US$5.53 billion) and the World Bank's extensive 25-year cooperation history further bolster success probability.

Its success relies on strengthening these capacities, improving linkages between inputs and outcomes, and effective implementation of integrated, locally-driven approaches.

It is designed to enhance the impact and efficiency of the existing Guangxi Consolidated Poverty Reduction Program (CPRP). The CPRP is explicitly stated as a sub-set of the even larger Guangxi Rural Poverty Reduction Program (GRPRP). The project's Proposed Development Objective is to increase the adoption of environmentally sustainable practices by Targeted Microenterprises. The borrower for the project is the Government of Bangladesh, and the implementing agency is Palli Karma-Sahayak Foundation (PKSF).

The project is structured around three main components: Enhancing Services and Enabling Systems, budgeted at US$24.00 million; Strengthened Access to Finance for environmentally friendly and resilient microenterprises, budgeted at US$75.00 million; and Project Management, Communication and Knowledge Management and Monitoring & Evaluation, budgeted at US$11.00 million. The total financing for the "Strengthened Access to Finance" component is US$93 million, comprising IDA US$75 million and PKSF US$18 million.

The project aims to address several critical issues, including high environmental impacts from polluting microenterprises, insufficient growth of environmentally friendly and green microenterprises, low climate resilience of microenterprises, and unsafe practices and products such as contaminated food. It recognizes barriers and market failures like information asymmetry, lack of knowledge and technical capacity, poor access to markets that value better environmental quality, high enforcement costs, and low access to quality finance, including the absence of profit/loss records or bank statements. Public sector interventions are deemed necessary due to externalities, information asymmetry, capacity gaps, a poor regulatory environment, and high enforcement costs, with the public sector playing a catalytic role in mainstreaming environmental and climate resilience into microenterprise financing.

Project activities and outputs include raising awareness and providing information for microenterprises and consumers, supporting certification and safety programs, facilitating value chain and premium market access, financing commercially viable and environmentally friendly practices, offering technical support to replace old technology with resource-efficient alternatives, and providing common services and facilities through a cluster-based approach. The project envisions short-term outcomes such as enhanced services and enabling systems, improved capacity of microenterprises to identify cost-effective technologies and green business opportunities, improved awareness of climate risks, increased capacity to grow and access markets, and strengthened access to finance for environmentally friendly and resilient microenterprises. Ultimately, the long-term outcome is a more dynamic, lower polluting, resource-efficient, and resilient microenterprise sector.

The project will target commercially viable microenterprises from the manufacturing (e.g., leather, mini textiles, light engineering, plastic factory, food processing, metal products) and agribusiness sectors (e.g., livestock, horticulture, aquaculture, poultry). The average loan size is projected to be US$1,250. While many beneficiaries are informal microenterprises, the project encourages a transition to more formal registration, recognizing that women entrepreneurs, who largely own agribusinesses, will require extra support in this process. PKSF will conduct a study to identify an appropriate registration system for microenterprises in Bangladesh. To be eligible for the line of credit under Component 2, microenterprises will need some form of government recognition. The project also promotes ambitious microenterprises with potential to become small enterprises, needing product verification, with increasing registration targets.

PKSF will provide financing to microenterprises through its existing network of performance-ranked Partner Organizations (POs), selected based on strong financial and social performance. PKSF's approach for appraising commercial viability and repayment capacity, building on its impressive performance of over 99 percent loan repayment, will be utilized. Subcomponent 1.1, "Support for Enabling Systems," involves US$11.5 million in grants from PKSF to POs for non-commercially viable services such as eco-labeling, premium market access, capacity building, and operating costs. Subcomponent 2.1, "Adoption of Innovative and Environmentally Friendly Technologies/Practices," uses IDA US$37.5 million and PKSF US$9 million, totaling US$46.5 million, to support borrowers updating existing businesses or launching new ones with sustainable practices, prioritizing clusters with high negative environmental impact and proven technical solutions. The project has identified 68 types of priority microenterprise subsectors from agribusiness and manufacturing likely to yield high impact and demand, with 30 types prioritized for Subcomponent 2.1, which may be expanded after the first year in consultation with the Bank. The project emphasizes supporting commercially viable microenterprises that adopt environmentally friendly and resilient green business practices, utilizing the success of microfinance in Bangladesh to achieve these environmental and climate beneficial activities.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: Targeted outcomes include increasing the adoption of environmentally sustainable practices by targeted microenterprises, enhancing services and enabling systems, improving microenterprise capacity for green business, and strengthening access to finance. The long-term goal is a dynamic, lower polluting, resource-efficient, and resilient microenterprise sector. Success requires 20,000 microenterprises adopting sustainable practices (30% female-owned); 60% of beneficiaries rating project interventions satisfactory; 40% of microenterprises continuing adopted practices (30% female-owned); 40,000 microenterprises signing loan agreements (30% female-owned); 25 clusters receiving common services; and 70% of microenterprises receiving capacity-building support.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: External context: Bangladesh shows strong economic growth and poverty reduction, with microenterprises critical for livelihoods. PKSF provides robust microfinance infrastructure, and the GoB is committed to green growth. However, Bangladesh is highly vulnerable to climate change impacts and pollution. Key external market failures include information asymmetry, poor access to green markets, limited quality finance, and weak regulatory enforcement for numerous, informal microenterprises, all posing significant challenges to success.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The Sustainable Enterprise Project involves considerable complexity. It addresses multifaceted challenges like high environmental impacts, insufficient growth of green microenterprises, low climate resilience, and unsafe practices, alongside market failures (information asymmetry, lack of capacity, poor market access, limited finance). Public sector intervention is crucial due to externalities, capacity gaps, and regulatory issues. The project supports formalizing informal microenterprises, requires detailed safeguard assessments (EMF, SMF, TPF), robust appraisal for Partner Organizations and microenterprises, and a Grievance Redress Mechanism. Its innovative approach to integrate environmental and climate benefits into microfinance adds to its intricate nature.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The Sustainable Enterprise Project (P163250) is an Investment Project Financing instrument, functioning as a larger, independent program. It is not integrated into a separate, overarching program but stands as a significant initiative itself. It leverages PKSF's existing network of Partner Organizations for financing delivery and utilizes Bangladesh's microfinance success to cohesively integrate environmental and climate benefits into financial activities, making it a self-contained and impactful program.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The Sustainable Enterprise Project is fully financed at US$130 million (IDA US$110M, Gov. US$20M, PKSF US$18M) with no financing gap.Sub-activities:1. Enhancing Services (US$24M): Subcomponent 1.1: US$11.5M grants for non-commercial services (eco-labeling, capacity building).2. Access to Finance (US$93M): Subcomponent 2.1: US$46.5M for adopting green technologies. Subcomponent 2.2: US$46.5M for expanding green microenterprises.3. Project Management (US$11M).Financing is robust, aided by PKSF's strong repayment. However, high enforcement costs for environmental compliance, supporting women's formal registration, and subsidizing non-commercial services pose potential resource demands that could affect activity success."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The implementing organization, Palli Karma-Sahayak Foundation (PKSF), demonstrates strong capabilities and significant investment in the Sustainable Enterprise Project. PKSF is a viable financial institution with good financial efficiency (4% return on assets), strong capital adequacy (52%), acceptable credit quality (non-performing loans below 4%), diverse funding sources, and adequate institutional capacity. It has a proven track record, having performed as a wholesale organization for on-lending IDA funds to the microfinance sector for almost 20 years, and boasts an impressive loan repayment rate of over 99 percent. PKSF will establish a dedicated Project Management Unit (PMU) with a full-time project coordinator and specialists in finance, procurement, monitoring and evaluation, knowledge management, occupational health and safety, and green business development, ensuring adequate technical and fiduciary expertise. The World Bank will oversee the establishment and staffing of this unit, and PKSF will provide annual work plans and budgets for review.

PKSF's skin-in-the-game is substantial, contributing US$18 million to the project's "Strengthened Access to Finance" component (including US$9 million for environmentally friendly technologies and practices) and an additional US$2 million to project management, totaling US$20 million (approximately 14% of the total project financing). Furthermore, through its Partner Organizations (POs), PKSF commits to operating and managing Integrated Common Facilities for at least 10 years after project closure if microenterprises do not take them over, indicating a long-term commitment beyond the project's closing date.

Other relevant stakeholders include:
*   The Government of Bangladesh (GoB) acts as the borrower, facilitating fund channeling through the Ministry of Finance, and maintains a good working relationship with PKSF. A Project Steering Committee (PSC), chaired by the Secretary of the Financial Institutions Division, will provide project supervision. The government's recognition is also required for microenterprises to be eligible for the credit line, aiding formality.
*   Partner Organizations (POs), selected based on strong financial and social performance, are crucial for implementing the project on the ground. They will deliver financing and non-commercially viable services (grants from PKSF) to microenterprises, manage cluster-level Integrated Common Facilities, and encourage microenterprises to eventually take over these shared services. Their selection criteria, agreed with the World Bank, help ensure their effectiveness.
*   Microenterprises (MEs) are the primary beneficiaries, and their active engagement is fostered through consultative processes and feedback mechanisms. The project design addresses barriers like information asymmetry and lack of capacity, and includes support for women entrepreneurs in formalization, aiming for long-term sustainability.
*   The International Development Association (IDA)/The World Bank provides the majority of the funding and plays a critical oversight and management role, setting implementation requirements, approving plans, and closely monitoring progress.

Overall, PKSF's strong financial health, extensive experience, high repayment rates, and significant financial contribution, coupled with the structured support from the GoB and the operational role of vetted POs, position the organization well to effectively implement the activity. The commitment to citizen engagement and long-term facility management further indicates a robust approach to achieving project outcomes.
ACTIVITY RISKS: High: Political/Governance. Moderate: Macroeconomic, Environment/Social. Substantial: Sector Strategies/Policies, Technical Design, Institutional Capacity, Fiduciary, Stakeholders. Specifics: PKSF/POs limited green business/environmental compliance experience. Fiduciary risk due to multiple NGOs, decentralized procurement, PO capacity. Reputational risk from non-selected POs. MEs prioritize short-term profit, fail to adopt green practices (capacity/technical/financial constraints). MEs vulnerable to climate change (lack info/capacity/finance). Unsafe ME practices, contaminated products. Growth barriers: info asymmetry, tech access, high enforcement, poor market access, low quality finance. Child labor.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "Positive indicators for the Sustainable Enterprise Project include a robust monitoring and evaluation (M&E) system, which leverages PKSF's proven intense monitoring system and includes real-time data, GIS, Activity to Output Monitoring, field-level counter monitoring by Partner Organizations (POs), and independent third-party impact and satisfaction surveys. The project features a rigorous approval process for funding and loans, with joint evaluations by PKSF and the World Bank, review by a Technical Committee of experts, and performance-based continuation of loan disbursements. PKSF, as an apex organization with acknowledged leadership, brings strong institutional capacity, extensive experience in microenterprise lending since 1990 (reaching 1.3 million borrowers through 178 POs), and a proven track record from successfully implementing the World Bank-financed Climate Change Resilience Fund-supported CCCP (2012\n2016). PKSF and its POs also demonstrate good financial sustainability. The project's holistic design integrates financial and capacity-building components to mainstream environmentally friendly practices, ensuring sustainability. Its wide geographical and microenterprise coverage, targeting around 40,000 microenterprises, is expected to yield significant demonstration impacts with tangible environmental and health benefits. Strategic partnerships, including leveraging PKSF's established capacity, complementing other ongoing projects, engaging FAO expertise for green agribusiness, and utilizing indigenous knowledge, further strengthen the project. Furthermore, procurement risks are mitigated by PKSF's past success in procurement management and specific measures like hiring a dedicated specialist, providing PO training, and conducting regular quality reviews and audits."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P163283
ACTIVITY TITLE: BF - DPO2 series on Energy and PFM
ORIGINAL PLANNED START DATE: 2017-11-06
ORIGINAL PLANNED END DATE: 2019-06-30

ACTIVITY SCOPE: Single location

PLANNED TOTAL DISBURSEMENT (USD): 59,016,947

ACTIVITY LOCATION(S): Recipient countries: BF 100.00% | Location: Ouagadougou

LOCATION GDP PER CAPITA, USD: 987

PARTICIPATING ORGANIZATIONS: World Bank

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The activity, titled "SECOND ENERGY AND FISCAL MANAGEMENT DEVELOPMENT POLICY FINANCING," is the second and final single-tranche operation within a programmatic series of two consecutive development policy financing (DPF) operations. The borrower is Burkina Faso, with the Ministry of Economy, Finance and Development (MEFD) serving as the implementation agency. Financing includes an IDA grant of SDR 17.8 million (US$25 million equivalent) and an IDA credit of EURO 43 million (US$50 million equivalent) on standard IDA terms, featuring a final maturity of 38 years with a six-year grace period. The Program Development Objectives (PDOs) for this operation are to support Government efforts to (i) improve the financial sustainability of the energy sector; (ii) enable private sector participation in the energy sector and diversify the energy mix; and (iii) strengthen tax collection and public procurement processes. The Operation ID is P163283, and the overall risk for the activity is assessed as Substantial, with identified short and long-term climate and disaster risks.

The results indicators for improving the financial sustainability of the energy sector include a reduction of cross arrears between the Government, SONABHY, and SONABEL from CFAF 140 billion (June 30, 2016 baseline) to below CFAF 1 billion (2018 target); improved financial performance of SONABEL (EBITDA) from CFAF 14 billion (2015 baseline) to CFAF 40 billion or higher (2018 target); and a reduction in customer's outstanding bills due to SONABEL from CFAF 55 billion (2015 baseline) to below CFAF 39 billion (2018 target). For enabling private sector participation in the energy sector and diversifying the energy mix, targets include increasing solar PV capacity as a percentage of total installed capacity from 0 percent (2015 baseline) to 15 percent (2018 target); increasing capacity available on the interconnected network (excluding guaranteed imports) from 52 percent (2015 baseline) to 62 percent (2018 target); signing and successfully negotiating 2 Independent Power Producer (IPP) projects (from 0 in 2015); and having 1 IPP under a standardized competitive process (from 0 percent in 2015). To strengthen tax collection and public procurement processes, targets include increasing VAT revenues from 6.0 percent of GDP (2015 baseline) to 7.0 percent of GDP (2018 target); increasing non-VAT tax revenues from 8 percent of GDP (2015 baseline) to 10 percent of GDP (2018 target); and reducing the average number of days to complete public procurement from invitation to bid to contract signed and approved from 142 days (2015 baseline) to 100 days (2018 target).

For forecasting overall activity success, the operation is well-aligned with Burkina Faso's 2016-2020 National Economic and Social Development Plan (PNDES), specifically Pillar 1 (economic governance) and Pillar 3 (structural transformation), while also impacting Pillar 2 (human capital) through improved electricity access. The PNDES aims for 8.5 percent real GDP growth and a decline in the poverty rate from 40.3 percent in 2014 to 35 percent by end-2020. The macroeconomic outlook is favorable, with projected GDP growth around 6 percent in 2018-2020, supported by various sectors and sustained public investment. The external current account deficit is projected around 8 percent of GDP, financed largely by FDI and concessional government borrowing, and the fiscal deficit is expected to narrow to 5 percent of GDP in 2018, converging to 3 percent by 2019, based on successful revenue mobilization reforms. The government is also improving administrative capacity for Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs), which are key for PNDES investments but also carry risks, and reducing procurement delays, including in the energy sector.

The energy sector in Burkina Faso faces significant challenges, including a low national electrification rate of 18 percent (60 percent urban, 3 percent rural), load shedding, poor service quality, and demand growing at 10 percent annually while installed generation capacity (315 MW, largely thermal and aging hydropower) is barely sufficient. The government's strategy is to increase access affordably and sustainably by shifting to renewable energy and affordable electricity imports, aiming for 100 percent urban and 49 percent rural access by 2025. This involves reducing dependency on imported fuels, increasing renewable energy sources (target 25 percent of installed capacity from renewables by 2025, including 170 MWp solar PV), leveraging regional integration for imports, and developing firm baseload capacity. The strategy aims to decrease generation costs by 20 percent by 2025.

The DPF series benefits from effective collaboration with the IMF and World Bank, including joint analyses and review missions. Its design incorporates lessons from previous DPFs and diagnostics, emphasizing the need for stronger commitment to reforms, realistic goal-setting given limited capacity, simplified design, sustained follow-up of prior actions, and leveraging World Bank support through parallel policy-based support and investment financing. The mutually reinforcing nature of the operation's three pillars, where higher revenue mobilization supports energy sector investments and improved electricity access boosts private sector activity and revenue, is also expected to contribute to its success.

ACTIVITY TARGETS: The activity targets include improving energy sector financial sustainability, enabling private sector participation and diversifying the energy mix, and strengthening tax collection and public procurement processes. Specific targets for financial sustainability are: reduce cross arrears to below CFAF 1 billion (from 140B), increase SONABEL's EBITDA to CFAF 40B+ (from 14B), and reduce customer outstanding bills to below CFAF 39B (from 55B). For energy sector participation/diversification: increase solar PV capacity to 15% of total installed capacity (from 0%), increase interconnected network capacity to 62% (from 52%), sign/negotiate 2 IPP projects (from 0), and have 1 IPP under standardized competitive process (from 0%). For tax/procurement: increase VAT revenues to 7.0% of GDP (from 6.0%), non-VAT tax revenues to 10% of GDP (from 8%), and reduce average public procurement days to 100 days (from 142 days). Additionally, the energy sector aims for 100% urban and 49% rural access by 2025, with 25% of installed capacity from renewables and a 20% decrease in generation costs by 2025.

ACTIVITY CONTEXT: The activity benefits from strong government commitment, aligned with the National Economic and Social Development Plan (PNDES) and international climate commitments. Macroeconomic projections are favorable, with projected GDP growth around 6% and narrowing fiscal deficit. Strong donor coordination (IMF, WB) and ongoing PFM reforms (aligned with WAEMU directives) enhance the context. However, a difficult security context with terrorist attacks and social unrest poses a significant risk. Persistent energy sector challenges include low electrification, high dependence on imported fuels, and the poor financial state of SONABEL, creating a tough operating environment.

ACTIVITY COMPLEXITY: The activity, "BF - DPO2 series on Energy and PFM," is the second and final operation in a programmatic DPF series, indicating a structured yet complex design. It addresses significant challenges in Burkina Faso's energy sector (low electrification, load shedding, aging infrastructure, high costs) and public finance management (tax collection, procurement). The overall risk is assessed as Substantial, with climate risks. It involves ambitious PDOs and policy reforms across multiple sectors, requiring strong governmental commitment, realistic goal-setting, and sustained follow-up, leveraging collaboration with IMF and World Bank.

ACTIVITY INTEGRATEDNESS: The activity is highly integrated. It is explicitly "the second and final single-tranche operation within a programmatic series of two consecutive development policy financing (DPF) operations," meaning it is cohesively part of a larger, ongoing DPF program. This programmatic series, in turn, is well-aligned with and supports Burkina Faso's 2016-2020 National Economic and Social Development Plan (PNDES) across its economic governance, human capital, and structural transformation pillars. It also aligns with the Government's strategies for the energy sector and public finance management, including the Economic and Finance Sector Policy (POSEF). The activity is not independent or isolated but rather a critical component of broader national and international development efforts.

FINANCING DETAILS: "The DPO2 series provides US$75 million equivalent in financing (SDR 17.8M grant, EUR 43M credit) to improve energy sector financial sustainability, enable private sector participation, and strengthen tax collection/public procurement. A key sub-activity is clearing energy sector cross arrears, with the Government committing FCFA 95 billion in 2017 (FCFA 60B payments, FCFA 35B tax credit) to SONABEL and SONABHY. The 2018 budget allocates FCFA 21 billion for fuel subsidies to SONABHY, matching the 2017 cap of US$34 million for fuel subsidies, indicating planned funding for this item. Efforts include designing a permanent arrangement by end-2018 to ensure timely payment of electricity consumption by public clients, preventing new arrears and improving state-owned enterprises' creditworthiness. Financing for the overall activity appears well-managed, leveraging IDA grants/credits and benefiting from government commitment to revenue mobilization and arrears clearance. The DPF's multi-pillar approach, where improved revenue collection (supported by tax reforms) finances energy sector investments and improved electricity access boosts private sector activity, strengthens financing success. The energy sector's reliance on budget transfers for cost recovery indicates a structural funding challenge. However, the government's strategy to shift to renewables and leverage Independent Power Producers (IPPs) aims to reduce long-term generation costs by 20% by 2025. The favorable macroeconomic outlook (projected GDP growth ~6%, narrowing fiscal deficit) provides a supportive environment for financing, though the energy sector's significant infrastructure needs (e.g., 170 MWp solar PV target by 2025) will require substantial, ongoing investment beyond current DPF scope, including private sector participation."

IMPLEMENTER PERFORMANCE CONTEXT: The Ministry of Economy, Finance and Development (MEFD) is the key implementing agency with previous DPF experience. The Government demonstrates high investment, aligning the activity with its PNDES, adopted after broad stakeholder consultations. WB assessed the Government's commitment to PFM reforms as satisfactory, with noted progress in financial governance. While staff mobility has weakened monitoring capacity, MEFD has appointed a program coordinator, indicating efforts to address this. The overall risk is Substantial, with institutional capacity rated as a Substantial risk.
ACTIVITY RISKS: Overall risk is Substantial, with climate and disaster risks. Macroeconomic risks include a widening fiscal deficit (6.5% GDP 2017), revenue shortfalls (poor tax admin, fraud, political instability), and commodity price sensitivity. Energy sector risks involve SONABEL's deteriorated finances, reliance on subsidies, tariffs not covering costs, dependency on volatile imported fuels/logistical disruptions, and rapid demand outstripping supply. Other risks include PPPs, tightened lending capacity, fragile security, and procurement delays.

ACTIVITY POSSIBILITIES: "The activity aligns with Burkina Faso's PNDES, supported by a favorable macroeconomic outlook. Government shows commitment to improved PPPs, reduced procurement delays, and a clear energy strategy. Design benefits from extensive analytical work, lessons learned, and collaboration with IMF/World Bank. Achievements: accelerated clearance of energy sector cross arrears, improved SONABEL financial performance (positive operating profit since 2010), timely payments, and significantly reduced fuel costs. SONABEL initiated a revenue protection program with smart meters. Institutional risks are mitigated by increased World Bank technical engagement and partners."
